lower-mount-royal.name: Lower Mount Royal
lower-mount-royal.subtitle: 17 Ave SW Character Blocks with Legal Suite Upside
lower-mount-royal.str_intro:
Lower Mount Royal occupies the wedge of inner-city Calgary directly south of 17th Avenue SW — the commercial strip locals call the Red Mile — and north of the escarpment that separates it from Upper Mount Royal. Originally platted in the early 1900s as an extension of one of Calgary's first elite residential districts, the neighbourhood retains a dense streetscape of Edwardian and Colonial Revival character homes on compact lots between 14th and 20th Streets SW. The combination of walkable access to downtown (roughly 15 minutes by foot or bicycle along the Beltline grid) and a nationally recognized dining and entertainment corridor makes it a consistent high-demand address for short-stay guests.

The 17th Avenue SW corridor immediately north of the community functions as the neighbourhood's commercial spine, offering fine dining, cocktail bars, boutique retail, and weekend patios that draw visitors from across the city. Mount Royal Park and Cartier Park — a small hilltop green space with panoramic downtown views — provide calm counterpoint to the avenue's energy. The MNP Community & Sports Centre on 12th Avenue SW delivers Olympic-pool facilities within walking distance, and the Mount Royal Community Association at 2317 10 Street SW hosts an outdoor skating rink and seasonal programming.

The housing stock in Lower Mount Royal is a genuine mixed palette: pre-1940 character homes with front porches and brick or stucco facades sit alongside post-2000 infill townhomes, brownstones, and low-rise condo buildings, mostly four storeys or under. Many older single-family lots have been converted to include legal secondary suites — basement or carriage-house configurations — making them among the more versatile STR-ready properties in inner Calgary. New executive townhomes and boutique condo projects have appeared along the 17th Avenue corridor and the adjacent lanes, lifting average renovation standards across the block.
lower-mount-royal.str_lifestyle: The 17th Avenue SW corridor immediately north of the community functions as the neighbourhood's commercial spine, offering fine dining, cocktail bars, boutique retail, and weekend patios that draw visitors from across the city. Mount Royal Park and Cartier Park — a small hilltop green space with panoramic downtown views — provide calm counterpoint to the avenue's energy. The MNP Community & Sports Centre on 12th Avenue SW delivers Olympic-pool facilities within walking distance, and the Mount Royal Community Association at 2317 10 Street SW hosts an outdoor skating rink and seasonal programming.
lower-mount-royal.str_housing: The housing stock in Lower Mount Royal is a genuine mixed palette: pre-1940 character homes with front porches and brick or stucco facades sit alongside post-2000 infill townhomes, brownstones, and low-rise condo buildings, mostly four storeys or under. Many older single-family lots have been converted to include legal secondary suites — basement or carriage-house configurations — making them among the more versatile STR-ready properties in inner Calgary. New executive townhomes and boutique condo projects have appeared along the 17th Avenue corridor and the adjacent lanes, lifting average renovation standards across the block.

renter_intel.title: Heads Up on Parking
renter_intel.description: Renters should be aware that parking can be a significant challenge in Lower Mount Royal. Many older buildings lack dedicated parking, and street parking is often metered or requires residential permits, which can be both difficult to obtain and costly. It is crucial to factor parking costs and availability into your budget and daily commute planning.

renter_intel.title: Pet Policies Vary
renter_intel.description: For pet owners, it is essential to understand that pet policies are not uniform across all rental properties. Prospective renters with pets should prepare a comprehensive pet resume and be ready to discuss their pet's needs directly with landlords, as many buildings enforce specific restrictions or require additional deposits.

renter_intel.title: Transit Accessibility
renter_intel.description: While Lower Mount Royal does not have a direct CTrain station, the neighborhood is well-served by numerous bus routes along 17th Avenue SW, providing excellent connectivity to downtown Calgary and other parts of the city. The Victoria Park/Stampede CTrain Station is also accessible via a short bus ride or a 20-25 minute walk.

renter_intel.title: Noise Considerations
renter_intel.description: Due to the popularity and vibrant nightlife along 17th Avenue SW, properties closer to this main commercial strip may experience higher noise levels, especially on weekends. Renters who prioritize a quieter living environment might consider looking for residences further away from the immediate vicinity of 17th Avenue SW.

whats_nearby.items: Calgary's premier urban commercial strip, with 300+ restaurants, bars, and boutiques within a five-minute walk of most Lower Mount Royal addresses.
Hilltop green spaces with downtown skyline views, tree canopy, and pathway connections to the Elbow River system to the south.
Full-service recreation facility on 12th Avenue SW offering Olympic pools, fitness centres, and arena ice, roughly a 10-minute walk from the neighbourhood core.

lower-mount-royal.peak_season: July (Stampede and 17th Ave events). Strong through September.
lower-mount-royal.best_property_type: Character home with legal suite or newer infill near 17th Ave SW.
lower-mount-royal.guest_profile: Upscale short-stay guests, festival-goers, corporate travellers, 17th Ave diners.
lower-mount-royal.stand_out: Character homes on the ridge with downtown views and separate-entry suites. Properties within 5 minutes' walk of 17th Ave SW.
lower-mount-royal.distinctive_fact: Lower Mount Royal sits on the ridge above 17th Ave SW — listings here combine the walkability of the Beltline with the quiet residential character that condo-heavy buildings can't offer. The combination drives premium rates.
lower-mount-royal.ltr_description:
Lower Mount Royal, nestled in Calgary's vibrant inner-city, offers a unique blend of historic charm and modern urban living. This neighborhood, bordered by the bustling 17th Avenue SW to the north, attracts a diverse demographic, from young professionals seeking an active lifestyle to established residents appreciating its walkability and proximity to downtown. Unlike its more traditional counterpart, Upper Mount Royal, this area has evolved into a higher-density community, characterized by a mix of stately early 20th-century homes and contemporary apartment and condominium buildings. Its central location provides residents with immediate access to a wealth of amenities, making it a highly sought-after address for those who thrive on city energy.

Strolling through Lower Mount Royal reveals a dynamic atmosphere, particularly along 17th Avenue SW, often referred to as the "Red Mile." Here, the streets buzz with activity, offering an array of restaurants, cafes, boutiques, and entertainment venues that cater to various tastes. The neighborhood's tree-lined residential streets, however, provide a quieter contrast, offering a sense of tranquility amidst the urban pulse. On weekends and evenings, the area comes alive with people enjoying patios, live music, and diverse culinary experiences. This lively yet residential balance sets it apart from the more purely commercial feel of the Beltline, offering a more integrated living experience.

For renters, Lower Mount Royal presents a varied housing stock, predominantly featuring low-rise apartment buildings and condominiums, alongside some executive townhomes and converted heritage properties. While the architectural diversity is appealing, renters should be aware that purpose-built rental buildings might be older, potentially leading to variations in amenities like in-suite laundry or modern finishes. Parking can be a significant consideration; while some buildings offer underground parking, street parking is often metered or requires residential permits, which can be challenging to secure and costly. Pet policies vary widely by building, so prospective renters with pets should inquire directly. The high demand for rentals in this desirable area can also mean competitive pricing and quicker decision-making is often required.

ltr_vibe_tags.title: Cafe Culture
ltr_vibe_tags.description: The neighborhood boasts a thriving cafe scene, with popular spots like Deville Coffee and Q.LAB Coffee Experience located conveniently on 17th Avenue SW.

ltr_vibe_tags.title: Dining Hotspot
ltr_vibe_tags.description: 17th Avenue SW is a culinary destination, offering a wide range of dining experiences from casual eateries to fine dining establishments such as UNA Pizza + Wine, The Coup, Maven, and Porch.

ltr_vibe_tags.title: Urban Green Space
ltr_vibe_tags.description: Residents can find respite in the neighborhood's green spaces, including Mount Royal Park and Cartier Park, which offer serene environments and scenic city views.

ltr_vibe_tags.title: Walkable & Connected
ltr_vibe_tags.description: Lower Mount Royal is highly walkable, providing easy access to downtown Calgary and surrounding areas. Numerous bus routes along 17th Avenue SW further enhance connectivity.

ltr_vibe_tags.title: Historic Charm Meets Modern
ltr_vibe_tags.description: The architectural landscape of Lower Mount Royal is a testament to its evolution, featuring a harmonious blend of stately early 20th-century homes and contemporary condominium developments.

eau-claire.name: Eau Claire
eau-claire.subtitle: Bow River Condos at Calgary's Festival District Doorstep
eau-claire.str_intro:
Eau Claire occupies a narrow strip of inner-city Calgary along the south bank of the Bow River, bounded by Prince's Island Park to the north, 3rd Avenue SW to the south, 2nd Street SW to the east, and 9th Street SW to the west. The neighbourhood takes its name from the Eau Claire Lumber Company established in 1886, and by the late 20th century it was redeveloped into one of the city's most desirable riverfront residential enclaves. Its position — directly north of the downtown commercial core and adjacent to Calgary's designated Festival District — means residents and guests live within a 10-minute walk of Stephen Avenue, the Plus 15 skywalk network, and the city's main cultural event infrastructure.

Prince's Island Park, a 20-hectare urban island in the Bow River reachable by footbridge, anchors the neighbourhood's outdoor offer: it hosts the Calgary Folk Music Festival, Shakespeare in the Park, and a year-round river café, as well as flower gardens, hiking trails, and outdoor skating in winter. The Santiago Calatrava-designed Peace Bridge — a recognizable Calgary landmark — connects Eau Claire to Kensington's boutique shops and restaurants on the north bank, typically a 5-minute walk. Eau Claire Market, currently undergoing redevelopment with a new outdoor plaza slated for 2025, provides an immediate retail and restaurant node at the community's centre, and the YMCA Fitness Centre sits steps from the major condo clusters.

Residential supply in Eau Claire is almost exclusively condominium: a collection of established luxury towers and low-rise complexes built between the early 1980s and the mid-2000s, including Eau Claire Estates (500 Eau Claire Ave SW, built 1981), Prince's Island Estates (400 Eau Claire Ave SW, built 1995), La Caille Parke Place, Chateau La Caille, Pointe on the Bow, Princeton Grand, and The Concord. Most buildings offer city skyline, mountain, and river views; many include 24/7 concierge, heated underground parking, and on-site fitness or spa facilities. Single-family homes are effectively absent, and new supply is rare, making available units genuinely scarce.
eau-claire.str_lifestyle: Prince's Island Park, a 20-hectare urban island in the Bow River reachable by footbridge, anchors the neighbourhood's outdoor offer: it hosts the Calgary Folk Music Festival, Shakespeare in the Park, and a year-round river café, as well as flower gardens, hiking trails, and outdoor skating in winter. The Santiago Calatrava-designed Peace Bridge — a recognizable Calgary landmark — connects Eau Claire to Kensington's boutique shops and restaurants on the north bank, typically a 5-minute walk. Eau Claire Market, currently undergoing redevelopment with a new outdoor plaza slated for 2025, provides an immediate retail and restaurant node at the community's centre, and the YMCA Fitness Centre sits steps from the major condo clusters.

Most buildings offer city skyline, mountain, and river views; many include 24/7 concierge, heated underground parking, and on-site fitness or spa facilities. Single-family homes are effectively absent, and new supply is rare, making available units genuinely scarce.

renter_intel.title: Heads Up
renter_intel.description: Eau Claire Market is scheduled for demolition starting July 1, 2024, to make way for the Green Line LRT station. This will temporarily impact local shopping and dining options, but promises enhanced transit connectivity in the future.

renter_intel.title: Local Insight
renter_intel.description: While the neighborhood is highly walkable, a car can be beneficial for larger grocery runs. Safeway in Kensington is a popular option, but smaller convenience stores are available within Eau Claire.

renter_intel.title: Transit Tip
renter_intel.description: The area is well-served by bus routes, and the future 2 Street SW Station (Green Line LRT) will significantly boost public transit access, making car-free living more feasible.

renter_intel.title: Pet Resume Tip
renter_intel.description: Given the prevalence of condominiums, prepare a comprehensive pet resume for your furry friends, including references and vaccination records, as pet policies can be stringent.

whats_nearby.items: A 20-hectare island park on the Bow River hosting major festivals, a river café, gardens, and pathway connections — directly accessible on foot from every Eau Claire building.
Calgary's premier pedestrian commercial street with restaurants, cultural venues, and retail is a 10-minute walk south through the downtown core.
Santiago Calatrava's iconic pedestrian bridge connects Eau Claire to Kensington Village's independent cafés, restaurants, and boutiques on the north bank of the Bow River.

eau-claire.peak_season: June–September (Bow River pathway, Prince's Island Park events, Eau Claire Market). July peak for Stampede.
eau-claire.best_property_type: Condo with Bow River or Prince's Island Park views in a STR-permissive building.
eau-claire.guest_profile: Outdoor enthusiasts, couples seeking a scenic urban stay, corporate travellers, festival-goers.
eau-claire.stand_out: Condos with Bow River or Prince's Island Park views in STR-permissive buildings. Units on the upper floors with unobstructed river views command the highest rates.
eau-claire.distinctive_fact: Eau Claire is the only inner-city neighbourhood where guests can walk to both the Bow River pathway and Prince's Island Park from their front door. Listings that market this outdoor access consistently outperform comparable downtown condos by 15–25%.

health.name: Passport Health Downtown Calgary Travel Clinic

eau-claire.ltr_description:
Eau Claire, nestled immediately north of Downtown Calgary and along the scenic Bow River, offers a distinctive urban living experience. This neighbourhood is characterized by its modern riverside condominiums and a vibrant mix of shopping, dining, and green spaces. It appeals to professionals and urban dwellers seeking a dynamic lifestyle with immediate access to the city's core and natural riverfront amenities. Unlike the more historically residential areas of Calgary, Eau Claire stands out as a planned urban development, blending contemporary architecture with expansive parkland.

Walking through Eau Claire reveals a lively yet serene atmosphere. During the day, the Bow River Pathway buzzes with cyclists and pedestrians, while Prince's Island Park hosts various festivals and offers a tranquil escape. Evenings bring a sophisticated energy, with residents and visitors frequenting upscale restaurants and cafes like JOEY Eau Claire and Minas Brazilian Steakhouse. The area feels more akin to a bustling European promenade than a typical North American downtown, with public art installations and meticulously maintained green spaces contributing to its unique charm.

For renters, Eau Claire primarily offers a high concentration of condominium and apartment units, reflecting its urban design. Purpose-built rentals are common, often featuring modern amenities. Parking can be a significant consideration; while many buildings offer underground parking, it often comes at an additional cost, and street parking is limited. Pet policies vary widely by building, with some being very pet-friendly and others having strict restrictions or size limits. Renters should be prepared for higher rental costs compared to other Calgary neighbourhoods, a trade-off for the prime location and lifestyle.

ltr_vibe_tags.title: Riverside Strolls
ltr_vibe_tags.description: Enjoy leisurely walks or bike rides along the Bow River Pathway, connecting to Prince's Island Park.

ltr_vibe_tags.title: Culinary Hotspot
ltr_vibe_tags.description: Explore diverse dining options from casual cafes like Hutch Cafe to fine dining at Minas Brazilian Steakhouse.

ltr_vibe_tags.title: Urban Oasis
ltr_vibe_tags.description: Relax in West Eau Claire Park or the revitalized Eau Claire Plaza, offering green spaces amidst the city.

ltr_vibe_tags.title: Boutique Finds
ltr_vibe_tags.description: Discover unique shops and services, including Davidson & Co. Jewels and PAWS Discovery.

ltr_vibe_tags.title: Festival District
ltr_vibe_tags.description: Experience cultural events and performances at Prince's Island Park, a hub for summer festivals.

cliff-bungalow.name: Cliff Bungalow
cliff-bungalow.subtitle: Olmsted-Planned Bungalow Blocks, Minutes from the Stampede Grounds
cliff-bungalow.str_intro:
Cliff Bungalow is a small, dense inner-city neighbourhood centred along 5th Street SW, wedged between 17th Avenue SW to the north, the Elbow River to the south, and 4th Street SW to the east. The neighbourhood was originally designed in 1909–10 by the Olmsted firm as a Canadian Pacific Railway residential district — an eastern extension of the Mount Royal Garden Suburb — and retains a Garden City street layout with deep garden setbacks and mature tree canopy on most blocks. Two brick school buildings from 1919 and 1920 (Holy Angels School and Cliff Bungalow School) still anchor the neighbourhood's interior, and the 1915-planted green ash allée along 5A Street SW remains a significant streetscape feature.

The 4th Street SW corridor — shared with the adjacent Mission neighbourhood — is one of Calgary's most walkable commercial districts, with over 300 independently owned boutiques, cafés, and restaurants operating along a 10-block stretch. The annual Lilac Festival on 4th Street, held each spring, brings tens of thousands of visitors directly into the community. Calgary Stampede Park is roughly a 10–15-minute walk east, making Cliff Bungalow one of the closest residential neighbourhoods to the grounds and a consistent demand driver during the 10-day Stampede each July. The Elbow River pathway system provides immediate recreational access to the south, with connections to Sandy Beach and the river trail network.

The housing stock reflects the neighbourhood's pre-1940 origins: single-storey and one-and-a-half storey bungalows with Edwardian detailing predominate on the residential streets west of 4th Street, with a significant portion of the stock eligible for or already holding legal secondary suites. Many properties retain original brick or stucco exteriors, oversize front stoops, and mature lot trees. The City of Calgary has designated Cliff Bungalow as a heritage area with additional design review for alterations, which limits the pace of demolition-rebuild infill but also preserves the character that differentiates this supply from standard inner-city condos.
cliff-bungalow.str_lifestyle: The 4th Street SW corridor — shared with the adjacent Mission neighbourhood — is one of Calgary's most walkable commercial districts, with over 300 independently owned boutiques, cafés, and restaurants operating along a 10-block stretch. The annual Lilac Festival on 4th Street, held each spring, brings tens of thousands of visitors directly into the community. Calgary Stampede Park is roughly a 10–15-minute walk east, making Cliff Bungalow one of the closest residential neighbourhoods to the grounds and a consistent demand driver during the 10-day Stampede each July. The Elbow River pathway system provides immediate recreational access to the south, with connections to Sandy Beach and the river trail network.

Many properties retain original brick or stucco exteriors, oversize front stoops, and mature lot trees. The City of Calgary has designated Cliff Bungalow as a heritage area with additional design review for alterations, which limits the pace of demolition-rebuild infill but also preserves the character that differentiates this supply from standard inner-city condos.

renter_intel.title: Pet Resume Tip
renter_intel.description: If you have pets, prepare a comprehensive pet resume detailing their training, temperament, and vet history to stand out, as pet policies vary and often involve fees.

renter_intel.title: Parking Heads Up
renter_intel.description: While some rentals include parking, it's not guaranteed. Always confirm parking arrangements and potential costs, especially if you own a vehicle.

renter_intel.title: Local Insight
renter_intel.description: Explore the smaller, independent shops and cafes along 4th Street SW for unique finds and a true sense of local community.

renter_intel.title: Transit Tip
renter_intel.description: Leverage the excellent bus service and nearby C-Train access for an easy commute to downtown, potentially saving on transportation costs and reducing the need for a car.

whats_nearby.items: Over 300 independent restaurants, cafés, and boutiques along a walkable 10-block commercial strip shared with Mission, anchored by the annual Lilac Festival.
The Calgary Stampede grounds and BMO Centre convention facility are roughly a 10–15-minute walk east, making this one of the closest residential neighbourhoods to the venue.
The Elbow River linear pathway runs along the neighbourhood's southern edge, providing direct walking and cycling access to Sandy Beach, River Park, and Glenmore Reservoir.

cliff-bungalow.peak_season: July (Stampede and 4th Street events). Strong through September.
cliff-bungalow.best_property_type: Pre-1940 bungalow with legal basement suite. Cliff Bungalow's housing stock is almost entirely character homes.
cliff-bungalow.guest_profile: Festival-goers, 4th Street diners, couples seeking a character-home experience.
cliff-bungalow.stand_out: Pre-1940 bungalows with legal basement suites within walking distance of 4th Street SW.
cliff-bungalow.distinctive_fact: Cliff Bungalow is one of Calgary's smallest and most cohesive character neighbourhoods. Its proximity to 4th Street SW and the Stampede grounds, combined with authentic pre-war housing stock, creates a guest experience that modern condos cannot replicate.
cliff-bungalow.ltr_description:
Cliff Bungalow, nestled in Calgary's vibrant inner-city southwest, offers a unique blend of historic charm and modern convenience. Bordered by the scenic Elbow River to the south, the bustling Uptown 17th Avenue to the north, and the lively Fourth Street to the east, this neighborhood is a magnet for those seeking an active urban lifestyle without sacrificing a sense of community. Its tree-lined streets and mature homes create a distinct character, setting it apart from the newer, high-rise dominated areas of Calgary. Residents here enjoy unparalleled access to downtown, making it an ideal location for professionals and urban explorers alike.

Strolling through Cliff Bungalow, especially on a weekend or evening, reveals a relaxed yet sophisticated atmosphere. You'll encounter locals enjoying the patios of establishments like Porch or The Rooftop YYC, or perhaps grabbing a coffee at The Sharp Music Cafe Brunch. The proximity to the Elbow Park Pathway encourages outdoor activity, with residents often seen walking or cycling along the river. This neighborhood offers a quieter, more established feel compared to the constant buzz of nearby 17th Avenue, providing a welcome retreat while still being steps away from diverse dining and entertainment options. It's a place where you can easily transition from a peaceful riverside walk to a lively evening out.

For renters, Cliff Bungalow primarily features low-rise apartments, renovated townhomes, and suites within mature homes, offering a more intimate living experience than large apartment complexes. While many units include parking, often in a garage or designated stall, some may have unassigned parking, so verifying this detail is crucial. Pet policies vary significantly; while many landlords are pet-friendly, this often comes with additional fees and requires prior approval. Renters should be prepared for a mix of older buildings with character and newer, updated units. Laundry facilities are typically in-suite or shared within smaller buildings. The demand for rental properties in this desirable area can be high, so prompt action is often necessary when a suitable unit becomes available.

ltr_vibe_tags.title: Riverside Retreat
ltr_vibe_tags.description: Enjoy serene walks and bike rides along the Elbow Park Pathway, offering a natural escape within the city.

ltr_vibe_tags.title: Culinary Corridor
ltr_vibe_tags.description: Explore diverse dining experiences along 4th Street SW and 17th Avenue SW, with options like Porch and Elephant & Castle.

ltr_vibe_tags.title: Walkable & Connected
ltr_vibe_tags.description: Experience excellent walkability to amenities, transit, and downtown, reducing reliance on a car.

ltr_vibe_tags.title: Historic Charm
ltr_vibe_tags.description: Discover character-filled low-rise apartments and renovated townhomes amidst mature, tree-lined streets.

ltr_vibe_tags.title: Cafe Culture
ltr_vibe_tags.description: Start your day with a coffee from local spots like The Sharp Music Cafe Brunch, contributing to a relaxed neighborhood rhythm.

point-mckay.name: Point Mckay
point-mckay.subtitle: Riverfront Towers and Garden Townhomes Above the Bow
point-mckay.str_intro:
Point McKay occupies a compact parcel on the north bank of the Bow River in Calgary's northwest inner city, bounded by Bowness Road to the north, 37th Street NW to the east, Edworthy Park to the west, and the Bow River escarpment to the south. The neighbourhood was developed between 1977 and 1981 as one of Calgary's first planned urban villages: the 21-storey Riverside Tower I and II high-rises (completed 1979 and 1981 respectively, totalling 295 units) anchor the western edge along Parkdale Boulevard, while six clusters of curved-street townhomes — approximately 505 units plus 30 duplexes — fill the interior. The development is named after Alfred Sidney McKay, who homesteaded the land in the late 19th century; his original sandstone home from circa 1900 still stands inside the community.

Point McKay's defining amenity is direct Bow River pathway access from virtually every front door, with the trail network connecting cyclists and runners to downtown Calgary in one direction and Edworthy Park in the other. The Riverside Club — the fitness facility anchored at street level in the Riverside Towers — offers a pool, gym, tennis courts, and golf practice facilities available to building residents at discounted rates. Angel's Cappuccino & Ice Cream café in adjacent Edworthy Park and the Lazy Loaf & Kettle in Parkdale provide immediate walkable café options. Foothills Medical Centre and the University of Calgary are both within a 10-minute drive, underpinning consistent demand from medical professionals and academic visitors.

Supply in Point McKay is almost entirely condominium and townhome: the Riverside I and II towers offer one- and two-bedroom condo suites with river valley or downtown skyline views, while the townhome clusters provide two- and three-bedroom units with private garages and rear greenspace access to common landscaped areas. Current asking prices for condos range from approximately $290,000 to $450,000; townhomes trade between $565,000 and $830,000 depending on location and renovation level. Most units are 1970s–80s vintage with varying degrees of interior updating; WinSport (Canada Olympic Park) is accessible in roughly 10–15 minutes by car via the Trans-Canada Highway, making this a logical base for ski-season guests.

The Riverside Club — the fitness facility anchored at street level in the Riverside Towers — offers a pool, gym, tennis courts, and golf practice facilities available to building residents at discounted rates. Angel's Cappuccino & Ice Cream café in adjacent Edworthy Park and the Lazy Loaf & Kettle in Parkdale provide immediate walkable café options. Foothills Medical Centre and the University of Calgary are both within a 10-minute drive, underpinning consistent demand from medical professionals and academic visitors.

Current asking prices for condos range from approximately $290,000 to $450,000; townhomes trade between $565,000 and $830,000 depending on location and renovation level. Most units are 1970s–80s vintage with varying degrees of interior updating; WinSport (Canada Olympic Park) is accessible in roughly 10–15 minutes by car via the Trans-Canada Highway, making this a logical base for ski-season guests.

renter_intel.title: Heads Up — pet rules are strict
renter_intel.description: Many condo boards here are cat-only or have aggressive weight limits on dogs. Always check the specific building's bylaws before applying — a Pet Resume helps.

renter_intel.title: Transit Tip
renter_intel.description: No C-Train station inside Point McKay. The closest practical transit is the Route 1 bus along Bowness Road heading to downtown, or you walk/bike 15 minutes to Sunnyside C-Train.

renter_intel.title: Local Insight
renter_intel.description: The footbridge to Edworthy Park is the secret weapon — it gives you instant access to one of Calgary's best off-leash dog areas and a direct path to Lawrey Gardens.

renter_intel.title: Pet Resume Tip
renter_intel.description: If your building does allow dogs, you've hit the jackpot — Edworthy Park's off-leash zone is across the bridge, and the river pathway is wall-to-wall dog walkers every morning.

whats_nearby.items: Continuous Bow River pathway directly accessible from the community links cyclists and walkers to downtown Calgary to the east and Edworthy Park's Douglas Fir trails and pebble beach to the west.
Year-round ski, bobsled, mountain biking, and skating facility at 88 Canada Olympic Road SW is approximately 10–15 minutes by car, a key demand driver for winter-sport visitors.
One of Calgary's largest acute-care hospitals sits just north of the community, supporting extended-stay demand from medical staff, visiting specialists, and patient families.

point-mckay.peak_season: June–September (Bow River access, WinSport proximity). Strong through summer.
point-mckay.best_property_type: Condo or townhome with Bow River views. Point McKay's riverfront condos are the primary STR asset.
point-mckay.guest_profile: Outdoor enthusiasts using the Bow River pathway, mountain-bound travellers, couples seeking a scenic stay.
point-mckay.stand_out: Riverfront condos with Bow River views and STR-permissive condo bylaws. Properties near the Bow River pathway system.
point-mckay.distinctive_fact: Point McKay sits on a peninsula formed by a bend in the Bow River, giving it river views on three sides. Listings with river views command a significant premium over comparable properties without views. The Bow River pathway system is literally at the doorstep.

health.name: Holistic Institute of Health and Fertility

point-mckay.ltr_description:
Point McKay is the small wedge of riverfront condos and townhomes squeezed between Bowness Road and the Bow River, just east of Parkdale. It's tiny — a few streets, a handful of low-rise condo complexes, and that's basically it. The whole community was once part of the town of Montgomery before being annexed in 1963, and it still has that slightly forgotten, "wait, this is in the city?" quality. Renters end up here for one reason: river access without inner-city prices. You're a five-minute drive to Foothills Hospital, ten minutes to downtown, and the Bow River pathway runs along your back door. If you work at the hospital, the Children's Hospital, or U of C, this is one of the most undervalued spots in the city.

Walking Point McKay feels like a riverside retirement village that happens to be in the inner city. The streets are quiet, almost weirdly so — no through traffic, no commercial strip, just curving residential roads ending at the river. Most of the housing is 1970s and '80s low-rise condo complexes with brown brick and balconies overlooking the pathway. The Bow River pathway here is one of the best stretches in the city: a footbridge crosses to Edworthy Park on the south bank, and from your front door you can be in Bowness Park (upstream) or Kensington (downstream) without ever touching a car. Lazy Loaf & Kettle on Bowness Road is the one beloved local spot — a bakery-café where every regular knows the staff. Beyond that, you're walking or driving to Parkdale, Montgomery, or Kensington for actual amenities.

This is condo and townhome country — almost no detached rental stock. Most units are 1-bed and 2-bed condos in the older complexes along Point McKay Drive, Point McKay Crescent, and Point McKay Gardens, with rents typically lower than equivalent units in Kensington or Parkdale because the buildings are older and the location is quieter. Many of these condo boards have strict pet rules — some allow cats only, some have weight limits on dogs, some are pet-free entirely — so always confirm the pet policy with the specific building, not just the listing agent. Parking is usually a titled or assigned underground stall plus limited visitor parking. Laundry is often in-suite in renovated units, shared in older ones. The river views command a premium; back-facing units without the view rent for noticeably less. Watch out for older buildings with poly-B plumbing or aluminum wiring — ask before signing.

ltr_vibe_tags.title: Riverfront Bubble
ltr_vibe_tags.description: Five streets, one river, almost no through traffic

ltr_vibe_tags.title: Hospital Commuter's Secret
ltr_vibe_tags.description: Foothills and Children's Hospital are a five-minute drive

ltr_vibe_tags.title: Pathway Front Door
ltr_vibe_tags.description: The Bow River pathway is your back yard

ltr_vibe_tags.title: Forgotten Pocket
ltr_vibe_tags.description: Most Calgarians have never set foot here

ltr_vibe_tags.title: Older Condo Stock
ltr_vibe_tags.description: 1970s–'80s low-rises, not glass towers

mahogany.name: Mahogany
mahogany.subtitle: Calgary's Largest Freshwater Lake Community — Whole-Home Detached with Beach Club Access
mahogany.str_intro:
Mahogany is a master-planned community in southeast Calgary developed by Hopewell Residential from 2007, now extending across more than 1,200 acres of planned land. The community is centred on a 63-acre freshwater lake — the largest of its kind in Calgary — with 21 acres of private beach, two gated beach clubs (Mahogany Beach Club on Marine Drive and West Beach), and 74 acres of natural wetlands threaded with interpretive boardwalks. Access to Deerfoot Trail and Stoney Trail places the community roughly 25–30 minutes from downtown Calgary, and the South Health Campus hospital is minutes away via 52nd Street SE.

Lake privileges drive the community's lifestyle proposition and its STR differentiation: Mahogany HOA membership credentials entitle registered residents (including tenants holding a current lease) to beach access, paddleboarding, kayaking, pedal boats, swimming, lake-side fire pits, beach volleyball, and winter skating on the lake when ice conditions allow. Westman Village — an urban-resort mixed-use development adjacent to the lake — adds café, dining, and entertainment options at a walkable distance. The 22-kilometre internal pathway network, Mahogany Village Market (Sobeys, Shoppers, dining), and Brookfield Residential YMCA at Seton round out the daily-needs infrastructure.

The housing stock is predominantly detached single-family homes built from approximately 2010 onward, with architectural styles ranging from craftsman and modern farmhouse to contemporary; lakefront estate lots on Mahogany Island and the Reserve Collection represent the premium tier. Townhomes and duplexes provide entry-level access to lake privileges, while the Westman Village development added condominium and penthouse product directly adjacent to the water. For STR purposes, whole-home detached properties with lake privileges are the target asset class: the beach club and lake access transfer to tenants holding a valid lease, a detail that must be confirmed with the MHOA at the time of any rental agreement.

Westman Village — an urban-resort mixed-use development adjacent to the lake — adds café, dining, and entertainment options at a walkable distance. The 22-kilometre internal pathway network, Mahogany Village Market (Sobeys, Shoppers, dining), and Brookfield Residential YMCA at Seton round out the daily-needs infrastructure.

Townhomes and duplexes provide entry-level access to lake privileges, while the Westman Village development added condominium and penthouse product directly adjacent to the water. renter_intel.title: Lake Privilege Transfer to Tenants
renter_intel.description: The Mahogany HOA formally transfers lake and beach club access credentials to tenants who provide a current lease and government-issued ID showing the Mahogany address — a concrete, documentable amenity that operators can market directly to guests.

renter_intel.title: Summer Demand Concentration
renter_intel.description: Bookings for Mahogany properties are heavily weighted toward summer months when beach, paddleboard, and lake swimming access are operational, requiring active revenue management to improve shoulder-season and winter occupancy.

renter_intel.title: Rare Supply in a Growing Community
renter_intel.description: Whole-home detached listings with lake-adjacency remain scarce relative to demand; the 'Moderate/Southeast/Rare' classification reflects constrained available inventory despite the community's ongoing build-out.

whats_nearby.category: Mahogany Beach Club and West Beach
whats_nearby.items: Two gated private beach facilities on the 63-acre lake, offering swimming, paddleboarding, kayaking, beach volleyball, fire pits, and a splash park, accessible to residents and their guests via HOA membership credentials.
A mixed-use lakeside development with cafés, restaurants, event space, and a fitness studio at the water's edge, providing walkable dining and social infrastructure within the community.

whats_nearby.category: South Health Campus & Seton Urban District
whats_nearby.items: Calgary's newest major hospital and the adjacent Seton retail and entertainment precinct — including Cineplex VIP cinemas, YMCA, and service retail — are approximately 5 minutes from Mahogany's main access points.

mahogany.peak_season: June–August (lake access is the primary draw). School-year weekends secondary.
mahogany.best_property_type: Whole-home detached with direct lake access or deeded lake privileges.
mahogany.guest_profile: Multi-generational families, lake vacationers, long-weekend trippers from nearby provinces.
mahogany.stand_out: Detached homes within walking distance of the lake. 3+ bedrooms to capture family group bookings.
mahogany.distinctive_fact: Lake access is the entire value proposition here. Listings that advertise 'walk to lake' or 'private beach access' see 2–3× the bookings of identical homes without it.

auburn-bay.name: Auburn Bay
auburn-bay.subtitle: Calgary's premier freshwater lake community — four-season recreation, a maturing southeast corridor, and rare residential lake access
auburn-bay.str_intro:
Auburn Bay is a master-planned lake community in Calgary's southeast quadrant, anchored by a private 43-acre freshwater lake at its geographic centre. Developed by Brookfield Residential Properties, the neighbourhood sits just east of Deerfoot Trail and south of Stoney Trail, with 52 Street SE as its primary north-south corridor. Residents have year-round access to Auburn Bay Lake — swimming and paddleboarding in summer, skating in winter — a lifestyle feature that distinguishes this community from the majority of Calgary's suburban inventory. The 7,000 sq ft Auburn House lodge serves as the community hub, operating beach, tennis courts, and a meeting facility exclusively for residents and their guests.

The community's appeal concentrates around outdoor programming and proximity to the Seton Urban District, Calgary's largest purpose-built urban mixed-use district, located minutes to the south. Seton anchors the area with South Health Campus (one of Alberta's busiest acute-care hospitals), a 250,000 sq ft YMCA, Cineplex Cinemas VIP, and extensive retail along Seton Boulevard. Mahogany Village Market on 52 Street SE provides everyday grocery and service needs via Sobeys and Shoppers Drug Mart. Pathway networks connect Auburn Bay internally and extend toward neighbouring McKenzie Towne and Cranston, making active commuting viable for residents of the inner ring.

Housing in Auburn Bay spans detached single-family homes, semi-detached, townhomes, and low-rise condominiums, with detached product typically pricing at or slightly above the Calgary benchmark given the lake-access premium. Lakefront and lake-privilege lots command the highest premiums; homes on Auburn Bay Boulevard SE and Auburnshire Drive SE nearest the water are in consistently high demand. Schools within or adjacent to the community include Auburn Bay School (K–4, CBE) and Prince of Peace Catholic School; Joane Cardinal-Schubert High School serves grades 10–12 in a neighbouring community. A planned southeastern C-Train extension corridor runs parallel to 52 Street SE on the community's eastern boundary, though confirmed service dates remain subject to City of Calgary capital approval.

Seton anchors the area with South Health Campus (one of Alberta's busiest acute-care hospitals), a 250,000 sq ft YMCA, Cineplex Cinemas VIP, and extensive retail along Seton Boulevard. Mahogany Village Market on 52 Street SE provides everyday grocery and service needs via Sobeys and Shoppers Drug Mart. Schools within or adjacent to the community include Auburn Bay School (K–4, CBE) and Prince of Peace Catholic School; Joane Cardinal-Schubert High School serves grades 10–12 in a neighbouring community. A planned southeastern C-Train extension corridor runs parallel to 52 Street SE on the community's eastern boundary, though confirmed service dates remain subject to City of Calgary capital approval.

renter_intel.title: Lake Access is Resident-Restricted
renter_intel.description: Auburn Bay Lake access belongs exclusively to property owners in the community; renters require the resident owner to sponsor their access, making lake-privilege a key marketing differentiator for landlords who actively communicate this benefit to tenants.

renter_intel.title: Strong Rental Inventory Mix
renter_intel.description: RentFaster data shows 70+ active rental listings across condos (~$1,761/mo avg), townhouses (~$2,092/mo avg), and detached houses (~$2,735/mo avg), indicating a functional rental supply stack for investors across multiple price tiers.

renter_intel.title: Hospital and YMCA Employment Base
renter_intel.description: South Health Campus and the Seton YMCA represent a concentrated employment node minutes from Auburn Bay, generating stable demand from healthcare professionals and service staff seeking rentals close to work.

whats_nearby.items: Calgary's largest new mixed-use urban district, immediately south via Seton Boulevard, integrates South Health Campus, YMCA, cinema, grocery, and dining in a walkable format.
Retail hub on 52 Street SE anchored by Sobeys and Shoppers Drug Mart, serving the day-to-day needs of multiple southeast lake communities.
The northern edge of Auburn Bay connects directly to Stoney Trail, giving residents access to the full city ring road network and cutting cross-city commute times significantly.

auburn-bay.peak_season: June–August (lake access). School-year long weekends secondary.
auburn-bay.best_property_type: Detached home with lake access or deeded beach privileges.
auburn-bay.stand_out: Detached homes with lake access or deeded beach privileges. 3+ bedrooms for family groups.
auburn-bay.distinctive_fact: Auburn Bay's lake is the second-largest private lake community in Calgary after Mahogany. Listings with lake access consistently outperform those without by a factor of 2–3× in summer bookings.

groceries.name: Calgary Co-op Auburn Bay Food Centre

belmont.name: Belmont
belmont.subtitle: South Calgary's newest master-planned community — Macleod Trail access, Spruce Meadows proximity, and a future-facing amenity pipeline
belmont.str_intro:
Belmont is one of Calgary's most recently established communities, located in the city's deep south anchored between 194th Avenue SW to the north, Sheriff King Street to the west, and 210th Avenue SW to the south — with direct arterial access onto Macleod Trail and Stoney Trail. Established in 2018 and built by multiple top-tier Calgary builders including Morrison Homes and Homes by Avi, Belmont was designed as a complete community with a long-horizon amenity plan that includes a City of Calgary Recreation Centre, public library, and high school site along 210th Avenue SW. A future LRT transit centre on the community's east side — planned to integrate with the existing Somerset/Bridlewood C-Train line — is included in the area structure plan, though construction timelines are subject to City capital approval.

Belmont's lifestyle offering today centres on 40 acres of park with interconnected paved pathways, sculpted ponds, and playgrounds calibrated for varied age groups, while the broader south corridor delivers most current retail and entertainment needs. Spruce Meadows, one of the world's premier equestrian facilities and a year-round events venue, sits only minutes west along 210th Avenue. The Granary Road market — a farmers market, petting zoo, and summer day camp destination — operates nearby on 226 Avenue SW. Shawnessy Village Shopping Centre to the north on Macleod Trail consolidates big-box retail including Walmart, Canadian Tire, and Sport Check; Silverado Marketplace provides closer grocery (Sobeys) and pharmacy (Shoppers Drug Mart) needs on the community's northern edge.

Belmont's housing mix includes individually titled semi-attached townhomes (many with no condo fees), attached townhomes, and a growing detached single-family stock — the community targets both first-time buyers and move-up purchasers relocating from denser urban areas. Architectural styles span Tudor, Prairie, Arts and Crafts, and Modern, with quality finishing from builders operating under the community's design guidelines. Schools currently require a short drive to Silverado (Ron Southern School) or Legacy (All Saints High School); the area structure plan identifies future school sites within Belmont itself. Existing storm pond and pathway network form the primary active-lifestyle infrastructure until the planned recreation centre and library are delivered.

renter_intel.title: Investor-Friendly Townhome Product
renter_intel.description: Many Belmont semi-attached townhomes are individually titled with no condo fees and are explicitly marketed as rental-friendly, reducing carrying cost complexity for STR or long-term rental investors.

renter_intel.title: Near-Term Amenity Gap Creates Entry Opportunity
renter_intel.description: With the recreation centre, library, and LRT station still in the planning/approval pipeline, current pricing reflects the community's early-stage amenity profile — investors acquiring now may benefit from appreciation as anchors are delivered.

renter_intel.title: Mountain Gateway Demand Driver
renter_intel.description: Quick access to Stoney Trail and Macleod Trail positions Belmont as a logical base for workers and seasonal visitors oriented toward Kananaskis, Canmore, and Banff, supporting demand for furnished and short-term inventory.

whats_nearby.items: World-class equestrian and events facility minutes west on 210th Avenue SW, hosting major competitions, markets, and the nationally attended Masters Tournament each fall.
Major big-box and service retail node on Macleod Trail to the north, providing Walmart, Canadian Tire, Sport Check, Home Depot, and cinema access within a short drive.
One of Alberta's largest acute-care hospitals in the Seton district, a short drive east via Stoney Trail, representing a significant employment anchor for south Calgary renters.

belmont.peak_season: June–August (Spruce Meadows summer tournaments, mountain gateway). July Stampede secondary.
belmont.best_property_type: Newer detached 3-4 bed with double garage. Walkout basement suite adds a second revenue stream.
belmont.guest_profile: Spruce Meadows equestrian event attendees, mountain-bound families, corporate travellers on Macleod Trail.
belmont.stand_out: Newer 3-4 bed detached homes with double garages. Properties that market Spruce Meadows proximity and the '1.5 hrs to Banff' angle see the strongest summer bookings.
belmont.distinctive_fact: BNBCalc comparables for this postal zone show 3-bed whole homes achieving $45K–$95K annual revenue. The top performer ('The Venti') earned $78,637 actual revenue at 57% occupancy. Belmont's new-build stock and Macleod Trail access make it one of the strongest emerging STR markets in SW Calgary.

aspen-woods.name: Aspen Woods
aspen-woods.subtitle: West Calgary's definitive luxury enclave — elite private schools, mountain sightlines, and a land-scarce market that sustains premium valuations
aspen-woods.str_intro:
Aspen Woods occupies a prestige position in Calgary's southwest quadrant, bounded by 17 Avenue SW to the north, 85 Street SW to the east, and Highway 8 (Glenmore Trail) to the south, with Stoney Trail providing western ring-road access. Established from 2001 on land annexed in 1956, the community has built out across multiple named sub-neighbourhoods — Aspen Ridge, Aspen Meadows, Aspen Stone, and Aspen Hills — each with distinct lot sizes and price tiers but unified by the overarching prestige positioning. Downtown Calgary is approximately 20 minutes via Bow Trail or 17 Avenue SW; Canmore and Banff are within 60–90 minutes on Highway 1, making Aspen Woods a natural choice for high-income professionals who value both urban proximity and mountain access.

The commercial anchor is Aspen Landing Shopping Centre on Aspenshire Drive SW, which houses Safeway, Starbucks, DeVille Coffee, fitness studios, medical and dental offices, and a range of boutique services. The 69 Street LRT Station provides direct C-Train access to downtown Calgary, representing rare rapid-transit convenience in Calgary's west-end suburban fabric. Westside Recreation Centre on 85 Street SW delivers a full-facility aquatic park, skating rink, climbing wall, and fitness programming. Griffith Woods Park — a provincially significant natural area in adjacent Discovery Ridge — and Edworthy Park to the northeast provide natural pathway corridors used extensively by Aspen Woods residents.

The Aspen Woods housing market sits at a 100–110% premium over the Calgary-wide detached benchmark, with detached homes averaging approximately $1,380,000–$1,530,000 as of early 2026 data. Land scarcity — the community is largely built out — combined with concentrated demand from private school families sustains this premium through market cycles. Aspen Ridge, the estate-tier pocket of the community, contains homes ranging from $1.8M to over $10M and backs onto ravine greenspace. Rundle College (Fraser Institute 9/10), Webber Academy (10/10), and Calgary French & International School (10/10) are all within minutes, making this the strongest private school cluster in Calgary. A West Calgary Catholic High School accommodating 1,230 students is planned to open in 2028 within the community on a 20-acre site.

Griffith Woods Park — a provincially significant natural area in adjacent Discovery Ridge — and Edworthy Park to the northeast provide natural pathway corridors used extensively by Aspen Woods residents.

Rundle College (Fraser Institute 9/10), Webber Academy (10/10), and Calgary French & International School (10/10) are all within minutes, making this the strongest private school cluster in Calgary. A West Calgary Catholic High School accommodating 1,230 students is planned to open in 2028 within the community on a 20-acre site.

renter_intel.title: Thin Rental Supply Creates Premium Yields
renter_intel.description: Aspen Woods has a structurally low rental vacancy rate — Zillow lists only 2 houses for rent at a time in the community — meaning tenants competing for available detached product regularly pay $3,400–$3,500+/month, supporting strong yield on acquisition cost.

renter_intel.title: Private School Family Tenant Profile
renter_intel.description: Families relocating to Calgary specifically for Webber Academy, Calgary French & International School, or Rundle College represent a stable, creditworthy tenant segment who prioritize walkable proximity to school campuses and willingness to pay market-premium rents for 2–4 year terms.

renter_intel.title: Townhome Tier Near Aspen Landing
renter_intel.description: The Aspen Stone pocket closest to Aspen Landing has the community's highest concentration of executive townhomes, priced between $600K–$1.5M to own, attracting professional renters seeking lock-and-leave convenience with LRT access and boutique retail walkability.

whats_nearby.items: Upscale daily-needs retail hub on Aspenshire Drive SW, anchored by Safeway, Starbucks, and DeVille Coffee, with medical, dental, and boutique services in a walkable format.
West-end terminus of the CTrain's 201 Line (Blue Line), providing rapid transit to downtown Calgary and connection to the broader CTrain network, a rare transit asset for this quadrant.
Full-facility recreation centre on 85 Street SW offering an aquatic park, skating rink, fitness gym, and climbing wall, serving as the primary indoor recreation anchor for west Calgary communities.

aspen-woods.peak_season: June–September (mountain gateway, luxury market). Winter secondary for ski season.
aspen-woods.best_property_type: Luxury detached home with mountain views. Aspen Woods' upscale housing stock commands premium rates.
aspen-woods.guest_profile: Luxury travellers, mountain-bound guests, corporate travellers, families visiting west Calgary.
aspen-woods.stand_out: Luxury detached homes with mountain views and high-end finishes. Properties within a 10-minute drive of the Trans-Canada Highway.
aspen-woods.distinctive_fact: Aspen Woods is Calgary's premier luxury mountain gateway neighbourhood — properties here command the highest nightly rates in the west end. Listings that target the luxury traveller segment (high-end finishes, mountain views, proximity to Banff) consistently outperform those targeting budget travellers.

springbank-hill.name: Springbank Hill
springbank-hill.subtitle: West Calgary ridge living — panoramic Rocky Mountain sightlines, walkout estate homes, and Elbow River valley access at the city's edge
springbank-hill.str_intro:
Springbank Hill is one of Calgary's largest communities by land area, occupying the southwest quadrant bounded by 17 Avenue SW to the north, 69 Street SW to the east, Glenmore Trail to the south, and 93 Street SW to the west. Established in 1999 on land annexed in 1994, the neighbourhood has developed across a series of distinct sub-communities — Springborough, Summit of Montreux, Anatapi, Spring Valley, and The Slopes — each positioned along the community's signature topography of rolling hills and ridgeline formations. The community's elevation delivers unobstructed sightlines to the Elbow River valley, the southern foothills, and the Rocky Mountain front range, making Springbank Hill one of the few Calgary communities where mountain views from principal rooms are a defining and widespread feature rather than a select-lot amenity.

Springbank Hill's pathway network connects directly into Griffith Woods Park — a provincially protected natural area along the Elbow River adjacent to Discovery Ridge — and extends north toward Edworthy Park, providing kilometres of trail access from neighbourhood streets. Westhills Towne Centre on 69 Street SW, anchored by Real Canadian Superstore, Winners, and Cineplex Cinemas, serves as the primary commercial hub; Aspen Landing Shopping Centre is accessible along 17 Avenue SW for boutique and daily-service retail. Ernest Manning High School and Rundle College are within the immediate community catchment; Westside Recreation Centre on 85 Street SW provides full-facility programming including aquatic, ice, and fitness amenities within a short drive.

The housing stock centres on luxury single-family detached homes with walkout basements, a feature made possible by the community's hillside topography and highly valued for the elevated deck and natural light access it provides on lower levels. Sub-communities like Mystic Ridge and Summit of Montreux contain premium estate product with home values reaching $2–$3M+; more accessible pricing exists in Springborough and along Spring View Drive SW where townhome and condo inventory provides an entry tier. Avenue Calgary's data places the 2017 median assessed value at $672,000, meaningfully above the city median; current detached averages in community listings consistently exceed $1M. The proximity to Stoney Trail provides a reliable western city-edge connection to Highway 1 for mountain access.

Westhills Towne Centre on 69 Street SW, anchored by Real Canadian Superstore, Winners, and Cineplex Cinemas, serves as the primary commercial hub; Aspen Landing Shopping Centre is accessible along 17 Avenue SW for boutique and daily-service retail. Ernest Manning High School and Rundle College are within the immediate community catchment; Westside Recreation Centre on 85 Street SW provides full-facility programming including aquatic, ice, and fitness amenities within a short drive.

Sub-communities like Mystic Ridge and Summit of Montreux contain premium estate product with home values reaching $2–$3M+; more accessible pricing exists in Springborough and along Spring View Drive SW where townhome and condo inventory provides an entry tier. Avenue Calgary's data places the 2017 median assessed value at $672,000, meaningfully above the city median; current detached averages in community listings consistently exceed $1M. The proximity to Stoney Trail provides a reliable western city-edge connection to Highway 1 for mountain access.

renter_intel.title: Walkout Basements as Secondary Suite Opportunity
renter_intel.description: The community's widespread walkout basement inventory creates a structural opportunity for investors to operate primary-plus-suite configurations, with the lower-level unit benefiting from grade-level access, natural light, and private outdoor space.

renter_intel.title: Low Rental Supply, High Tenant Quality
renter_intel.description: Rental listings in Springbank Hill are sparse — typically under 10 active houses on major platforms — which pushes well-presented detached rentals to $3,500–$5,000+/month for 4-bedroom product, attracting corporate relocation and high-income family tenants.

renter_intel.title: Mountain Access Drives Short-Term Premium
renter_intel.description: Springbank Hill's western positioning on Stoney Trail, with Highway 1 to Banff and Kananaskis Country accessible within 15 minutes, supports above-average nightly rates for furnished short-term rental inventory marketed to outdoor recreation visitors and ski-season guests.

whats_nearby.items: A 92-hectare provincially significant natural area along the Elbow River in adjacent Discovery Ridge, accessible via Springbank Hill's pathway network and providing old-growth forest, river trails, and wildlife habitat.

Major retail and entertainment hub on 69 Street SW anchored by Real Canadian Superstore, Winners, Indigo, and Cineplex Cinemas, serving as Springbank Hill's primary big-box and daily-needs commercial destination.
A major acute-care hospital providing comprehensive medical services within a short drive east, representing a significant employment node for the west Calgary rental market.

springbank-hill.peak_season: June–September (mountain views, proximity to Elbow River). Winter secondary for ski season.
springbank-hill.best_property_type: Newer detached home with walkout basement and mountain views. Springbank Hill's elevated position gives it some of the best mountain views in Calgary.
springbank-hill.guest_profile: Mountain-bound travellers, luxury travellers, families visiting west Calgary.
springbank-hill.stand_out: Homes with mountain views and walkout basements on the west side of the community. Properties near the Elbow River pathway system.
springbank-hill.distinctive_fact: Springbank Hill sits on a ridge that offers unobstructed mountain views. Listings with mountain views consistently command a 20–30% premium over comparable properties without views. The view itself is the product.

parkhill.name: Parkhill
parkhill.subtitle: Inner-city ridge character — Elbow River trails, Stanley Park, Stampede proximity, and one of Calgary's oldest residential streetscapes
parkhill.str_intro:
Parkhill is an inner-city residential neighbourhood established in 1910, making it one of Calgary's oldest continuously inhabited communities. Bounded by Mission Road to the north, Macleod Trail to the east, 42nd Avenue SW to the south, and the Elbow River and Stanley Park to the west, Parkhill sits on a hillside above the Elbow River valley — a topographic position that gives the western edge of the community elevated sightlines toward Stanley Park, the river flats, and the Rocky Mountains beyond. The community is served directly by the 39th Avenue CTrain Station on the 201 (Blue) Line, placing downtown Calgary within minutes without a car, and the 4th Street SW restaurant and retail corridor is accessible on foot or by bike along Mission Road.

Parkhill's street-level identity is shaped by its connection to Stanley Park — a 21-hectare city park running along the Elbow River through the community's western boundary — and its position along the so-called Barley Belt, Calgary's informal descriptor for the cluster of craft breweries and taprooms concentrated along the Erlton and Ramsay corridors. The 33rd Avenue viewpoint on the community's northern ridge offers a celebrated panoramic city view and serves as a prime Stampede fireworks-watching destination each July. A short ride south on the CTrain or cycling via the Elbow River pathway brings residents to Stampede Park — the 100-hectare Calgary Stampede grounds host year-round events beyond the annual rodeo. The off-leash Roxboro Nature Preserve borders Parkhill to the north across Mission Road, and Princess Obolensky Park (within Stanley Park) sits atop the southern ridge with unobstructed river valley and mountain views.

Parkhill's western half operates under a Conservation Area designation, encouraging preservation of the original early-to-mid 20th century housing stock and limiting redevelopment to infill forms compatible with the neighbourhood's character. The result is a textured inventory of character homes — many on hillside streets with lanes and mature tree canopy — alongside selective modern infill that commands significant premiums for ridge positioning and river proximity. The eastern side of the community transitions toward commercial and mixed-use development adjacent to Macleod Trail. Average listing prices for available inventory as tracked by JustinHavre show an average of approximately $1.69M across current listings, with the highest-tier property at $3.45M, reflecting the depth of the inner-city prestige market here. CTrain access, 4th Street walkability, and the proximity to both Stampede Park and the Mission dining district underpin Parkhill's persistent appeal to the urban professional renter segment.
parkhill.str_lifestyle: Parkhill's street-level identity is shaped by its connection to Stanley Park — a 21-hectare city park running along the Elbow River through the community's western boundary — and its position along the so-called Barley Belt, Calgary's informal descriptor for the cluster of craft breweries and taprooms concentrated along the Erlton and Ramsay corridors. The 33rd Avenue viewpoint on the community's northern ridge offers a celebrated panoramic city view and serves as a prime Stampede fireworks-watching destination each July. A short ride south on the CTrain or cycling via the Elbow River pathway brings residents to Stampede Park — the 100-hectare Calgary Stampede grounds host year-round events beyond the annual rodeo. The off-leash Roxboro Nature Preserve borders Parkhill to the north across Mission Road, and Princess Obolensky Park (within Stanley Park) sits atop the southern ridge with unobstructed river valley and mountain views.
parkhill.str_housing: Parkhill's western half operates under a Conservation Area designation, encouraging preservation of the original early-to-mid 20th century housing stock and limiting redevelopment to infill forms compatible with the neighbourhood's character. The result is a textured inventory of character homes — many on hillside streets with lanes and mature tree canopy — alongside selective modern infill that commands significant premiums for ridge positioning and river proximity. The eastern side of the community transitions toward commercial and mixed-use development adjacent to Macleod Trail. Average listing prices for available inventory as tracked by JustinHavre show an average of approximately $1.69M across current listings, with the highest-tier property at $3.45M, reflecting the depth of the inner-city prestige market here. CTrain access, 4th Street walkability, and the proximity to both Stampede Park and the Mission dining district underpin Parkhill's persistent appeal to the urban professional renter segment.

renter_intel.title: Urban Professional Tenant Demand
renter_intel.description: Proximity to the 39th Avenue CTrain Station, the 4th Street Mission dining district, and downtown Calgary by bike positions Parkhill as a high-demand address for car-light urban professionals willing to pay inner-city premiums for transit access and walkable lifestyle.

renter_intel.title: Conservation Area Constrains Supply
renter_intel.description: The western half's Conservation Area designation limits new supply additions, creating structural scarcity that sustains rental rates and supports long-term value retention for character home investors.

renter_intel.title: Stampede and Event Season Uplift
renter_intel.description: Parkhill's 10-minute cycling or CTrain distance to Stampede Park makes it one of Calgary's best-positioned communities for July Stampede STR rate spikes, with demand extending to other Scotiabank Saddledome and BMO Centre events throughout the year.

whats_nearby.items: A 21-hectare city park along the Elbow River valley directly adjacent to Parkhill's western edge, offering disc golf, paddling access, pathways, and the Princess Obolensky ridge viewpoint.
Calgary's densest concentration of independent restaurants, wine bars, and specialty retail, accessible on foot via Mission Road along Parkhill's northern boundary.
The 100-hectare Calgary Stampede campus and convention complex, reachable in under 10 minutes by CTrain or bike via the Elbow River pathway, generating year-round event-driven visitor demand.

parkhill.peak_season: July (Stampede proximity and Elbow River access). Strong through September.
parkhill.best_property_type: Character home with legal suite near the Elbow River.
parkhill.stand_out: Character homes with legal suites near the Elbow River. Properties with downtown views on the ridge.
parkhill.distinctive_fact: Parkhill sits on the ridge above the Elbow River with views toward downtown — its combination of Stampede proximity and Elbow River access creates a strong STR niche.

montgomery.name: Montgomery
montgomery.subtitle: Bow River Access, Hospital Corridor, and Northwest Urban Upside
montgomery.str_intro:
Montgomery occupies the northwest bend of the Bow River, bounded by Shaganappi Trail to the east, 32nd Avenue and Market Mall to the north, and the river to the south and west. The community was originally established in 1911 and absorbed into the City of Calgary in 1963, leaving it with a distinct street grid and a mixed housing stock that ranges from post-war bungalows to new infill construction along Bowness Road NW. With a 2021 population of 4,175 across approximately 1,873 properties, the neighbourhood remains one of the more compact inner-northwest communities. Average assessed value reached $729,065 in 2024—about 5% below the city-wide average—while year-over-year appreciation of 15.4% outpaced the city average of 14.0%.

The neighbourhood's outdoor draw centres on Shouldice Athletic Park, which sits directly across the Bow River and offers baseball diamonds, soccer fields, and a swimming complex, connected to Montgomery via the Bow River Pathway. The Trans-Canada Highway corridor along the community's northern edge hosts the Calgary Farmers' Market at Market Mall, along with everyday retail anchors including Real Canadian Superstore and Safeway. Locally, Bowness Road NW provides a walkable commercial strip with cafés, the award-winning NOtaBLE restaurant, and neighbourhood-scale services. A meaningful share of residents commute on foot or by transit—about 8% use public transit, and the walkability score reflects proximity to several bus routes and the Banff Trail CTrain station roughly two kilometres east.

Montgomery's housing mix skews toward detached single-family homes and semi-detached infills on the original townsite lots, with a smaller share of condominiums and basement suites that account for much of the rental supply. Renter households represented roughly 33% of dwellings as of the last census, a rate that has likely grown alongside the recent infill cycle. New basement suites in brand-new infills along Bowness Road NW were listing at approximately $1,350/month (600 sq ft, utilities included) in 2025, while four-bedroom main-floor units were advertising closer to $1,900/month all-in. The community's proximity to Foothills Medical Centre, Alberta Children's Hospital, and the University of Calgary (all within five minutes by car) sustains a durable pool of healthcare and academic renters.
montgomery.str_lifestyle: The neighbourhood's outdoor draw centres on Shouldice Athletic Park, which sits directly across the Bow River and offers baseball diamonds, soccer fields, and a swimming complex, connected to Montgomery via the Bow River Pathway. The Trans-Canada Highway corridor along the community's northern edge hosts the Calgary Farmers' Market at Market Mall, along with everyday retail anchors including Real Canadian Superstore and Safeway. Locally, Bowness Road NW provides a walkable commercial strip with cafés, the award-winning NOtaBLE restaurant, and neighbourhood-scale services. A meaningful share of residents commute on foot or by transit—about 8% use public transit, and the walkability score reflects proximity to several bus routes and the Banff Trail CTrain station roughly two kilometres east.
