The community's proximity to Foothills Medical Centre, Alberta Children's Hospital, and the University of Calgary (all within five minutes by car) sustains a durable pool of healthcare and academic renters.

renter_intel.title: Heads Up — Flood Awareness
renter_intel.description: Lower Montgomery sits in the Bow River flood plain. 2013 flooding affected parts of the neighbourhood. For basement suites near the river, ask about flood history and insurance.

renter_intel.title: Local Insight — Two Commercial Strips, Two Vibes
renter_intel.description: Bowness Road is the new walkable main street with bike lanes and infill. 16 Ave NW / Trans-Canada is the older car-oriented strip with vintage motels and fast food. Pick based on what you want walking distance.

renter_intel.title: Transit Tip
renter_intel.description: No LRT directly in Montgomery. Express bus to downtown is efficient, and University and hospital workers can bus or bike in under 10 minutes. Car still useful for everything else.

renter_intel.title: Pet Resume Tip
renter_intel.description: Most newer buildings welcome pets (often with a $25-50/month fee). Older basement suites and walk-ups vary — ask early. Bow River Pathway makes this a great dog neighbourhood.

whats_nearby.items: A large multi-sport facility across the Bow River offering baseball, soccer, football fields, and an aquatic centre, connected to Montgomery via the Bow River Pathway.

whats_nearby.category: Market Mall and Calgary Farmers' Market
whats_nearby.items: Calgary's largest enclosed mall and the adjacent year-round Farmers' Market are both within the community's northern boundary at 32nd Avenue NW, providing grocery, dining, and retail within walking distance.

whats_nearby.category: Foothills Medical Centre and Alberta Children's Hospital
whats_nearby.items: Two of Calgary's largest hospital campuses are a five-minute drive east on 16th Avenue NW, making Montgomery a top-of-list community for travel nurses, residents, and medical researchers.

montgomery.peak_season: June–September (Bow River access, proximity to Foothills Hospital and University of Calgary). Winter secondary for ski season.
montgomery.best_property_type: Character bungalow with legal suite near the Bow River or the University of Calgary.
montgomery.guest_profile: Medical travellers, university visitors, mountain-bound travellers, outdoor enthusiasts.
montgomery.stand_out: Character bungalows with legal suites near the Bow River or Shouldice Park. Properties within a 15-minute drive of Foothills Hospital.
montgomery.distinctive_fact: Montgomery sits at the confluence of the Bow River and Shouldice Park — its combination of river access, proximity to Foothills Hospital, and mountain gateway position creates a diverse demand base that is more resilient than single-driver neighbourhoods.

health.name: Holistic Institute of Health and Fertility

montgomery.ltr_description:
Montgomery is the inner-city neighbourhood that punches well above its weight. Tucked along the Bow River in Calgary's northwest, just below Foothills Medical Centre and Alberta Children's Hospital, and a 5-minute drive from both the University of Calgary and Market Mall, Montgomery delivers a remarkably specific renter pitch: river access, healthcare/university proximity, and a quick-exit route to the Rocky Mountains via the Trans-Canada Highway. Originally incorporated as its own town in 1911 and annexed by Calgary in 1963, Montgomery has its own identity (and its own Business Improvement Area along Bowness Road/Montgomery Way). Avenue Magazine has named it a Top 10 community for resale potential. The neighbourhood is in the middle of a quiet renaissance — new mixed-use builds like Montgomery Square (52 homes, 9 commercial spaces), the upcoming Monty 44 (110 homes), and a wave of craft businesses (Rising Tides Tap Room, Fringe Coffee, NOtaBLE) have transformed Bowness Road into one of the city's more interesting up-and-coming main streets.

Montgomery has two distinct halves. Upper Montgomery sits on the river escarpment with quick access to Bowness Road, University District, and Market Mall — walkable, increasingly modern, with new infills and mid-rise condos. Lower Montgomery sits in the Bow River flood plain, closer to the water, with older bungalows and a more village feel. Bowness Road is the main commercial spine and has been transformed with a dedicated bike lane, new streetscaping, and a fast-growing roster of businesses: NOtaBLE for award-winning wood-fired rotisserie, Fringe Coffee for warm-living-room vibes, Lily Vietnamese Submarines for banh mi, Wiener Schnitzel Haus for authentic Austrian, Pazzer's Pub for perogies and beer, Papa Nikolas for pizza, Rising Tides Tap Room for craft beer. The 16 Ave NW/Trans-Canada side is the more car-oriented commercial strip — vintage motels, gas stations, fast food. Compared to Bowness's retro funkiness or Varsity's upscale family feel, Montgomery sits in the middle: more urban than Bowness, less polished than Varsity, with the best river access of the three.

Montgomery's rental stock is varied: older walk-up apartments along Bowness Road and 16 Ave, basement and secondary suites in mid-century bungalows, and a growing supply of new condos and townhomes from the infill wave (Montgomery Square at $1,580+ for one bedroom, Monty 44 coming). In-suite laundry is standard in new builds and many recent renovations. Parking is generally easier than in inner-city neighbourhoods further east — many units include a stall, and street parking is free. The neighbourhood's #1 renter draw is medical/university worker proximity: Foothills, Alberta Children's, the U of C, and the University Research Park are all within 5-10 minutes. The 16 Ave/Trans-Canada commercial side can be noisy — Bowness Road and Memorial Drive are quieter. Bow River flood risk is worth understanding for lower-lying basement suites (2013 flooding affected parts of the area). Express bus to downtown is solid; no LRT directly in the community.

ltr_vibe_tags.title: Bow River at Your Door
ltr_vibe_tags.description: Shouldice Park, Edworthy Park access, Paddle Station rentals, and the Bow River Pathway run right through Montgomery. Bike downtown in 20 minutes.

ltr_vibe_tags.title: Hospital & University Adjacent
ltr_vibe_tags.description: 5-10 minutes to Foothills Medical Centre, Alberta Children's Hospital, the U of C, and the University Research Park. Massive rental demand from healthcare and academic workers.

ltr_vibe_tags.title: Bowness Road Renaissance
ltr_vibe_tags.description: NOtaBLE, Fringe Coffee, Lily Vietnamese, Rising Tides Tap Room, Pazzer's Pub — an up-and-coming main street with a craft business mix.

ltr_vibe_tags.title: Mountain Quick-Exit
ltr_vibe_tags.description: Trans-Canada Highway right there means Canmore is 45 minutes, Banff is 60. Easiest mountain access of any inner-city neighbourhood.

ltr_vibe_tags.title: Inner-City Without the Inner-City Price
ltr_vibe_tags.description: More affordable than Hillhurst or Sunnyside, more new construction than Bowness, with comparable amenity access.

parkdale.name: Parkdale
parkdale.subtitle: Riverside Inner-City Pocket Anchored by Calgary's Medical Corridor
parkdale.str_intro:
Parkdale is a compact, mature inner-city neighbourhood on the north bank of the Bow River, sandwiched between Point McKay to the west and West Hillhurst to the east, with 28 Street NW forming the eastern boundary and Shaganappi Trail NW to the west. The area was annexed by Calgary in 1910 but saw most of its residential development from the late 1940s onward, and it retains that post-war bungalow character alongside a growing stock of modern infills and duplexes. Foothills Medical Centre occupies the elevated plateau immediately north of the community, and Alberta Children's Hospital is minutes away, making Parkdale one of the most hospital-proximate residential neighbourhoods in the city. Memorial Drive forms the southern edge and provides direct downtown access as well as connections to Highway 1 west toward the mountains.

Parkdale Boulevard NW is the community's primary north-south artery, running from the Bow River Pathway directly up to the hospital campus above, and it functions as a commuter spine for the large share of residents who work at the medical centre. The Bow River Pathway passes along the southern edge of the community, connecting to Edworthy Park to the west and the inner-city pathway network east toward downtown. Karl Baker off-leash dog park sits adjacent to the community and is a consistent daily draw, while Shouldice Athletic Park is reachable within minutes via the pathway. The Lazy Loaf & Kettle, a longstanding neighbourhood café and bakery, anchors local daytime retail activity, and the Kensington commercial district with Una Pizza and The Village Ice Cream is a short walk east along 10th Street NW.

Parkdale's housing stock divides between original post-war bungalows on large lots—many of which are targets for custom infill redevelopment—and a growing number of modern duplexes and luxury infills commanding $1.1M+ for detached product. The Braunrealestate.ca market dashboard showed 24 active listings with an average price of $1,170,258 and average price per square foot of $666 as of late 2025. Condominiums and townhomes are present but represent a minority of the stock; their scarcity supports above-average asking rents for suites in owner-occupied infills. The neighbourhood's renter mix skews heavily toward Foothills Medical Centre staff, University of Calgary students and faculty, and downtown professionals using Memorial Drive for a seven-minute commute.

The Bow River Pathway passes along the southern edge of the community, connecting to Edworthy Park to the west and the inner-city pathway network east toward downtown. Karl Baker off-leash dog park sits adjacent to the community and is a consistent daily draw, while Shouldice Athletic Park is reachable within minutes via the pathway. The Lazy Loaf & Kettle, a longstanding neighbourhood café and bakery, anchors local daytime retail activity, and the Kensington commercial district with Una Pizza and The Village Ice Cream is a short walk east along 10th Street NW.

Condominiums and townhomes are present but represent a minority of the stock; their scarcity supports above-average asking rents for suites in owner-occupied infills. The neighbourhood's renter mix skews heavily toward Foothills Medical Centre staff, University of Calgary students and faculty, and downtown professionals using Memorial Drive for a seven-minute commute.

renter_intel.title: Transit Tip
renter_intel.description: No LRT in Parkdale, but the Banff Trail CTrain Station is a 15-min walk over Crowchild Trail. Memorial Drive express buses get you downtown in ~10 minutes.

renter_intel.title: Heads Up — Major Road Noise
renter_intel.description: Memorial Drive and Crowchild Trail are loud. The interior streets are quiet, but rentals fronting either road can be noisy day and night.

renter_intel.title: Local Insight — Hospital Demand
renter_intel.description: Rental demand spikes when medical residents rotate (typically late June/early July). If you're not a healthcare worker, expect competition.

renter_intel.title: Pet Resume Tip
renter_intel.description: Many older walk-ups and individual landlords are pet-friendly. The pathway system and Karl Baker Off-Leash Park (in St. Andrews Heights, short walk) make this great dog territory.

whats_nearby.items: One of Alberta's largest acute-care hospital campuses, including the Tom Baker Cancer Centre, sits on the escarpment directly north of Parkdale's residential core.

whats_nearby.category: Bow River Pathway and Karl Baker Off-Leash Park
whats_nearby.items: The Bow River Pathway runs along Parkdale's southern boundary, connecting to the Karl Baker off-leash dog park and giving residents immediate access to the city-wide pathway network.
Calgary's most walkable urban shopping district, centered on Kensington Road NW, is a short walk east and offers independent restaurants, cafés, boutiques, and grocery access.

parkdale.peak_season: June–September (Foothills Hospital proximity, Bow River access). Football season secondary.
parkdale.best_property_type: Character bungalow with legal suite near Foothills Hospital or the Bow River pathway.
parkdale.guest_profile: Medical travellers, university visitors, outdoor enthusiasts, mountain-bound travellers.
parkdale.stand_out: Character bungalows with legal suites within walking distance of Foothills Hospital. Properties near the Bow River pathway system.
parkdale.distinctive_fact: Parkdale sits between Foothills Hospital and the Bow River — its combination of medical travel demand and outdoor recreation access creates a demand base that is more resilient than single-driver neighbourhoods.

health.name: Calgary Laboratory Services Patient Service C...

parkdale.ltr_description:
Parkdale is a small, quiet inner-city neighbourhood (population ~2,000) tucked along the north bank of the Bow River between West Hillhurst and Point McKay, sitting directly below the Foothills Hospital embankment. It's one of Calgary's most location-efficient communities for a very specific kind of renter: medical professionals working at Foothills Medical Centre or Alberta Children's Hospital, students at the University of Calgary, and anyone who wants Bow River Pathway access right out their back door. Originally annexed in 1910 but mostly developed as a post-WWII bungalow neighbourhood (Wartime Housing Models and Victory Housing from the late 1940s), Parkdale has been in transformation for the last 20+ years — old bungalows being replaced by modern infills, duplexes, and a small but growing wave of condos and townhomes. The result: a hidden-pocket community with rural-quiet streets, river access, hospital proximity, and a quick downtown commute via Memorial Drive.

Parkdale feels like an inner-city neighbourhood that forgot to get loud. The Bow River pathway runs along the southern edge with constant cyclist and pedestrian traffic, but the residential streets themselves are remarkably quiet — partly because Parkdale is bounded by river, hospital, and major roads, creating a natural pocket that doesn't see through-traffic. The community has a strong Community Association running events year-round (community garden, parade of garage sales, clean-up days, sporting teams, and an outdoor skating rink that converts to beach volleyball in summer). Three-quarters of residents have post-secondary degrees, reflecting the academic/medical lean. Compared to Hillhurst's commercial buzz and Bowness's funkier vibe, Parkdale is the quiet older sibling — fewer restaurants and shops in the community itself, but easy walks across to Kensington Village to the east or the University District to the north. The Riley House (a 1910 heritage farmhouse) sits below the St. Andrews Heights escarpment, reflecting the area's pioneer ranching history.

Parkdale's rental stock is varied but limited: older walk-up apartments along Memorial Drive and toward the river, basement and legal secondary suites in 1950s bungalows, newer infill townhomes and duplexes (often with two-bed legal basement suites), and a small number of modern condo developments. Many bungalow basement suites have separate entrances and full kitchens — popular with medical residents and grad students. Student demand from the U of C (a 10-minute bus or 15-minute walk over the Crowchild Trail bridge) is strong; healthcare worker demand from Foothills is constant. In-suite laundry varies — common in newer infills, less so in older walk-ups and basement suites. Parking is generally easier than in Hillhurst or Sunnyside since the housing stock is mostly low-density and many properties include driveways. Memorial Drive and Crowchild Trail are loud — rentals directly on them trade quiet for the convenience. Worth noting: 32% of Parkdale households spend 30%+ of income on shelter, so it's not necessarily cheap despite the lower-key feel.

ltr_vibe_tags.title: Bow River at Your Doorstep
ltr_vibe_tags.description: Parkdale Boulevard runs parallel to the river; the Bow River Pathway loops east to the Peace Bridge and west to Edworthy Park via the Harry Booth bridge.

ltr_vibe_tags.title: Hospital Worker Magnet
ltr_vibe_tags.description: Foothills Medical Centre and the new Cancer Centre are a 5-minute walk uphill. Alberta Children's Hospital and U of C are 5 minutes by car.

ltr_vibe_tags.title: Pocket-Quiet Streets
ltr_vibe_tags.description: Bounded by river, hospital, and major roads — no through-traffic. One of the quietest inner-city neighbourhoods in Calgary.

ltr_vibe_tags.title: Mountain Quick-Exit
ltr_vibe_tags.description: Trans-Canada Highway is right there — Banff in 60 minutes, Canmore in 45.

ltr_vibe_tags.title: Infill Wave
ltr_vibe_tags.description: Old wartime bungalows steadily being replaced by modern infills. The neighbourhood is changing block by block.

currie-barracks.name: Currie Barracks
currie-barracks.subtitle: Master-Planned Southwest Redevelopment on Former CFB Calgary Lands
currie-barracks.str_intro:
Currie Barracks occupies 195 acres of former Canadian Forces Base Calgary lands in inner southwest Calgary, bounded by Crowchild Trail to the east, Richardson Way SW to the south, and Sarcee Road to the west—a ten-minute drive from the downtown core. The base, originally opened in 1935 and named after General Sir Arthur Currie, was decommissioned in 1998 and subsequently transferred to Canada Lands Company for comprehensive mixed-use redevelopment. The redevelopment plan calls for approximately 5,700 housing units of various types, 250,000 square feet of retail, roughly 613,000 square feet of office space, a private school, and nearly 23 acres of parks and open space at full build-out. Heritage buildings from the military era—including the Officers' Mess along Dieppe Avenue SW—have been preserved and repurposed, including The Inn on Officers' Garden, which operates as a dining and hospitality venue.

Currie's internal street network is designed around a walkable main street with retail canopies for weather protection, pedestrian mews, and bike lanes throughout, with bus-rapid transit access within a five-minute walk of all residents per the master plan. The community is flanked by Mount Royal University directly across Crowchild Trail—a two-minute drive or a short walk via dedicated pathways—and Marda Loop's established commercial district is accessible in approximately five minutes via 33rd Avenue SW, providing access to independent restaurants, specialty grocers, breweries, and boutique fitness studios. Currie Bark Park and Airplane Park (a heritage reference to the former RCAF airstrip) provide internal green space, and the community's tree-lined boulevards and heritage building streetscapes distinguish it visually from other new southwest Calgary developments. Wild Rose Brewery and Barracks Fitness currently operate within the redevelopment footprint.

The housing mix at Currie spans rowhouses, stacked townhomes, and mid-rise condo buildings, with a deliberate blend of market and 20%-below-market units integrated into each development phase per Canada Lands Company requirements. Burma Star Road SW is the primary residential address, with the Axess condo complex at 95 Burma Star Road SW representing the largest purpose-built rental and ownership inventory. One-bedroom suites in the Axess building were trading in the $325,000–$360,000 ownership range as of early 2025, with rental listings in the $1,850–$2,200 range for one-bedroom units with underground parking. The community's density—13 times higher than the Calgary average per the CNU—combined with walkability and MRU proximity makes it a strong STR and mid-term rental candidate, though overall supply remains limited relative to demand given the ongoing phased build-out.

The community is flanked by Mount Royal University directly across Crowchild Trail—a two-minute drive or a short walk via dedicated pathways—and Marda Loop's established commercial district is accessible in approximately five minutes via 33rd Avenue SW, providing access to independent restaurants, specialty grocers, breweries, and boutique fitness studios. Currie Bark Park and Airplane Park (a heritage reference to the former RCAF airstrip) provide internal green space, and the community's tree-lined boulevards and heritage building streetscapes distinguish it visually from other new southwest Calgary developments. Wild Rose Brewery and Barracks Fitness currently operate within the redevelopment footprint.

Burma Star Road SW is the primary residential address, with the Axess condo complex at 95 Burma Star Road SW representing the largest purpose-built rental and ownership inventory. One-bedroom suites in the Axess building were trading in the $325,000–$360,000 ownership range as of early 2025, with rental listings in the $1,850–$2,200 range for one-bedroom units with underground parking. The community's density—13 times higher than the Calgary average per the CNU—combined with walkability and MRU proximity makes it a strong STR and mid-term rental candidate, though overall supply remains limited relative to demand given the ongoing phased build-out.

renter_intel.title: Mount Royal University Catchment
renter_intel.description: Mount Royal University is directly across Crowchild Trail—a walkable distance—drawing a consistent pool of students and faculty who prefer off-campus housing in a fully amenitized urban environment.

renter_intel.title: Limited Rental Supply in Active Build Phase
renter_intel.description: Currie averages roughly one to two rental listings per month according to Zumper, and the phased Canada Lands build-out means supply additions are gradual—keeping vacancy structurally low for early-positioned investors.

renter_intel.title: Below-Market Unit Integration
renter_intel.description: Canada Lands Company mandates that 20% of units in new phases be priced 30% below market, which moderates overall average rents but also diversifies the renter demographic and stabilizes occupancy across phases.

whats_nearby.items: One of Calgary's most active outdoor shopping and dining corridors, centred on 33rd Avenue SW, is approximately five minutes from Currie and offers independent restaurants, cafés, breweries, and boutique retail.
A full-service university directly across Crowchild Trail with approximately 12,000 students, providing a built-in renter base and walkable academic employment for Currie residents.

A heritage-building hospitality venue within Currie's own footprint along Dieppe Avenue SW, housing a restaurant, café, and craft beverage offerings that serve as the community's internal social anchor.

currie-barracks.peak_season: June–September (Marda Loop proximity, Mount Royal University). Long weekends.
currie-barracks.best_property_type: Newer infill or townhome near Marda Loop or Mount Royal University.
currie-barracks.guest_profile: Boutique travellers, Marda Loop visitors, Mount Royal University visitors, families.
currie-barracks.stand_out: Newer infill homes near the Currie commercial area and Marda Loop. Properties with private yards.
currie-barracks.distinctive_fact: Currie Barracks is a planned community with a strong sense of place — its heritage buildings, tree-lined streets, and proximity to Marda Loop create a guest experience that feels distinctive. Listings that market the neighbourhood's history and character see stronger bookings.

university-district.name: University District
university-district.subtitle: Calgary's Only LEED-ND Platinum Community, Steps from UCalgary
university-district.str_intro:
University District is a 200-acre master-planned mixed-use community on land endowed to the University of Calgary, developed and operated by University of Calgary Properties Group. Located in northwest Calgary immediately adjacent to the UCalgary main campus, the community occupies a sloped site overlooking the Bow River Valley and is bordered by Crowchild Trail to the east and 32nd Avenue NW to the south. It is Alberta's first—and as of publication, only—LEED Neighbourhood Development (LEED-ND) Platinum certified community, a designation reflecting its integrated approach to energy efficiency, transit connectivity, green space, and sustainable building design. The community is served by MAX transit stations providing enhanced bus-rapid transit service and is within walking distance of the University CTrain station on the Red Line.

The Retail Main Street running through the centre of University District is the community's commercial and social spine, featuring fine dining, coffee shops, a cinema, grocery, and specialty retail in a pedestrian-first streetscape with weather-protection features suited to Calgary's climate. Central Commons Park serves as the four-season outdoor gathering anchor, positioned centrally between the main residential buildings and the main street. The community includes 12 kilometres of interconnected pathways and 40 acres of greenspace, connecting to the broader northwest pathway network and to UCalgary's campus. Market Mall is within a five-minute drive north on 32nd Avenue NW, and both Foothills Medical Centre and Alberta Children's Hospital are reachable in under ten minutes, giving University District an unusually strong employment and amenity catchment for a community still in active build-out.

University District's residential product is almost exclusively condominiums and townhomes—entry-level condos at Dean's Landing and Argyle start in the mid-$300,000s, while larger two-bedroom suites at Autumn and Esquire sit in the $500,000–$700,000 range. The development has publicly listed starting prices from approximately $334,900, with new 2026 projects Cadence (condos) and Vanguard (townhomes) by Luxuria Homes representing the latest phase additions. Purpose-built rental units are available within the community, positioned alongside ownership product. The 'Rare' rental supply characterization in the context data reflects the fact that individual condo owners who rent out units represent most of the STR and MTR inventory—there is no large rental-specific building dominating supply—which keeps available listings thin and supports premium rent-per-square-foot.
university-district.str_lifestyle: The Retail Main Street running through the centre of University District is the community's commercial and social spine, featuring fine dining, coffee shops, a cinema, grocery, and specialty retail in a pedestrian-first streetscape with weather-protection features suited to Calgary's climate. Central Commons Park serves as the four-season outdoor gathering anchor, positioned centrally between the main residential buildings and the main street. The community includes 12 kilometres of interconnected pathways and 40 acres of greenspace, connecting to the broader northwest pathway network and to UCalgary's campus. Market Mall is within a five-minute drive north on 32nd Avenue NW, and both Foothills Medical Centre and Alberta Children's Hospital are reachable in under ten minutes, giving University District an unusually strong employment and amenity catchment for a community still in active build-out.
university-district.str_housing: University District's residential product is almost exclusively condominiums and townhomes—entry-level condos at Dean's Landing and Argyle start in the mid-$300,000s, while larger two-bedroom suites at Autumn and Esquire sit in the $500,000–$700,000 range. The development has publicly listed starting prices from approximately $334,900, with new 2026 projects Cadence (condos) and Vanguard (townhomes) by Luxuria Homes representing the latest phase additions. Purpose-built rental units are available within the community, positioned alongside ownership product. The 'Rare' rental supply characterization in the context data reflects the fact that individual condo owners who rent out units represent most of the STR and MTR inventory—there is no large rental-specific building dominating supply—which keeps available listings thin and supports premium rent-per-square-foot.

renter_intel.title: UCalgary Staff and Graduate Student Base
renter_intel.description: University District is physically connected to UCalgary's campus and is the preferred residential choice for graduate students, postdoctoral fellows, and faculty who prioritize walkable access to campus amenities.

renter_intel.title: Condo-Only Supply Creates Scarcity Premium
renter_intel.description: The absence of purpose-built large rental towers means individual condo owners control the STR and MTR inventory, keeping available listings rare and supporting above-average rents relative to the northwest Calgary average.

renter_intel.title: Hospital and Medical Research Proximity
renter_intel.description: Foothills Medical Centre and Alberta Children's Hospital are both within a ten-minute drive, and the community's transit connections extend its appeal to healthcare workers who do not own vehicles.

whats_nearby.items: The UCalgary campus is directly adjacent, with pedestrian and pathway connections making it accessible without a vehicle—a defining advantage for student, faculty, and researcher renters.
An active pedestrian commercial street within the community itself offers grocery, dining, entertainment, and services, reducing residents' dependence on off-site retail.

One of Calgary's largest enclosed shopping centres is approximately five minutes north on 32nd Avenue NW, providing major-brand retail, grocers, and dining not yet fully replicated in the community's own retail build-out.

university-district.peak_season: May–August (convocation season, summer conferences). September–November for football season.
university-district.best_property_type: Condo in a STR-permissive building within the University District development.
university-district.guest_profile: Visiting academics, conference attendees, families of students, sports event attendees.
university-district.stand_out: Condos in STR-permissive buildings within walking distance of the University of Calgary campus and the University District commercial area.
university-district.distinctive_fact: University District is Calgary's newest master-planned community, designed around walkability and the University of Calgary campus. Its purpose-built retail, restaurants, and amenities create a guest experience that older university-adjacent communities cannot match.

spruce-cliff.name: Spruce Cliff
spruce-cliff.subtitle: Ridge-Top Condos, Golf Course Views, and Bow River Pathway Access in Southwest Calgary
spruce-cliff.str_intro:
Spruce Cliff is a southwest Calgary neighbourhood bounded by the CPR tracks and the Bow River to the north, Shaganappi Point Golf Course and 33 Street SW to the east, Bow Trail to the south, and 38 Street SW to the west—a footprint of approximately 1.1 square kilometres. The community was officially established in 1950 on land annexed in 1910 and has a 2021 population of 4,195 across roughly 2,929 properties. Its defining physical feature is the community escarpment along Spruce Drive SW, from which residents have direct sight lines to the Bow River valley, downtown Calgary, and the front ranges of the Rocky Mountains. Average assessed property value reached $362,820 in 2024—53% below the city average—while year-over-year appreciation of 18.2% outpaced the city average of 14.0%, reflecting the neighbourhood's growing investor recognition.

Edworthy Park, accessible directly from the community's northern edge, provides year-round outdoor recreation including the Quarry Road Trail and Douglas Fir Trail hiking paths, river access, off-leash areas, and cycling routes that connect to the broader Bow River Pathway system. The Shaganappi Point Golf Course abuts the community to the east and doubles as a cross-country ski venue in winter, offering a rare year-round outdoor amenity immediately at residents' doorstep. The community's escarpment location on Spruce Drive SW provides some of the most dramatic unobstructed downtown and mountain views available in a southwest inner-city address. Westbrook Mall and the Westbrook LRT station are a five-minute walk east, providing grocery access (Walmart, Safeway), a Calgary Public Library branch (Nicholls Family Library), and direct LRT service to downtown in approximately five minutes.

Spruce Cliff's housing stock splits roughly 53% condominiums and apartments to 47% single-family and townhome product, with a renter-to-owner ratio of approximately 59:41 as of the 2021 Census—one of the highest renter concentrations of any southwest Calgary community. The Ovation, Encore, and Copperwood high-rise buildings along Hemlock Crescent SW and Spruce Place SW anchor the condo segment, offering one- and two-bedroom suites with golf course and downtown views in the $295,000–$380,000 ownership range. Condo fees in these established buildings remain moderate, supported by proactive strata boards, and rental listings in the $1,400–$1,800 range for one-bedroom units are consistent with southwest Calgary norms. The neighbourhood's low assessed values relative to the rest of the inner city, combined with the Westbrook LRT connection and Edworthy Park access, create a durable value proposition for long-term hold investors.

The Shaganappi Point Golf Course abuts the community to the east and doubles as a cross-country ski venue in winter, offering a rare year-round outdoor amenity immediately at residents' doorstep. The community's escarpment location on Spruce Drive SW provides some of the most dramatic unobstructed downtown and mountain views available in a southwest inner-city address. renter_intel.title: High Renter Concentration (59%) Creates Reliable Demand
renter_intel.description: Spruce Cliff's 59% renter rate, well above the Calgary average of 31%, reflects deeply embedded rental demand in a community where condo and apartment stock dominates and ownership barriers remain real.

renter_intel.title: Westbrook LRT and Five-Minute Downtown Commute
renter_intel.description: The Westbrook CTrain station is a five-minute walk from most of the condo cluster on Spruce Place SW and Hemlock Crescent SW, making Spruce Cliff one of the best-connected non-downtown SW communities for transit-dependent renters.

renter_intel.title: Below-City Average Entry Prices Support Investor Yield
renter_intel.description: With assessed values averaging $362,820—53% below the city-wide average—and rents tracking alongside the broader inner-city market, Spruce Cliff offers yield-to-price ratios that are difficult to replicate in comparably located communities.

whats_nearby.category: Edworthy Park and Douglas Fir Trail
whats_nearby.items: A large natural park on the north bank of the Bow River with the heritage Quarry Road Trail and Douglas Fir Trail, offering hiking, cycling, river access, and off-leash areas directly from the community's northern boundary.

An 18-hole public golf course bordering the community to the east that converts to a free cross-country ski venue in winter, providing year-round recreational value within walking distance of most Spruce Cliff residences.
A connected shopping centre housing Walmart, Safeway, and a Calgary Public Library branch, with the Westbrook CTrain station providing direct five-minute service to downtown Calgary.

spruce-cliff.peak_season: June–September (Bow River access, proximity to Edworthy Park). Mountain gateway secondary.
spruce-cliff.best_property_type: Character bungalow with legal suite near Edworthy Park or the Bow River pathway.
spruce-cliff.guest_profile: Outdoor enthusiasts, mountain-bound travellers, couples seeking a riverside stay.
spruce-cliff.stand_out: Character bungalows with legal suites near Edworthy Park or the Bow River pathway. Properties with river or park views.
spruce-cliff.distinctive_fact: Spruce Cliff sits on the ridge above Edworthy Park and the Bow River — properties with river or park views see strong bookings from outdoor enthusiasts who want a scenic stay without the Bowness price premium.

windsor-park.name: Windsor Park
windsor-park.subtitle: Inner-City Access, Elbow River Edge — Calgary's Quiet Overachiever
windsor-park.str_intro:
Windsor Park sits at a genuinely advantageous address in Calgary's inner southwest, bounded by Elbow Drive to the west, Macleod Trail to the east, 50 Avenue SW to the north, and 58 Avenue SW to the south. The neighbourhood traces its roots to 1940 and was annexed to the city in 1951, giving it a maturity and street-tree canopy that newer communities cannot replicate. Population stands at 4,410 (2021 Census), with 38 percent of residents aged 20–39 — a cohort that indexes strongly for rental demand. Year-over-year assessed values rose 20.9 percent to an average of $648,983, outpacing the city-wide 14 percent gain, signalling deepening investor and owner-occupier interest in this inner-ring corridor.

Day-to-day life in Windsor Park revolves around the proximity of Calgary's largest regional mall, Chinook Centre, located under one kilometre southeast at Macleod Trail and Glenmore Trail — a draw that puts fashion, grocery, dining, and service retail within a five-minute walk or drive. The Calgary Golf & Country Club borders the western edge, and the Elbow River pathway network connects directly to the broader city pathway grid, making the community a natural fit for cyclists and runners. Rockyview General Hospital sits roughly 2 km south, and downtown Calgary is approximately 5 km north via Macleod Trail or the Elbow Drive/4 Street corridor, positioning Windsor Park at the centre of Calgary's south-central employment arc that stretches from Stampede Park through Mission.

The housing stock reflects a prolonged buildout from the 1940s through the 1990s — the City of Calgary records an average construction year of 1984 across the neighbourhood's 1,868 properties. The mix spans post-war bungalows, infill duplexes, low-rise apartment buildings clustered near 57 Avenue SW and Elbow Drive, and newer multi-family projects such as The Windsor at 5112 Elbow Drive SW, which targets the premium segment at $1,900–$2,673 for one- and two-bedroom suites. Rental inventory is deep relative to the neighbourhood's size: current market listings show apartment-type units averaging approximately $2,009/month, townhouses at $2,527, and basement suites from $1,000–$1,650 — a range that enables STR operators to calibrate product to both mid-market transient guests and longer executive-stay tenants benefiting from the Chinook Centre and Stampede Park proximity.

The Calgary Golf & Country Club borders the western edge, and the Elbow River pathway network connects directly to the broader city pathway grid, making the community a natural fit for cyclists and runners. renter_intel.title: Strong 20–39 Renter Cohort
renter_intel.description: 38 percent of Windsor Park's 4,410 residents are aged 20–39, a working-age concentration that sustains consistent demand for well-located one- and two-bedroom rentals near Chinook Centre and the Elbow Drive corridor.

renter_intel.title: Layered Rental Price Bands
renter_intel.description: Active inventory spans from $1,000 basement suites to $3,249 premium apartments, giving STR and long-term rental operators the flexibility to target multiple guest-income tiers in the same neighbourhood.

renter_intel.title: Infill Pipeline Supports Mid-Density STR Product
renter_intel.description: 47 new-construction permits and 20 demolition permits issued since January 2024 confirm active lot-by-lot renewal, with new infill duplexes and townhouses increasingly viable as legal STR or furnished-rental units.

whats_nearby.items: Calgary's largest enclosed mall, 0.96 km southeast at Macleod Trail and Glenmore Trail, provides retail, dining, grocery, and transit connections including the adjacent Chinook CTrain station on the Red Line.
A full-service regional hospital approximately 2.2 km south, generating sustained demand from healthcare staff and travelling medical professionals for furnished short- and mid-term rentals.

whats_nearby.category: Elbow River Pathway & Calgary Golf & Country Club
whats_nearby.items: The western edge of Windsor Park borders the private Calgary Golf & Country Club grounds and connects to the Elbow River multi-use pathway, providing walkers, cyclists, and off-season skiers direct access to the riverine green corridor.

windsor-park.peak_season: July (Stampede proximity and proximity to Chinook Centre). Strong through September.
windsor-park.best_property_type: Character bungalow with legal suite near Chinook Centre.
windsor-park.guest_profile: Festival-goers, shopping destination visitors, budget-conscious inner-city guests.
windsor-park.stand_out: Character bungalows with legal suites near Chinook Centre.
windsor-park.distinctive_fact: Windsor Park sits adjacent to Chinook Centre — Calgary's largest mall — and within a short drive of the Stampede grounds. Listings that market the shopping and dining access see stronger bookings than those that don't.

varsity.name: Varsity
varsity.subtitle: Northwest Calgary's Academic Anchor — Where UCalgary Demand Meets Established Residential
varsity.str_intro:
Varsity occupies a plateau in Calgary's northwest quadrant, bounded by Crowchild Trail to the north and east, the Bow River valley to the west, and 32nd Avenue NW to the south — placing it immediately adjacent to the University of Calgary campus and the University District mixed-use development. The community was built out in three phases from the 1960s onward, evolving into the sub-areas of Varsity Acres, Varsity Village, and Varsity Estates, with a combined 2021 population of approximately 12,035. Forty-three percent of residents are aged 20–39, a figure directly reflective of the student, researcher, and early-career professional population drawn by the University of Calgary and neighbouring institutions. With 38 percent of housing used for renting and 39 percent of buildings classified as condominiums or apartments, Varsity sits at the upper tier of rental-intensity among Calgary's established northwest communities.

Market Mall anchors retail life in Varsity Village, a 200-plus-store regional centre with major grocery, fashion, and service tenants including a Saks Fifth Avenue Off 5th; it is within walking distance for Varsity Village residents and a short drive from Varsity Acres and Varsity Estates. The University of Calgary campus immediately south offers cultural programming, sporting events, and alumni-economy activity through the MacEwan Student Centre and arts venues. Bowmont Natural Park and the Bow River pathway system trace the community's western edge, providing river valley access for cyclists, trail runners, and cross-country skiers. The University District development — adjacent to Varsity's southern boundary — has added a walkable main street of cafés, specialty retail, a Save-On-Foods anchor, and new mid-to-premium rental buildings, expanding the effective amenity corridor without requiring a car.

Varsity Estates commands the community's premium price tier: large single-detached homes on wide lots, many backing onto Bowmont Park or with Bow River valley outlooks. Varsity Village concentrates the community's multi-family rental inventory, anchored by Market Mall and served by the 40th Avenue NW and Varsity Drive CTrain corridor (69 Street Station, West LRT line). One-bedroom rents in 2026 run approximately $1,500–$1,900/month and two-bedroom units $2,000–$2,400, consistent with adjacent northwest communities competing for UCalgary faculty, medical staff, and hospital-adjacent tenants at Foothills Medical Centre roughly 4–5 km south. The Radburn-influenced internal pathway network of Varsity Village creates a pedestrian-friendly street pattern that differentiates the product and supports above-average retention among professional renters.

The University of Calgary campus immediately south offers cultural programming, sporting events, and alumni-economy activity through the MacEwan Student Centre and arts venues. Bowmont Natural Park and the Bow River pathway system trace the community's western edge, providing river valley access for cyclists, trail runners, and cross-country skiers. The University District development — adjacent to Varsity's southern boundary — has added a walkable main street of cafés, specialty retail, a Save-On-Foods anchor, and new mid-to-premium rental buildings, expanding the effective amenity corridor without requiring a car.

Varsity Village concentrates the community's multi-family rental inventory, anchored by Market Mall and served by the 40th Avenue NW and Varsity Drive CTrain corridor (69 Street Station, West LRT line). One-bedroom rents in 2026 run approximately $1,500–$1,900/month and two-bedroom units $2,000–$2,400, consistent with adjacent northwest communities competing for UCalgary faculty, medical staff, and hospital-adjacent tenants at Foothills Medical Centre roughly 4–5 km south. The Radburn-influenced internal pathway network of Varsity Village creates a pedestrian-friendly street pattern that differentiates the product and supports above-average retention among professional renters.

renter_intel.title: High Rental-Tenure Rate for an Established NW Community
renter_intel.description: 38 percent of Varsity's housing is renter-occupied — well above Calgary norms for a mature single-family neighbourhood — driven by UCalgary proximity and the concentration of apartments and condos in Varsity Village.

renter_intel.title: CTrain Direct to Downtown and Hospital Corridor
renter_intel.description: The 69 Street CTrain station (West LRT line) provides car-free access to downtown, SAIT, and connections toward Foothills Hospital, making Varsity viable for professional and student renters without vehicles.

renter_intel.title: University District Activates the Southern Edge
renter_intel.description: The adjacent University District development adds new rental supply, walkable retail, and a Cineplex VIP Cinema, raising the lifestyle premium of Varsity-adjacent units and supporting STR demand during UCalgary convocations, conferences, and McMahon Stadium events.

whats_nearby.items: A 200-plus-retailer regional mall in Varsity Village offering full grocery, fashion, restaurants, and services, within walking distance of Varsity Village apartments and a five-minute drive from Varsity Estates.

Immediately adjacent to Varsity's southern boundary, UCalgary generates year-round academic and event traffic — a consistent driver of both long-term furnished-rental demand and peak STR bookings around convocation and Stampede.

A large natural ravine park along the Bow River bordering Varsity's western edge, offering river valley trails and off-leash dog areas that are a meaningful lifestyle differentiator in tenant marketing.

varsity.peak_season: June–September (University of Calgary proximity). Football season (September–November) secondary.
varsity.best_property_type: Character home with legal suite near the University of Calgary.
varsity.guest_profile: University visitors, medical travellers, families visiting students, sports event attendees.
varsity.stand_out: Character homes with legal suites within a 10-minute walk of the University of Calgary campus.
varsity.distinctive_fact: Varsity is the established residential neighbourhood immediately north of the University of Calgary — university-related travel (visiting academics, families of students, conference attendees) provides a steady demand base that is largely insensitive to seasonal tourism patterns.

shaganappi.name: Shaganappi
shaganappi.subtitle: 17 Ave SW at the LRT — Shaganappi's Inner-City Infill Case
shaganappi.str_intro:
Shaganappi occupies a compact inner-city site bounded by the Bow River to the north, Crowchild Trail to the east, 17th Avenue SW to the south, and 33rd Street SW to the west — a location that places it between downtown Calgary and the Westbrook commercial node in roughly 10 minutes by road or transit. The community association, established in 1955, covers Shaganappi, Sunalta West, and Upper Scarboro. The Shaganappi LRT station, opened in 2013 on the West LRT extension, sits adjacent to the Shaganappi Point Golf Course at the community's eastern edge, providing direct CTrain service to downtown's 7th Avenue corridor. The 17th Avenue SW Main Street designation along the southern boundary drives ongoing City investment in streetscaping, wider sidewalks, and infrastructure upgrades that are reshaping the commercial ground-floor environment.

Shaganappi's street life centres on 17th Avenue SW, which connects westward to Westbrook Mall — a Safeway-anchored neighbourhood centre at 33rd Street and 17th Avenue SW — and eastward into the restaurant and retail corridor of Lower Mount Royal and Mission. The Shaganappi Point Golf Course, operated by the City of Calgary at 1200 26 St SW, doubles as a cross-country ski area in winter, offering a genuine four-season outdoor amenity within the community boundary. Shaganappi Park off Bow Trail provides tennis courts, multiple skating rinks, a sledding hill, and the community hall, while Oliver Quarry Park west of Crowchild Trail adds off-leash area and additional topographic character referencing the sandstone quarry that once operated on the site. Bike pathways from the community lead directly to the Bow River system and downtown.

Shaganappi's housing stock is a textbook inner-city transition market: a mix of pre-war and post-war bungalows alongside active infill and multi-family intensification driven by the West LRT line. The Westbrook LRT station at 14 Avenue and 33 Street SW is the focal point of new transit-oriented development, including a recently approved six-storey, 127-unit Truman Homes rental project with studio, one-, and two-bedroom units — illustrative of the scale and tenure of incoming supply. The new Bow33 building just north of 17th Avenue advertises walkability scores of 88, with grocery, pharmacy, transit, and dining within a 10-minute walk. As an inner-city precinct with LRT access, proximity to the 17th Avenue dining corridor, and ongoing City Main Street investment, Shaganappi offers a well-supported STR and furnished-rental thesis anchored by commuter and lifestyle demand.

renter_intel.title: Heads Up — parking varies wildly
renter_intel.description: Some older walk-ups only have 3–4 stalls for 20+ units. Street parking is currently unrestricted but verify with the city before assuming.

renter_intel.title: Transit Tip
renter_intel.description: Shaganappi Point station is on the Blue Line (202 — Saddletowne to 69 Street). Westbrook station is a 10-min walk west and gives you a backup. Route 2 bus runs along 17th Ave for east-west trips.

renter_intel.title: Local Insight
renter_intel.description: Shaganappi Point Golf Course is a public 9-hole — anyone can play it. In winter the fairways become free toboggan and cross-country ski terrain.

renter_intel.title: Pet Resume Tip
renter_intel.description: The off-leash area at Shaganappi Park (near the community hall on 14th Ave SW) is small but genuinely used. Larger dog runs are a short drive at Edworthy or River Park.

whats_nearby.items: A Safeway-anchored neighbourhood shopping centre at 33rd Street and 17th Avenue SW, directly across from the Westbrook LRT station, providing everyday grocery, pharmacy, and service retail walkable from most Shaganappi addresses.

A City-operated 18-hole municipal course at 1200 26 St SW, with a par-68 championship layout and winter cross-country skiing, serving as a green buffer and seasonal amenity anchoring the community's eastern edge.

The 17th Avenue SW strip extending east into Lower Mount Royal concentrates independent restaurants, wine bars, cafés, and boutiques — including Cassis Bistro and The Himalayan restaurant — within less than one kilometre of Shaganappi's southern boundary.

shaganappi.peak_season: July (Stampede proximity and 17th Ave access). Strong through September.
shaganappi.best_property_type: Character bungalow with legal suite near 17th Ave SW.
shaganappi.guest_profile: Festival-goers, budget-conscious inner-city visitors, 17th Ave diners.
shaganappi.stand_out: Character bungalows with legal suites within a 10-minute walk of 17th Ave SW.
shaganappi.distinctive_fact: Shaganappi is one of Calgary's most underrated inner-city communities — its high density of character bungalows with legal suites and proximity to 17th Ave SW make it a strong value play for STR operators.
shaganappi.ltr_description:
Shaganappi is the small inner-city SW community sandwiched between 17th Avenue and the Bow River escarpment, with its own C-Train station — Shaganappi Point — sitting right inside it. That's the whole pitch: you get a 12-minute train ride to downtown, a 9-hole municipal golf course as your back yard, and rents that come in noticeably under Sunalta or Killarney. The catch is that Shaganappi isn't a "destination" neighbourhood the way Marda Loop or Inglewood is, so it lives in the shadow of louder neighbours. For renters who care about transit, value, and quiet streets more than restaurant rows, this is one of the best-kept secrets in the inner city.

Walking Shaganappi feels like an older, less-renovated version of Sunalta. The housing stock is a mix of 1940s–'50s bungalows on tree-lined streets, scattered infill duplexes, and a band of low-rise apartments concentrated near 17th Avenue. Shaganappi Park sits in the middle — community hall, tennis courts, basketball court, off-leash dog area — and the Shaganappi Point Golf Course occupies the entire western escarpment overlooking the Bow River. The 17th Avenue strip on the south edge feels like the working version of 17th in the Beltline: less polished, more genuinely local, with spots like Donegal Irish Pub and Sauce Italian Kitchen anchoring things. Westbrook Mall and Westbrook C-Train station are a 10-minute walk west, giving you Walmart, Safeway, and proper retail without leaving transit range.

Most rental supply here is in the apartment buildings clustered along 17th Avenue and 26th Street SW — older walk-ups from the '60s and '70s, mostly 1-bed and 2-bed units with rents that beat almost any other transit-adjacent inner-city option. You'll also find basement suites in the bungalow stock farther into the neighbourhood. Parking is usually surface (the older buildings often only have a handful of stalls, so confirm before signing — street parking is currently unrestricted but that can change). Many of the older walk-ups have shared coin laundry rather than in-suite. The neighbourhood is in slow infill mode, so you'll occasionally find a brand-new duplex or skinny home for rent at a higher price point. The C-Train station inside the neighbourhood is the headline feature — but the train runs above ground here, so units close to the line get some noise.

ltr_vibe_tags.title: C-Train In Your Pocket
ltr_vibe_tags.description: Shaganappi Point station is inside the community, 12 minutes to downtown

university-heights.name: University Heights
university-heights.subtitle: Hospital Row, University Gate — Calgary's Most Institutionally Driven Rental Pocket
university-heights.str_intro:
University Heights is one of Calgary's smallest yet most institutionally concentrated neighbourhoods, covering 0.7 km² in the northwest quadrant and bordered on four sides by major generators: the University of Calgary to the north and east, Foothills Medical Centre and the Alberta Children's Hospital to the southeast, 16 Avenue NW (Trans-Canada Highway) to the south, and Shaganappi Trail NW to the west. The community was established in 1963 and recorded a 2021 Census population of 2,965, with 43 percent of residents aged 20–39 — the highest young-adult concentration of the five communities in this batch. Average assessed property value sits at $1,859,578 — 142 percent above the Calgary-wide average — reflecting the dominance of large estate lots dating from the 1970s, many now in active redevelopment.

Daily life in University Heights is quietly utilitarian by design: proximity to UCalgary's facilities, the Foothills Medical Centre, and the Alberta Children's Hospital means residents typically walk or cycle to work, study, or clinical placement rather than commuting across the city. McMahon Stadium and the Foothills Athletic Park adjoin the neighbourhood's eastern boundary and generate seasonal event traffic — CFL games, track meets, and university competitions — that spills into local demand for short-term accommodation. Market Mall is accessible within a 10-minute drive north on Shaganappi Trail, and the University District mixed-use development along 32nd Avenue NW offers walkable coffee, grocery (Save-On-Foods), and dining without needing a car. Westmount Charter School and University Elementary serve the community's school-age population within the neighbourhood boundary.

University Heights' housing profile is bifurcated: at the ownership end, large estate bungalows and two-storey homes on generous lots reflect a 1970s mid-century build with recent infill and renovation pressure (102 building permits issued since January 2024, including 11 new construction). At the rental end, 66.5 percent of dwelling units are renter-occupied and 64.6 percent of buildings are condominiums or apartments — figures that are extraordinary for a nominally low-density residential community and are a direct function of institutional tenancy. One-bedroom apartments ran approximately $1,639/month and two-bedroom units $1,779 in mid-2025, with houses averaging $2,895. For STR operators, the combination of walking-distance hospital access, UCalgary visiting researcher and conference demand, and McMahon Stadium event peaks creates a multi-vector occupancy case year-round.

renter_intel.title: 66 Percent Renter-Occupied — Calgary's Highest Rate Among Single-Family-Zoned NW Communities
renter_intel.description: More than two-thirds of University Heights dwellings are renter-occupied, a structural condition driven by hospital and university tenancy rather than vacancy, making the rental market here exceptionally deep relative to the community's small footprint.

renter_intel.title: Medical Professional Demand Is Perennial
renter_intel.description: Walking proximity to Foothills Medical Centre, the Alberta Children's Hospital, and UCalgary's medical and health sciences faculties generates year-round furnished-rental demand from physicians, residents, fellows, and visiting specialists.

renter_intel.title: McMahon Stadium Event Demand
renter_intel.description: McMahon Stadium, adjacent to the community's eastern edge, hosts CFL regular-season and playoff games, university athletics, and major concerts, creating short-burst STR demand spikes during the May–November season.

whats_nearby.category: Foothills Medical Centre & Alberta Children's Hospital
whats_nearby.items: Calgary's largest adult acute-care hospital and the province's primary pediatric hospital are immediately adjacent to University Heights, constituting the single largest driver of rental demand in the community.

UCalgary's main campus borders the community to the north and east, with faculties of medicine, law, engineering, and arts generating student, faculty, and visiting researcher rental demand across all unit types.

The mixed-use University District development on UCalgary's endowment lands offers a walkable main street with grocery, cafés, a Cineplex VIP Cinema, and fitness studios — expanding the amenity range without requiring residents to leave the immediate area.

university-heights.peak_season: June–September (University of Calgary proximity, Foothills Hospital). Football season secondary.
university-heights.best_property_type: Character home with legal suite near the University of Calgary or Foothills Hospital.
university-heights.guest_profile: University visitors, medical travellers, families visiting students.
university-heights.stand_out: Character homes with legal suites within walking distance of the University of Calgary or Foothills Hospital.
university-heights.distinctive_fact: University Heights sits between the University of Calgary and Foothills Hospital — two of Calgary's largest demand generators for non-leisure travel. This dual-driver position makes it more resilient than single-driver university neighbourhoods.

christie-park.name: Christie Park
christie-park.subtitle: West Calgary Estate Living with LRT — Christie Park's Rocky Mountain Front Row
christie-park.str_intro:
Christie Park is one of Calgary's late-wave estate communities, annexed to the city in 1956 but not developed residentially until 1990, meaning the neighbourhood's 728-home inventory was purpose-built for the owner-occupier market with large lots, executive-scale homes, and boulevard landscaping. The community occupies a plateau at 1,210 m elevation in Calgary's southwest quadrant, bounded by Strathcona Drive to the north, Sarcee Trail to the east, 17 Avenue SW to the south, and 69 Street W to the west. Homes on the western and southern perimeter capture unobstructed Rocky Mountain views — a consistent listing differentiator that translates directly to STR and furnished-rental premium pricing. Average household income was $102,664 at the time of the 2001 Census and has grown substantially since, consistent with the estate character of the housing stock.

Christie Park is served by the Sirocco CTrain station on the West LRT Blue Line, located on 17 Avenue SW near 69 Street, enabling downtown commutes of roughly 30 minutes without a vehicle — an unusual transit-accessibility credential for an estate neighbourhood on Calgary's western fringe. Westhills Towne Centre, approximately 1.5 km south via 17 Avenue SW, is a large-format power centre anchored by Real Canadian Superstore, Winners, and Indigo, meeting most household retail needs. Aspen Landing Shopping Centre and West Market Square (anchored by Sunterra Market) provide upscale grocery and dining options within five minutes by car. The Westside Recreation Centre, minutes north via Sarcee Trail, provides a pool, twin ice arenas, and fitness programming that the SCA Community Association supplements with tennis courts, an outdoor skating pond, soccer fields, and a hockey rink within Christie Park itself.

Christie Park's housing is almost entirely detached single-family: approximately 94.5 percent of buildings are not condominiums or apartments, and historically only 4.8 percent of units have been renter-occupied — the lowest rental-tenure rate of the five communities in this batch. This makes Christie Park a premium STR rather than a long-term rental play: the quality of individual homes (executive walkouts with triple garages, mountain-view primary suites, finished basements) positions the neighbourhood for high-ADR furnished whole-home STR use, corporate relocations, and extended-stay demand from Foothills Medical Centre or Canada Olympic Park affiliates. Active MLS listings in 2025 showed detached home prices ranging from roughly $929,000 to $1,349,900 for estate properties, consistent with the neighbourhood's upscale positioning. Ernest Manning High School, Olympic Heights Elementary, and the SCA Community Association's child, youth, and adult programming underpin the family-lifestyle narrative.

renter_intel.title: Low Supply, High ADR — Estate STR Position
renter_intel.description: Fewer than 5 percent of Christie Park homes are rental-tenured, creating a tight inventory environment where well-appointed furnished whole-home STR listings command materially above-city-median ADRs driven by mountain views, home size, and transit access.

renter_intel.title: Sirocco CTrain Enables Corporate Relo and Medical Staff Demand
renter_intel.description: The Sirocco station on the West LRT Blue Line places downtown Calgary within a direct 30-minute ride, making Christie Park viable for corporate relocation assignments and Foothills Medical Centre staff who require a vehicle-free or reduced-vehicle commute.

renter_intel.title: Canada Olympic Park Proximity for Event and Seasonal Demand
renter_intel.description: WinSport Canada Olympic Park, approximately 5 km northwest via Trans-Canada Highway, generates winter ski-season and summer events traffic that supplements STR demand during shoulder and off-peak months.

whats_nearby.category: Westhills Towne Centre & Signal Hill Centre
whats_nearby.items: A large-format power centre complex on 17 Avenue SW south of Christie Park offering Real Canadian Superstore, Winners, Indigo, and multiple restaurant chains, providing comprehensive daily-needs retail without leaving the immediate west-Calgary corridor.

A City of Calgary recreation facility minutes north via Sarcee Trail, featuring a full aquatic centre, twin NHL-sized ice sheets, fitness centre, and programming for all ages — a primary amenity argument in tenant and guest marketing.

whats_nearby.category: Sirocco CTrain Station (West LRT Blue Line)
whats_nearby.items: Located at 17 Avenue SW near 69 Street, the Sirocco station provides direct LRT service to downtown Calgary, making this one of the few estate-calibre west Calgary neighbourhoods where genuine daily car-free commuting is practical.

christie-park.peak_season: June–September (mountain gateway, elevated position). Winter secondary for ski season.
christie-park.best_property_type: Detached home with mountain views and legal suite.
christie-park.guest_profile: Mountain-bound travellers, families visiting west Calgary.
christie-park.stand_out: Homes with mountain views and legal suites near the Westhills commercial area.
christie-park.distinctive_fact: Christie Park offers mountain views and mountain gateway access at a lower price point than Aspen Woods or Springbank Hill. Its proximity to the Westhills commercial area adds convenience for guests.

garrison-green.name: Garrison Green
garrison-green.subtitle: Planned Community Character Near Marda Loop at a Lower Entry Price
garrison-green.str_intro:
Garrison Green is a 32-hectare master-planned community in southwest Calgary, built on the former Canadian Forces Base Lincoln Park site and developed by Canada Lands Company on New Urbanist principles. The neighbourhood is bounded by Richardson Road SW to the west, Glenmore Trail to the south, and Crowchild Trail to the east — positioning it minutes from both Marda Loop and Mount Royal University. Peacekeeper's Park at its heart bears a wall of honour for Canadian peacekeepers lost on UN assignments, giving the community a distinctive civic identity few comparable suburbs can match. With an average year of construction around 2006, the housing stock is newer than most inner-city SW communities while remaining accessible in price — assessed values averaged $521,789 in 2024, roughly 32% below the city-wide benchmark.

Daily life in Garrison Green revolves around the Marda Loop commercial district on 33rd and 34th Avenues SW, a 10-minute walk that delivers independent restaurants, wine bars, specialty grocery, and boutique retail. The community's low-density street grid and mature-tree retention create a walkable, family-oriented feel, while the Elbow River pathway system and North Glenmore Park are reachable by bike within 15 minutes. Residents heading to Mount Royal University can do so entirely on foot, making this an unusually car-light lifestyle for a neighbourhood this far from downtown. Transit options along Crowchild Trail connect to the broader CTrain network, and Westhills Towne Centre is a short drive west for big-box retail.

The housing mix spans newer townhomes, semi-detached infills, and detached homes, with many properties featuring private yards — a rarity at this price point in SW Calgary. Investors targeting STR buyers should focus on infill townhomes and detached homes near the Marda Loop perimeter, where walkability scores are highest and the guest draw is strongest. The community's low disorder rate (10.1 events per 1,000 residents vs. a city average of 49.6) is a credible selling point for cautious owners. Separate-entry configurations are mixed rather than universal, so due diligence on individual properties is essential before listing.

renter_intel.title: Marda Loop Walkability Drives Premium Rates
renter_intel.description: Garrison Green's proximity to Marda Loop — Calgary's most vibrant inner-city commercial strip — is the primary STR differentiator. Guests value walkable access to independent dining and nightlife on 33rd and 34th Avenues SW, and listings that emphasise this connection in titles and descriptions outperform those that market the neighbourhood in isolation.

renter_intel.title: Mount Royal University Creates a Reliable Secondary Demand Stream
renter_intel.description: Mount Royal University is within walking distance, generating consistent bookings from visiting faculty, prospective students, and families attending convocation or campus events. This demand layer complements summer leisure travel and extends the shoulder seasons in a way that purely residential SW communities cannot match.

renter_intel.title: Newer Stock Means Lower Maintenance Risk
renter_intel.description: With an average construction year of 2006, Garrison Green properties carry less deferred-maintenance risk than the pre-war bungalows common in neighbouring Garrison Woods and Altadore. Newer mechanicals, better insulation, and contemporary layouts translate to fewer mid-stay issues and stronger guest reviews — a compounding advantage over time.

whats_nearby.items: The Marda Loop Business Improvement Area on 33rd and 34th Avenues SW offers some of Calgary's best independent dining, weekend farmers' markets, and walkable retail within 10 minutes of most Garrison Green addresses. It is the community's primary lifestyle anchor and a key line item in any effective STR listing description.

Garrison Green's central park features the Wall of Honour commemorating Canadian peacekeepers, mature trees retained from the original CFB site, and open lawn space that hosts the annual Peacekeepers' Day ceremony each August. The park is a quiet, photogenic amenity that photographs well for STR listings.

whats_nearby.category: North Glenmore Park and Elbow River Pathways
whats_nearby.items: North Glenmore Park and the continuous Elbow River multi-use pathway are reachable by bicycle from Garrison Green, offering riverside cycling, picnic areas, and off-leash dog access. Guests seeking outdoor recreation find these amenities a meaningful draw, particularly in summer.

garrison-green.peak_season: June–September (Marda Loop proximity, Mount Royal University).
garrison-green.best_property_type: Newer infill or townhome near Marda Loop.
garrison-green.guest_profile: Boutique travellers, Marda Loop visitors, Mount Royal University visitors.
garrison-green.stand_out: Newer infill homes near Marda Loop with private yards.
garrison-green.distinctive_fact: Garrison Green is the newer, more affordable sister community to Garrison Woods — similar planned community character and Marda Loop proximity at a lower entry price point.

mount-pleasant.name: Mount Pleasant
mount-pleasant.subtitle: Character Bungalows, Legal Suites, and Two Major Demand Generators
mount-pleasant.str_intro:
Mount Pleasant is one of Calgary's most character-rich north-central communities, with a housing stock dating predominantly from the 1940s and 1950s and a street network of mature, canopied residential blocks. The community is bounded by 16th Avenue NW (Trans-Canada Highway) to the south, Crowchild Trail to the west, and sits just east of the Confederation Park corridor — placing it within easy reach of SAIT Polytechnic and McMahon Stadium. Its high density of pre-war bungalows with legal basement suites gives STR operators a meaningful inventory advantage over newer suburban communities where suite penetration is lower. Centre Street NW bisects the neighbourhood, providing a direct transit corridor to downtown roughly 4 km south.

Mount Pleasant residents benefit from a walkable inner-city environment that has retained its working-class character while attracting a new wave of renovation investment and infill development. The community association maintains a Sportsplex with an indoor ice rink and outdoor pool at 602 — 21 Avenue NW, anchoring neighbourhood life through the colder months. Foothills Medical Centre and the Alberta Children's Hospital are a short drive west on Crowchild Trail, generating a steady stream of medical visitors and families seeking accommodation close to a major healthcare campus. Confederation Park's 56 hectares of greenspace is a 10-minute walk, offering toboggan hills, picnic areas, and multi-use pathways that extend year-round recreation options.

The STR opportunity in Mount Pleasant centres on character bungalows that have been updated to include legal basement suites with separate entries — a configuration that allows owner-occupancy of the upper unit while the lower unit runs as a licensed STR. Properties within a five-minute walk of SAIT at 1301 — 16 Avenue NW command the strongest guest interest from students, visiting faculty, and families of SAIT enrollees. McMahon Stadium at 1817 Crowchild Trail NW generates discrete event-driven bookings for CFL games and major concerts, creating predictable high-occupancy weekends throughout the summer and fall. Investors should budget for cosmetic renovation on most pre-1960 stock, but structural quality in the neighbourhood is generally solid.

The community association maintains a Sportsplex with an indoor ice rink and outdoor pool at 602 — 21 Avenue NW, anchoring neighbourhood life through the colder months. Foothills Medical Centre and the Alberta Children's Hospital are a short drive west on Crowchild Trail, generating a steady stream of medical visitors and families seeking accommodation close to a major healthcare campus. Confederation Park's 56 hectares of greenspace is a 10-minute walk, offering toboggan hills, picnic areas, and multi-use pathways that extend year-round recreation options.

renter_intel.title: SAIT Polytechnic Generates Year-Round Institutional Demand
renter_intel.description: SAIT at 1301 — 16 Avenue NW is one of Canada's largest polytechnics, with over 45,000 full- and part-time students annually. Visiting families, prospective students on campus tours, and short-term contractor training programs generate consistent weeknight and weekend bookings that are largely insensitive to tourism seasonality.

renter_intel.title: McMahon Stadium Event Spikes are Predictable and Bookable Months in Advance
renter_intel.description: The Calgary Stampeders play their home schedule at McMahon Stadium, 1817 Crowchild Trail NW, roughly 2 km from most Mount Pleasant addresses. Home games sell out regularly and generate sharp occupancy spikes on event nights. Operators who keep calendars open for game weekends and price accordingly capture a reliable premium over baseline rates.

renter_intel.title: Foothills and Alberta Children's Hospital Drive Medical Bookings
renter_intel.description: Foothills Medical Centre is one of western Canada's largest hospitals and the Alberta Children's Hospital is the province's premier paediatric facility. Families travelling for extended medical treatment or specialist appointments represent an undermarketed guest segment in Mount Pleasant — direct outreach to hospital social-work departments and online cancer/cardiac patient communities can build a loyal mid-week demand base.

whats_nearby.items: SAIT at 1301 — 16 Avenue NW is the neighbourhood's single most important demand generator, with 45,000+ enrolments annually. Proximity to the main campus is the lead selling point for STR listings targeting student-family and training-program guests.

Confederation Park, one of Calgary's largest municipal parks at 56 hectares, is within walking distance of most Mount Pleasant addresses. The park's toboggan hill, picnic areas, and multi-use pathway system make it a year-round amenity and a reliable guest draw for families travelling with children.

McMahon Stadium at 1817 Crowchild Trail NW hosts Calgary Stampeders home games and major concerts from May through November. Its presence within the neighbourhood creates predictable high-demand booking windows that operators can price into their dynamic rate strategy well in advance of the season.

mount-pleasant.peak_season: June–September (proximity to SAIT, McMahon Stadium, and the University of Calgary).
mount-pleasant.best_property_type: Character bungalow with legal suite near SAIT or McMahon Stadium.
mount-pleasant.guest_profile: SAIT students and visitors, sports event attendees, medical travellers.
mount-pleasant.stand_out: Character bungalows with legal suites within walking distance of SAIT or McMahon Stadium.
mount-pleasant.distinctive_fact: Mount Pleasant is one of Calgary's most character-rich north-central communities — its high density of pre-1960 bungalows with legal suites and proximity to SAIT and McMahon Stadium create a strong STR niche.

tuxedo-park.name: Tuxedo Park
tuxedo-park.subtitle: Pre-War Character, High Suite Density, and Inner-City Access
tuxedo-park.str_intro:
Tuxedo Park is one of Calgary's oldest residential communities, developed during the pre-World War I land boom and today recognised for its high concentration of pre-1960 bungalows with updated legal basement suites. The neighbourhood sits in the inner north quadrant, bounded by 32 Avenue NW to the north, Edmonton Trail to the east, 16 Avenue NW (Trans-Canada Highway) to the south, and 2 Street NW to the west — with Centre Street running through the community's spine as the primary downtown corridor. At roughly 3 km from the city centre, Tuxedo Park occupies a rare position: genuine inner-city character at prices that remain accessible relative to comparable communities south of the river. The Green Line LRT, when built, is expected to run along Centre Street, which would materially improve transit connectivity for the neighbourhood.

Tuxedo Park's mature tree-lined streets and century-old lot pattern create a walkable, human-scale environment that has become increasingly sought-after as inner-city demand intensifies. Centre Street and Edmonton Trail both offer accessible retail corridors with independent restaurants, cafés, and convenience services, while larger shopping destinations along 16 Avenue NW are a short drive. The community is well-positioned for guests who want a quieter residential experience while remaining close to major attractions — McMahon Stadium is approximately 3 km west and SAIT Polytechnic is similarly accessible. The active community association, incorporated in 1949, maintains community events and neighbourhood programming that contribute to the stable social character guests notice in reviews.

The STR case in Tuxedo Park mirrors that of Mount Pleasant: character bungalows with high-ceilinged main floors and legal basement suites with independent entries. The neighbourhood's high legal-suite density is confirmed by multiple real estate sources, making property selection relatively straightforward compared to communities where suite penetration is low. Investors should target properties on the southern half of the community, closer to 16 Avenue NW, for maximum proximity to SAIT and transit. Infill semi-detached homes represent a growing share of new inventory and often offer legal suites in purpose-built configurations that exceed older retrofits in guest comfort.
