Properties without active lake access should not be marketed on the lake angle. Separate entry suite stock is rated 'Rare', meaning whole-home detached is the dominant format. Summer demand peaks strongly from June through August; year-round secondary demand comes from airport proximity. The November–March slow season is the softest window. Verify that the specific property qualifies for lake access through the Residents Association before listing lake access as a feature.

renter_intel.title: Private 16-Acre Lake — Swimming, Fishing, Boating, Ice Skating
renter_intel.description: Coral Springs' private lake is stocked with rainbow trout from an onsite hatchery and offers a full recreational spectrum: swimming from the private beach, fishing, recreational boating, beach volleyball, and in winter, outdoor ice skating. Lake access is limited to community residents and their verified guests through the Residents Association. Properties qualifying for lake access see substantially stronger summer booking rates compared to non-lake Coral Springs addresses.

renter_intel.title: Don Hartman Northeast Sportsplex — In the Community
renter_intel.description: The Don Hartman Northeast Sportsplex, located within Coral Springs, is a major indoor sports facility with two NHL-sized hockey arenas with spectator seating, a full-size gymnasium for basketball and volleyball, and a fitness training room. This facility drives additional demand from sports teams and visiting athletes attending tournaments, particularly during the October–April hockey season.

renter_intel.title: Calgary International Airport — 15–20 min
renter_intel.description: Calgary International Airport is accessible from Coral Springs via McKnight Boulevard and Barlow Trail in approximately 15–20 minutes, with Stoney Trail providing an alternative ring-road route. This airport proximity creates a secondary, year-round demand base from airline crews, long-connection travellers, and visiting families who prefer a residential lake community over an airport-area hotel.

whats_nearby.category: Coral Springs Private Lake — In the Community
whats_nearby.items: The 16-acre private lake at the centre of Coral Springs provides swimming, fishing, beach volleyball, boating, and winter ice skating for resident members. The Coral Springs Residents Association manages lake access and hosts annual community events at the lakeside, including Winterfest, the corn roast, and the Halloween party.

The Don Hartman Northeast Sportsplex within Coral Springs provides two NHL-sized arenas with spectator seating, a gymnasium, and fitness facilities. The facility hosts regular community recreation programming as well as regional sports tournaments that draw visiting athletes and families from across the province.

whats_nearby.category: East Hills Shopping Centre / Sunridge Mall — 10–15 min
whats_nearby.items: East Hills Shopping Centre (anchored by Costco, Cineplex, and Walmart) on the east side of the city is accessible within 10–15 minutes via McKnight Boulevard. Sunridge Mall provides an additional NE shopping destination with food court and retail options. Both centres meet full provisioning and entertainment needs for extended-stay family groups.

coral-springs.peak_season: June–August (lake access is the primary draw). Year-round secondary from airport proximity.
coral-springs.best_property_type: Detached 3-4 bed with lake access privileges.
coral-springs.guest_profile: Families seeking lake access, airport travellers, multi-generational families.
coral-springs.stand_out: Detached homes with lake access privileges. Properties that market the lake access and airport proximity.
coral-springs.distinctive_fact: Coral Springs is a lake community in NE Calgary — properties with lake access privileges see stronger bookings from families seeking a lake vacation within the city. BNBCalc data for lake-adjacent NE communities shows consistent summer performance.

southwood.name: Southwood
southwood.subtitle: Dual C-Train Access in SW Calgary with Macleod Trail and Chinook Centre at the Door
southwood.str_intro:
Southwood is an established southwest Calgary community founded in 1959, bounded by Macleod Trail to the east, Anderson Road to the south, 14 Street SW to the west, and Southland Drive to the north. The community enjoys a notable transit advantage among south Calgary neighbourhoods: both Southland and Anderson C-Train stations on the Red Line fall within or immediately adjacent to its boundaries, providing downtown Calgary access in under 30 minutes. Elbow Drive bisects the community north to south, connecting Southwood directly to the downtown core without using major arterials. With a population of approximately 6,000, Southwood balances a quiet residential character with exceptional arterial connectivity.

Southwood Community Association (at 11 Sackville Drive SW) operates year-round programming and maintains the Southwood Public Library as a neighbourhood anchor. Residents draw on Southwood's proximity to multiple outdoor amenities: the Glenmore Reservoir pathway system is accessible north via 14 Street, while Fish Creek Provincial Park trails begin just south of Anderson Road. CF Chinook Centre — Calgary's largest mall — is a 5-minute drive north on Macleod Trail, and Southcentre Mall is accessible in similar time to the east. The area has two LRT stations plus multiple bus routes on Macleod Trail and Southland Drive, making it one of the better-served SW communities for car-free transit access.

Southwood's housing stock is primarily 1960s–1970s single-detached homes on Elbow Drive and surrounding residential streets, with the Southland Park commercial complex and Southwood Corner mini-mall providing in-community services along Macleod Trail. Best STR inventory is detached 3-bedroom homes on quieter interior streets rather than on the Macleod Trail corridor. Guest profile includes corporate travellers using the Macleod Trail corridor, families visiting south Calgary, and Chinook Centre shopping destination visitors — a less outdoor-recreation-focused profile than the reservoir-side communities to the west. Year-round transit access from the two LRT stations is a meaningful listing feature.
southwood.str_lifestyle: Southwood Community Association (at 11 Sackville Drive SW) operates year-round programming and maintains the Southwood Public Library as a neighbourhood anchor. Residents draw on Southwood's proximity to multiple outdoor amenities: the Glenmore Reservoir pathway system is accessible north via 14 Street, while Fish Creek Provincial Park trails begin just south of Anderson Road. CF Chinook Centre — Calgary's largest mall — is a 5-minute drive north on Macleod Trail, and Southcentre Mall is accessible in similar time to the east. The area has two LRT stations plus multiple bus routes on Macleod Trail and Southland Drive, making it one of the better-served SW communities for car-free transit access.
southwood.str_housing: Southwood's housing stock is primarily 1960s–1970s single-detached homes on Elbow Drive and surrounding residential streets, with the Southland Park commercial complex and Southwood Corner mini-mall providing in-community services along Macleod Trail. Best STR inventory is detached 3-bedroom homes on quieter interior streets rather than on the Macleod Trail corridor. Guest profile includes corporate travellers using the Macleod Trail corridor, families visiting south Calgary, and Chinook Centre shopping destination visitors — a less outdoor-recreation-focused profile than the reservoir-side communities to the west. Year-round transit access from the two LRT stations is a meaningful listing feature.

renter_intel.title: Southland and Anderson C-Train Stations — In the Community
renter_intel.description: Southwood is one of very few residential communities in south Calgary with two C-Train stations within its boundaries: Southland Station and Anderson Station on the Red Line. Guests can commute downtown in under 30 minutes and reach the Stampede grounds, Chinook Station, and 39 Avenue (Marda Loop adjacent) without a car. This dual transit access is a meaningful differentiator versus neighbouring SW communities.

renter_intel.title: Elbow Drive — Direct Downtown Corridor
renter_intel.description: Elbow Drive, which bisects Southwood from south to north, provides a direct arterial connection to inner-city Calgary without using Macleod Trail or 14 Street, significantly reducing peak-hour travel time to the Stampede grounds, Mission, 4th Street, and downtown. For guests attending multi-day events like Stampede, the Elbow Drive route is a practical advantage over more western SW communities.

renter_intel.title: CF Chinook Centre — 5 min on Macleod Trail
renter_intel.description: CF Chinook Centre at Macleod Trail and Glenmore Trail is Calgary's largest enclosed shopping mall, accessible in approximately 5 minutes north on Macleod Trail from Southwood. The mall's anchor tenants, restaurants, and entertainment options make it a full-day destination for shopping-oriented guests, particularly during inclement weather months when outdoor activities are limited.

whats_nearby.items: CF Chinook Centre at Macleod Trail and Glenmore Trail houses over 240 stores, multiple restaurant chains, a Coles bookstore, and entertainment options. The adjoining Glenmore Landing provides additional retail and the popular Glenmore Sailing School access just east. Together these nodes form the primary retail and dining hub for guests staying in Southwood.

Southcentre Mall on Anderson Road east of the community provides a second full retail destination with department stores, cinemas, and the Avenida Food Hall and Fresh Market artisan food market. This dual-mall proximity within 5 minutes of a single community is unusual in Calgary and adds practical value for extended-stay guests.

The Glenmore Reservoir pathway system is accessible from Southwood via 14 Street SW heading north, connecting to South Glenmore Park, the Glenmore Sailing Club, Heritage Park Historical Village, and the Weaselhead Flats. The full reservoir loop is 16 km. While Southwood's community profile is less reservoir-focused than the immediately adjacent SW communities, the pathway access is a genuine seasonal amenity.

southwood.peak_season: June–August (Macleod Trail access, Chinook Centre proximity). July Stampede secondary.
southwood.guest_profile: Corporate travellers, families visiting south Calgary, shopping destination visitors.
southwood.stand_out: Detached homes near Macleod Trail with easy access to Chinook Centre.
southwood.distinctive_fact: Southwood's Macleod Trail access and Chinook Centre proximity make it a viable option for corporate travellers and shopping destination visitors. BNBCalc data for this south zone shows consistent performance for well-run whole homes.

schools.name: Dr. E. P. Scarlett High School

lakeview.name: Lakeview
lakeview.subtitle: West Calgary Hillside Community Overlooking the Glenmore Reservoir
lakeview.str_intro:
Lakeview is a mature west Calgary community nestled along the north shore of the Glenmore Reservoir, bounded by Glenmore Trail to the north, Crowchild Trail to the east, Glenmore Park (North Glenmore Park) to the south, and 37 Street SW to the west. The community's elevated hillside position provides sweeping views of the Glenmore Reservoir from many properties — the name 'Lakeview' reflects this direct visual relationship with the reservoir rather than a private lake. North Glenmore Park runs along the community's southern boundary, providing immediate access to the 16 km reservoir pathway system, Calgary Canoe Club, Calgary Rowing Club, and Glenmore Sailing School. Downtown Calgary is 15–20 minutes via Crowchild Trail.

Lakeview offers an outstanding balance of urban connectivity and nature access. Crowchild Trail provides quick downtown access, while Glenmore Trail's west-bound connection to Stoney Trail enables direct mountain access. Mount Royal University is minutes away on the east side of Crowchild, bringing an academic energy to the area. Westhills Shopping Centre on the west end of Glenmore Trail provides Lakeview's primary retail hub — supplemented by the small Lakeview Plaza for daily essentials. Marda Loop, Calgary's most vibrant inner-city commercial strip, is accessible in under 10 minutes via Crowchild Trail, offering independent restaurants, boutiques, and the popular Lakeview Farmers' Market.

Lakeview's housing stock is primarily mature 1950s–1970s detached homes, with many properties having undergone significant renovation. The community's elevation and reservoir views make it a premium STR location in the west quadrant. Best inventory is detached 3-bedroom homes with southern exposure toward the reservoir, which provide the most compelling listing photography. Guest profile: outdoor recreation visitors (pathway cycling, reservoir paddling), mountain-bound travellers who prefer an inner-city feel over suburban communities, and Stampede guests seeking a quieter west-end base. Glenmore Sailing School and Calgary Canoe Club access is a genuine differentiated feature.
lakeview.str_lifestyle: Lakeview offers an outstanding balance of urban connectivity and nature access. Crowchild Trail provides quick downtown access, while Glenmore Trail's west-bound connection to Stoney Trail enables direct mountain access. Mount Royal University is minutes away on the east side of Crowchild, bringing an academic energy to the area. Westhills Shopping Centre on the west end of Glenmore Trail provides Lakeview's primary retail hub — supplemented by the small Lakeview Plaza for daily essentials. Marda Loop, Calgary's most vibrant inner-city commercial strip, is accessible in under 10 minutes via Crowchild Trail, offering independent restaurants, boutiques, and the popular Lakeview Farmers' Market.
lakeview.str_housing: Lakeview's housing stock is primarily mature 1950s–1970s detached homes, with many properties having undergone significant renovation. The community's elevation and reservoir views make it a premium STR location in the west quadrant. Best inventory is detached 3-bedroom homes with southern exposure toward the reservoir, which provide the most compelling listing photography. Guest profile: outdoor recreation visitors (pathway cycling, reservoir paddling), mountain-bound travellers who prefer an inner-city feel over suburban communities, and Stampede guests seeking a quieter west-end base. Glenmore Sailing School and Calgary Canoe Club access is a genuine differentiated feature.

renter_intel.title: Reservoir Views — The Primary STR Differentiator
renter_intel.description: Lakeview properties on the southern slopes of the community directly overlook the Glenmore Reservoir, providing an unusually scenic urban setting visible in listing photography. The reservoir view story — combined with direct pathway access to 16 km of waterfront trails — is the strongest STR marketing narrative for top-performing listings in this community. Homes with southern-facing rear yards or decks command the most competitive nightly rates.

renter_intel.title: North Glenmore Park — Walking Distance from South Lakeview
renter_intel.description: North Glenmore Park borders Lakeview's southern boundary, providing 208 acres of reservoir-front greenspace with tennis courts, paved and unpaved trails, fire pits and BBQ areas, a boat launch, and the Calgary Canoe Club and Calgary Rowing Club. Combined with the Glenmore Sailing School (City of Calgary, seasonal rentals from June–September), guests can access sailing, kayaking, canoeing, and cycling without a car.

renter_intel.title: Crowchild Trail — 15-Minute Downtown Connection
renter_intel.description: Crowchild Trail runs along Lakeview's eastern boundary, providing a direct, traffic-signal-minimised route to inner-city Calgary (Marda Loop, 4th Street SW, downtown) in 15–20 minutes. This connectivity makes Lakeview unusual among west Calgary communities in offering both nature immersion and rapid downtown access, broadening the STR guest profile to include event attendees and urban explorers.

whats_nearby.category: North Glenmore Park (Glenmore Sailing School, Pathways) — 5 min or Walk
whats_nearby.items: North Glenmore Park encompasses the reservoir's north shore from Lakeview to the Calgary Canoe Club and Rowing Club near Heritage Park. The City of Calgary's Glenmore Sailing School at the park's boat launch provides kayak and canoe rentals (June–Labour Day) and sailing lessons for all skill levels. The park is open year-round with cross-country skiing on snow in winter.

whats_nearby.category: Marda Loop — 10 min on Crowchild Trail
whats_nearby.items: Marda Loop — Calgary's premier inner-city lifestyle district centred on 33 Avenue and 4 Street SW — provides independent restaurants, wine bars, boutiques, yoga studios, and the popular Community Natural Foods. For guests who value neighbourhood culture over chain retail, Marda Loop is the closest urban dining and entertainment hub to Lakeview and is accessible in under 10 minutes.

Mount Royal University on Richard Road SW is 10 minutes from Lakeview via Crowchild Trail. The university drives periodic visitor demand (convocation seasons, campus events, parent visits) and a year-round academic staff market. MRU also houses the Taylor Centre for the Performing Arts, which generates occasional event-related accommodation demand from concert and performance attendees.

lakeview.peak_season: June–September (Glenmore Reservoir proximity, mountain gateway). Winter secondary for ski season.
lakeview.guest_profile: Outdoor enthusiasts, mountain-bound families, golf tourists.
lakeview.stand_out: Detached homes with Glenmore Reservoir views and pathway access.
lakeview.distinctive_fact: Lakeview literally overlooks the Glenmore Reservoir — properties with reservoir views see the strongest bookings in this community. BNBCalc data for this west zone shows consistent summer performance for well-run whole homes.

north-glenmore-park.name: North Glenmore Park
north-glenmore-park.subtitle: West Calgary Reservoir-Front Community with Sailing and Canoe Club Access
north-glenmore-park.str_intro:
North Glenmore Park is a small, established west Calgary community that sits directly on the northern shore of the Glenmore Reservoir, sharing its southern boundary with the North Glenmore Park open space — a 208-acre City of Calgary park that provides the community's primary recreational identity. The park houses the Calgary Rowing Club, Calgary Canoe Club, and the City of Calgary's Glenmore Sailing School, as well as extensive paved and unpaved trail networks, tennis courts, fire pits, BBQ facilities, and a public boat launch. Crowchild Trail and Glenmore Trail access connects residents to downtown Calgary in approximately 20 minutes and to the mountain corridor via Stoney Trail.

North Glenmore Park's lifestyle story is almost entirely outdoor-recreation-driven. The adjacent park's 16 km reservoir pathway runs east to Heritage Park Historical Village and west to the Weaselhead Flats, offering cyclists and walkers a full-day route within the city. The Glenmore Sailing School provides lessons and kayak and canoe rentals from June through Labour Day — an unusual urban water sport amenity. Westhills Shopping Centre on Glenmore Trail west provides retail access, while Marda Loop and the 17th Avenue corridor are accessible via Crowchild Trail for dining and nightlife. Mount Royal University is nearby, supporting an academic guest market.

North Glenmore Park is a very small community with a limited number of properties — the housing stock consists primarily of mature detached homes with some of the most direct Glenmore Reservoir access of any Calgary community. Best STR inventory is detached 3-bedroom homes with southern exposure or direct pathway access toward the park. The reservoir views and park adjacency are the premium pricing drivers. Guest profile: outdoor recreation visitors, paddlesport enthusiasts, mountain-bound travellers, and couples seeking a scenic west Calgary base. Supply constraint in this tiny community supports premium pricing.
north-glenmore-park.str_lifestyle: North Glenmore Park's lifestyle story is almost entirely outdoor-recreation-driven. The adjacent park's 16 km reservoir pathway runs east to Heritage Park Historical Village and west to the Weaselhead Flats, offering cyclists and walkers a full-day route within the city. The Glenmore Sailing School provides lessons and kayak and canoe rentals from June through Labour Day — an unusual urban water sport amenity. Westhills Shopping Centre on Glenmore Trail west provides retail access, while Marda Loop and the 17th Avenue corridor are accessible via Crowchild Trail for dining and nightlife. Mount Royal University is nearby, supporting an academic guest market.
north-glenmore-park.str_housing: North Glenmore Park is a very small community with a limited number of properties — the housing stock consists primarily of mature detached homes with some of the most direct Glenmore Reservoir access of any Calgary community. Best STR inventory is detached 3-bedroom homes with southern exposure or direct pathway access toward the park. The reservoir views and park adjacency are the premium pricing drivers. Guest profile: outdoor recreation visitors, paddlesport enthusiasts, mountain-bound travellers, and couples seeking a scenic west Calgary base. Supply constraint in this tiny community supports premium pricing.

renter_intel.title: Glenmore Sailing School and Canoe Club — Steps from the Community
renter_intel.description: The City of Calgary's Glenmore Sailing School at North Glenmore Park provides kayak and canoe rentals (June–Labour Day, weekdays 1–8 pm, weekends from 9:30 am), sailing lessons for all ages, and access to small boat hire on the reservoir. The Calgary Canoe Club and Calgary Rowing Club are also based here. This cluster of water sport organisations makes North Glenmore Park the most direct STR community in Calgary for water recreation access.

renter_intel.title: 16 km Reservoir Pathway — Doorstep Access
renter_intel.description: The 16 km Glenmore Reservoir multi-use pathway begins at North Glenmore Park and connects westward to the Weaselhead Flats and Heritage Park Historical Village to the east, passing South Glenmore Park, the Glenmore Sailing Club, and multiple beach and marina access points. For cycling or walking guests, this pathway provides hours of route variety without entering city traffic.

renter_intel.title: Heritage Park Historical Village — 15 min by Pathway or 10 min by Car
renter_intel.description: Heritage Park Historical Village is accessible from North Glenmore Park by cycling or walking west along the reservoir pathway (approximately 15 minutes) or by a 10-minute drive. The park is one of Calgary's top family tourism destinations and a half-day activity anchor for visiting families — particularly valuable for extended-stay guests looking for structured programming.

whats_nearby.category: North Glenmore Park (208 Acres, Boat Launch, Tennis, Trails) — Adjacent
whats_nearby.items: North Glenmore Park is a 208-acre City of Calgary open space running along the Glenmore Reservoir's north shore. It provides paved and unpaved multi-use trails, tennis courts, fire pits and BBQ areas, a children's playground, picnic facilities, and direct access to the public boat launch. Open year-round — cross-country skiing in winter when snow conditions allow.

The Earl Grey Golf Club — a private 18-hole club established in 1919 on the Glenmore Reservoir's northern edge — is one of Calgary's most prestigious private courses. Access is members-only; for STR guests seeking golf, the nearby public Lakeview Golf Course (a City-operated 9-hole course at North Glenmore Park with putting green overlooking the reservoir) provides an alternative.

Westhills Shopping Centre on Glenmore Trail west of the community provides a full grocery and retail hub, including Superstore, Cineplex, Chapters, and a range of restaurants. It is the primary large-format retail destination for west Calgary communities. Lakeview Plaza (closer, within the Lakeview community) handles daily essentials.

north-glenmore-park.peak_season: June–September (Glenmore Reservoir proximity, mountain gateway). Winter secondary for ski season.
north-glenmore-park.stand_out: Detached homes with Glenmore Reservoir views and pathway access.
north-glenmore-park.distinctive_fact: North Glenmore Park sits directly on the Glenmore Reservoir — properties with reservoir views or pathway access see the strongest bookings in this community. BNBCalc data for this west zone shows consistent summer performance.

hamptons.name: Hamptons
hamptons.subtitle: NW Calgary's Premier Golf Community with Mountain Views and Private Club Amenities
hamptons.str_intro:
The Hamptons is northwest Calgary's most prestigious residential community, developed from 1990 onward on a rolling upland plateau bounded by Stoney Trail NW to the north, Shaganappi Trail to the east, Country Hills Boulevard to the south, and Sarcee Trail to the west. The community is organised around The Hamptons Golf Club — a private, 6,869-yard par-72 course that has hosted Champions Tour qualifying events and exhibitions featuring international professional golfers. The community's elevated plateau position (reaching 1,245 m above sea level) provides panoramic mountain views from many properties — a meaningful lifestyle premium over lower-elevation NW Calgary communities. Stoney Trail on the northern boundary provides direct ring-road access to the mountains in under 90 minutes.

The Hamptons Community Association (mandatory for all homeowners at $200/year) maintains a well-funded amenity suite that rivals private club facilities: an Olympic-size hockey rink with heated, sheltered bleachers, a secondary kiddie rink, tennis courts and a practice court, paved bike path system throughout the community, and sports fields with soccer pitches and ball diamonds. The Hamptons Golf Club's upscale clubhouse, aquatic driving range, and two indoor simulators serve members year-round. Beacon Hill Shopping Centre (Costco, Home Depot, Canadian Tire) is accessible within 5–10 minutes, while Nose Hill Park — one of North America's largest urban parks at 1,129 hectares — is immediately accessible via Edgemont Boulevard for hiking and pathway use.

The Hamptons' housing stock is 85% single-family detached homes, with median household income of $125,042 and average home prices ranging from $800K for single-family to $2M+ for golf-course-fronting estate properties. STR acquisitions at these price points require careful yield modelling. Best STR inventory is detached 3- to 4-bedroom homes with mountain views or golf course proximity in the $145–$245 nightly range. Guest profile: mountain-bound travellers, golf tourists (note: golf club membership is separate from property purchase and cannot be promised to guests), families visiting NW Calgary, and ski-season visitors in winter. The HOA mandatory fee ($200/year) should be factored into operating costs.
hamptons.str_lifestyle: The Hamptons Community Association (mandatory for all homeowners at $200/year) maintains a well-funded amenity suite that rivals private club facilities: an Olympic-size hockey rink with heated, sheltered bleachers, a secondary kiddie rink, tennis courts and a practice court, paved bike path system throughout the community, and sports fields with soccer pitches and ball diamonds. The Hamptons Golf Club's upscale clubhouse, aquatic driving range, and two indoor simulators serve members year-round. Beacon Hill Shopping Centre (Costco, Home Depot, Canadian Tire) is accessible within 5–10 minutes, while Nose Hill Park — one of North America's largest urban parks at 1,129 hectares — is immediately accessible via Edgemont Boulevard for hiking and pathway use.

STR acquisitions at these price points require careful yield modelling. Best STR inventory is detached 3- to 4-bedroom homes with mountain views or golf course proximity in the $145–$245 nightly range. Guest profile: mountain-bound travellers, golf tourists (note: golf club membership is separate from property purchase and cannot be promised to guests), families visiting NW Calgary, and ski-season visitors in winter. The HOA mandatory fee ($200/year) should be factored into operating costs.

renter_intel.title: The Hamptons Golf Club — Championship Course in the Community
renter_intel.description: The Hamptons Golf Club is a private 6,869-yard, par-72 course with a slope of 140 — one of Calgary's most demanding layouts — featuring an aquatic driving range, short game area, two indoor simulators, and an upscale clubhouse with dining. The club has hosted Champions Tour qualifying events and golf legends' exhibitions. Golf club membership is separate from property ownership and requires a separate application and fees — STR hosts should not imply guest access without confirming their membership status.

renter_intel.title: Panoramic Mountain Views from Elevated Plateau
renter_intel.description: The Hamptons sits on a rolling upland plateau at elevations up to 1,245 m above sea level, generating panoramic views of the Rocky Mountains to the west, Calgary's downtown skyline to the south, and Nose Hill's grassland slopes to the east. For listing photography, the mountain view framing is the primary premium anchor — properties with unobstructed westward views achieve the strongest first-click conversion on listing platforms.

renter_intel.title: Stoney Trail to Mountains — Under 90 Minutes to Banff
renter_intel.description: Stoney Trail NW borders the community to the north, providing immediate ring-road access to Highway 1 west toward Banff National Park (under 90 minutes) and Kananaskis Country (under 45 minutes). For ski season guests, the drive to Lake Louise Ski Resort or Sunshine Village is comparable in time to many Banff-area lodging options but at a significantly lower accommodation price point. This mountain gateway positioning is the strongest off-peak demand driver.

whats_nearby.category: Nose Hill Park — 5 min on Edgemont Boulevard
whats_nearby.items: Nose Hill Park at 6465 14 Street NW is one of North America's largest urban parks, covering 1,129 hectares of natural grassland, archaeological sites, and multi-use trails on Calgary's northwest plateau. From The Hamptons, the park's southern and western entry points are accessible in 5 minutes by car or a short cycle, offering hiking, mountain biking, off-leash dog access, and panoramic city and mountain views.

Beacon Hill Shopping Centre on Country Hills Boulevard provides a major retail hub anchored by Costco, Home Depot, Canadian Tire, Winners, Sport Chek, and Real Canadian Superstore. For guests arriving for extended stays, Costco at Beacon Hill provides economical bulk grocery provisioning without requiring a trip to the inner city.

WinSport's Canada Olympic Park facility (a legacy of the 1988 Winter Olympics) is approximately 20 minutes from The Hamptons via Stoney Trail west and Bow Trail. The facility offers year-round mountain biking and hiking on Olympic-legacy slopes in summer, and ski, snowboard, tubing, and luge sliding in winter — providing structured mountain sport activities without a full mountain drive.

hamptons.peak_season: June–September (mountain views, golf, mountain gateway). Winter secondary for ski season.
hamptons.best_property_type: Detached 3-4 bed with mountain views near The Hamptons Golf Club.
hamptons.guest_profile: Mountain-bound travellers, golf tourists, families visiting NW Calgary.
hamptons.stand_out: Detached homes with mountain views near The Hamptons Golf Club.
hamptons.distinctive_fact: The Hamptons Golf Club is a genuine demand driver — properties near the course can market the golf access to golf tourists. Mountain views add a secondary premium. BNBCalc data for this NW zone shows consistent summer performance.

wildwood.name: Wildwood
wildwood.subtitle: West Calgary Character Bungalows Above Edworthy Park and the Bow River
wildwood.str_intro:
Wildwood is an intimate, established west Calgary community situated on a plateau above the south shore of the Bow River, directly adjacent to Edworthy Park at 5050 Spruce Drive SW. Developed primarily in the 1950s with its community association founded in 1956, Wildwood is bounded to the north by the Bow River and Edworthy Park, to the east by Spruce Cliff, and to the south by Bow Trail. The community's defining character is its stock of wide-lot character bungalows — many with legal suites — and its proximity to one of Calgary's most beloved natural parks. Downtown Calgary is approximately 15–20 minutes by car via Bow Trail, making Wildwood one of the more accessible west-end communities for urban-adjacent guests.

Edworthy Park's 169 hectares of natural area park at Wildwood's northern boundary includes the Douglas Fir Trail (a ravine hiking trail through old-growth forest), Lawrey Gardens, and the Bow River pathway — one of Calgary's most-used cycling and recreational corridors extending east to downtown and west through Bowmont Park and beyond. The Wildwood Community Association at 4411 Spruce Drive SW maintains a community centre with active programming including a playschool, sports fields, and outdoor amenities. Wildwood Elementary School serves the community. Bow Trail provides direct access to the inner-city 17th Avenue and Marda Loop districts within 10 minutes, supporting a walkable urban lifestyle character that is unusual for a west Calgary community.

Wildwood's separate-entry suite stock is rated 'High' compared to other communities in this batch — an important STR differentiator. Character bungalows with legal suites near Edworthy Park or the Bow River pathway are the ideal STR vehicle: the lower suite provides flexible two-party or owner-occupied income while the upper unit hosts guests in a charming, well-photographed space. The outdoor recreation narrative (Edworthy Park, Douglas Fir Trail, Bow River pathway) is the strongest listing story for summer-peak June–September. Mountain-bound travellers and couples seeking a riverside-adjacent city base are the core guest segments. The broad seasonal range is supported by year-round Bow River pathway use including winter cycling and cross-country skiing.
wildwood.str_lifestyle: Edworthy Park's 169 hectares of natural area park at Wildwood's northern boundary includes the Douglas Fir Trail (a ravine hiking trail through old-growth forest), Lawrey Gardens, and the Bow River pathway — one of Calgary's most-used cycling and recreational corridors extending east to downtown and west through Bowmont Park and beyond. The Wildwood Community Association at 4411 Spruce Drive SW maintains a community centre with active programming including a playschool, sports fields, and outdoor amenities. Wildwood Elementary School serves the community. Bow Trail provides direct access to the inner-city 17th Avenue and Marda Loop districts within 10 minutes, supporting a walkable urban lifestyle character that is unusual for a west Calgary community.

Character bungalows with legal suites near Edworthy Park or the Bow River pathway are the ideal STR vehicle: the lower suite provides flexible two-party or owner-occupied income while the upper unit hosts guests in a charming, well-photographed space. The outdoor recreation narrative (Edworthy Park, Douglas Fir Trail, Bow River pathway) is the strongest listing story for summer-peak June–September. Mountain-bound travellers and couples seeking a riverside-adjacent city base are the core guest segments. The broad seasonal range is supported by year-round Bow River pathway use including winter cycling and cross-country skiing.

renter_intel.title: Legal Suite Stock — Highest Separate Entry Availability in this Community Group
renter_intel.description: Wildwood's 'High' separate entry stock rating distinguishes it from most communities in this batch. Character bungalows with legal basement suites are the preferred investment vehicle, offering multi-income flexibility: the legal suite can serve as long-term rental income, owner-occupied space, or a second STR unit depending on the operator's strategy. Confirm suite legality with the City of Calgary development permit records before listing.

renter_intel.title: Edworthy Park and Douglas Fir Trail — Steps from the Front Door
renter_intel.description: Edworthy Park (169 hectares, 5050 Spruce Drive SW) is immediately north of Wildwood, accessible on foot from virtually any street in the community. The park's Douglas Fir Trail winds through a protected old-growth Douglas fir ravine — one of Calgary's most distinctive urban hikes — while the flat Bow River pathway at the river level connects east to downtown and west through Bowmont Park. Off-leash dog areas and river-bench picnic areas are open year-round.

renter_intel.title: Bow Trail — 15-Minute Downtown Connection
renter_intel.description: Bow Trail (a high-speed one-way arterial) connects Wildwood directly to downtown Calgary in approximately 15 minutes, passing through Shaganappi Point and terminating near the Eau Claire Market. For guests attending Calgary Stampede, concerts at the Scotiabank Saddledome, or downtown business, the direct Bow Trail route is faster than many inner-city suburbs. Return via Memorial Drive provides the riverside perspective.

whats_nearby.category: Edworthy Park (Bow River Pathway, Douglas Fir Trail) — Adjacent
whats_nearby.items: Edworthy Park at 5050 Spruce Drive SW is a 169-hectare natural area park along the Bow River. Access points from Wildwood's northern streets lead to the Douglas Fir Trail hiking loop, Lawrey Gardens (heritage gardens in the park), river-level Bow River pathway cycling, and a popular off-leash dog area. The park is open 5 am–11 pm year-round with no admission charge.

whats_nearby.category: Marda Loop and 17th Avenue — 10 min on Bow Trail
whats_nearby.items: Marda Loop (33 Avenue and 4 Street SW) and the 17th Avenue Restaurant Row are 10 minutes from Wildwood via Bow Trail and the 17th Avenue interchange. Both districts offer Calgary's best concentration of independent restaurants, cafés, wine bars, boutiques, and fitness studios, providing guests with a genuine inner-city social experience from a quieter residential base.

WinSport at Canada Olympic Park on Bow Trail is approximately 15 minutes from Wildwood heading west. Year-round programming includes mountain biking on Olympic-legacy slopes, zip-lining, and luge in summer; alpine skiing, snowboarding, and tube sliding in winter. The facility provides structured outdoor activity without the full mountain drive, appealing to guests with limited time.

wildwood.peak_season: June–September (Edworthy Park proximity, Bow River access). Mountain gateway secondary.
wildwood.best_property_type: Character bungalow with legal suite near Edworthy Park.
wildwood.guest_profile: Outdoor enthusiasts, mountain-bound travellers, couples seeking a riverside stay.
wildwood.stand_out: Character bungalows with legal suites near Edworthy Park or the Bow River pathway.
wildwood.distinctive_fact: Wildwood is one of Calgary's most underrated west-end communities — its character bungalows with legal suites and proximity to Edworthy Park and the Bow River create a strong outdoor recreation niche.

monterey-park.name: Monterey Park
monterey-park.subtitle: NE Calgary Community Adjacent to Elliston Park and GlobalFest's International Fireworks
monterey-park.str_intro:
Monterey Park is a northeast Calgary community adjacent to Elliston Park, located at 17 Avenue SE and 68 Street SE — the original venue for GlobalFest, Calgary's premier international fireworks and multicultural festival. The community is bounded by 64 Avenue NE to the north, Stoney Trail to the east, McKnight Boulevard to the south, and approximately 60 Street NE to the west. Its northeast quadrant position delivers strong airport proximity (Calgary International Airport is approximately 15–20 minutes via McKnight Boulevard and Barlow Trail), making it a viable year-round base for airline and airport-adjacent travellers. The community was developed in the 1990s with Californian-influenced streetscaping and a lake at its eastern edge.

Monterey Park's lifestyle narrative centres on Elliston Park — a major City of Calgary open space that has historically hosted GlobalFest's five-night international fireworks festival each August, drawing over 10,000 attendees per night from across the city. During GlobalFest weeks, adjacent community streets typically restrict non-resident parking, making a walkable Monterey Park address a significant guest advantage. Don Hartman Northeast Sportsplex (shared with Coral Springs to the north) provides indoor ice, gymnasium, and fitness access year-round. Bus routes 68, 87, 307, and 440 serve the Elliston Park area. Sunridge Mall and East Hills Shopping Centre are 10–15 minutes away for retail and provisioning.

Monterey Park's housing stock is primarily 1990s detached family homes. Best STR inventory is detached 3-bedroom homes with proximity to Elliston Park for the GlobalFest premium and airport travellers year-round. Separate entry suite stock is mixed. The primary seasonal demand spike is GlobalFest week in August; secondary demand comes from year-round airport proximity. Verify current GlobalFest venue (the festival relocated to Spruce Meadows in 2026) before marketing Elliston Park proximity as the festival anchor — it may be relevant for future years or confirmed temporary.

renter_intel.title: GlobalFest / Elliston Park — Annual International Fireworks Festival
renter_intel.description: Elliston Park (1827 68 Street SE, adjacent to Monterey Park) has historically hosted GlobalFest — a 5-night international fireworks and multicultural festival held each August, celebrating 20+ countries with pavilions, performances, food, and world-class pyrotechnics. During festival nights, parking restrictions are enforced throughout surrounding communities, making a walkable Monterey Park property a premium booking for the 50,000+ total annual attendees. IMPORTANT: Verify current venue for 2025 and 2026 — GlobalFest's official site lists 2026 dates at Spruce Meadows.

renter_intel.title: Calgary International Airport — 15–20 min
renter_intel.description: Calgary International Airport is accessible from Monterey Park in approximately 15–20 minutes via McKnight Boulevard east and Barlow Trail north, or via Stoney Trail. This proximity drives year-round baseline demand from airline crews, long-connection travellers, and international families visiting NE Calgary relatives. Whole-home listings consistently outperform shared spaces for this segment.

renter_intel.title: Elliston Park / Monterey Park Lake — Summer Recreational Amenity
renter_intel.description: Elliston Park and the Monterey Park lake system at the community's eastern edge provide open-space recreational access including walking paths, lake views, and picnic areas. While the lake in Monterey Park is less amenity-rich than the private Coral Springs lake to the north (no swimming or boating), the park setting provides a quiet outdoor character that complements the community's family residential identity.

whats_nearby.category: Elliston Park (GlobalFest Venue, Lake, Pathways) — Adjacent
whats_nearby.items: Elliston Park at 1827 68 Street SE (historically accessed at 17 Ave SE and 60 St SE per Visit Calgary) is a major City of Calgary open-space park with a lake, walking pathways, and event infrastructure. Beyond GlobalFest, the park hosts community events and provides a green buffer on Monterey Park's eastern boundary. Note the 2026 GlobalFest venue move to Spruce Meadows — verify annually.

The Don Hartman Northeast Sportsplex, shared between Coral Springs and Monterey Park area, provides two NHL-sized hockey arenas, a gymnasium, and a fitness training room. It is a year-round indoor recreation anchor for NE Calgary and a draw for regional sports tournaments from October through April.

whats_nearby.category: East Hills Shopping Centre and Sunridge Mall — 10–15 min
whats_nearby.items: East Hills Shopping Centre (Costco, Cineplex, Walmart, big-box retail) at the junction of Stoney Trail and 17 Avenue SE is 10 minutes from Monterey Park. Sunridge Mall at Sunridge Way NE provides a traditional enclosed mall experience. Together these retail nodes provide complete provisioning and entertainment access for extended-stay family groups.

monterey-park.peak_season: June–August (lake access, Elliston Park proximity). Year-round secondary from airport proximity.
monterey-park.best_property_type: Detached 3-bed near Elliston Park or with lake access.
monterey-park.guest_profile: Families seeking lake access, GlobalFest attendees, airport travellers.
monterey-park.stand_out: Detached homes near Elliston Park. Properties that market the GlobalFest proximity and airport convenience.
monterey-park.distinctive_fact: Monterey Park is adjacent to Elliston Park — home to GlobalFest, one of Calgary's largest summer festivals. Properties near the park see strong bookings during GlobalFest week. BNBCalc data for this NE zone shows consistent summer performance.

edgemont.name: Edgemont
edgemont.subtitle: Calgary's Largest NW Community on the Nose Hill Ridge with Mountain Panoramas
edgemont.str_intro:
Edgemont is one of Calgary's largest residential communities, home to approximately 16,000 residents and located in the northwest quadrant on a northwest extension of Nose Hill. Bounded by John Laurie Boulevard to the south, Sarcee Trail to the west, Country Hills Boulevard to the north, and Shaganappi Trail to the east, Edgemont sits at elevations reaching 1,245 metres above sea level — among the highest residential positions in the city. This elevation provides jaw-dropping panoramic views of the Rocky Mountains to the west and Calgary's downtown skyline to the south, visible from many streets throughout the community. Nose Hill Park, one of North America's largest urban parks (1,129 hectares), borders Edgemont directly to the east.

Edgemont's green space density is extraordinary for a suburban community: 30+ km of internal walking and biking paths, three off-leash parks, numerous ravines, and the Nose Hill Park border. The Edgemont Community Association at 33 Edgevalley Circle NW operates an active community centre with a gymnasium, commercial kitchen, and year-round programming — including the Edgemont Soccer Association, outdoor skating rinks, tennis courts, and summer sports camps. Market Mall and Beacon Hill Shopping Centre are both within a 10-minute drive, providing full retail access. Crowfoot Crossing LRT station (Blue Line) is accessible within 10–15 minutes, providing downtown rail connections.

Edgemont's housing stock is primarily 1980s–2000s single-detached homes across seven subareas (Edgemont Estates, Edgedale, Edenwold, Edgepark, Edgevalley, Edgebrook, and Edgeridge), with approximately 41% of residents having lived in the community for over 20 years — reflecting a highly stable, owner-occupied base. Best STR inventory is detached 3- to 4-bedroom homes with westward mountain views, particularly in the Edgeridge and Edgevalley subareas which command the best view angles. Guest profile: mountain-bound travellers using Stoney Trail and Country Hills Boulevard access, outdoor recreation visitors drawn to Nose Hill, and ski-season guests. Country Hills Boulevard provides direct Stoney Trail connectivity for mountain corridor access.

The Edgemont Community Association at 33 Edgevalley Circle NW operates an active community centre with a gymnasium, commercial kitchen, and year-round programming — including the Edgemont Soccer Association, outdoor skating rinks, tennis courts, and summer sports camps. Market Mall and Beacon Hill Shopping Centre are both within a 10-minute drive, providing full retail access. Country Hills Boulevard provides direct Stoney Trail connectivity for mountain corridor access.

renter_intel.title: Nose Hill Park — Walking Distance from East Edgemont
renter_intel.description: Nose Hill Park (1,129 hectares, 6465 14 Street NW) is one of North America's three largest urban parks, bordering Edgemont's eastern edge. The park's rough-fescue grasslands, ancient topography, and elevated viewpoints provide panoramic city and mountain vistas, deer and coyote sightings, and over 20 km of dirt and paved trails for hiking, mountain biking, and off-leash dog walking. From Edgemont's eastern streets, the Shaganappi Trail entrance is a 5-minute walk.

renter_intel.title: Mountain Views — A Defining Listing Feature
renter_intel.description: Edgemont's plateau elevation (up to 1,245 m) provides unobstructed westward views of the Rocky Mountain foothills and peaks from many streets in the community. In listing photography, mountain view framing from a rear deck or upper-floor window is one of the strongest click-through drivers on Alberta accommodation platforms. Properties in the western and ridge-top subareas (Edgevalley, Edgeridge) typically command the best view premiums.

renter_intel.title: Country Hills Boulevard — Direct Mountain Access via Stoney Trail
renter_intel.description: Country Hills Boulevard runs along Edgemont's southern boundary and connects directly to Stoney Trail, providing seamless mountain corridor access to Kananaskis Country (under 45 minutes) and Banff National Park (under 90 minutes). For ski-season guests, WinSport at Canada Olympic Park is approximately 20 minutes via Stoney Trail west. This direct mountain gateway access is a year-round demand driver that mitigates Edgemont's November–March slow season.

whats_nearby.category: Nose Hill Park — 5–10 min Walk from East Edgemont
whats_nearby.items: Nose Hill Park is a 1,129-hectare natural grassland park covering the hills north and east of Edgemont. Multiple entry points along Edgemont Boulevard and the community's eastern streets provide trailhead access to the park's extensive pathway network. The park's highest points offer one of the best unobstructed panoramic views in Calgary, encompassing the downtown skyline, the Rockies, the Bow River valley, and the airport.

whats_nearby.category: Beacon Hill Shopping Centre (Costco, Home Depot) — 10 min
whats_nearby.items: Beacon Hill Shopping Centre on Country Hills Boulevard NW provides the primary large-format retail hub for NW Calgary, anchored by Costco, Home Depot, Canadian Tire, Winners, and Real Canadian Superstore. For guests arriving for extended stays, Costco bulk grocery provisioning at Beacon Hill eliminates the need for multiple smaller shops.

Market Mall at Shaganappi Trail and 32 Avenue NW is a large enclosed shopping mall with department stores, cinema, restaurants, and specialty retail. It is the primary indoor shopping and entertainment destination for NW Calgary and is accessible in approximately 15 minutes from Edgemont via Shaganappi Trail south. The University of Calgary campus is adjacent.

edgemont.peak_season: June–September (mountain views, Nose Hill proximity, mountain gateway). Winter secondary for ski season.
edgemont.best_property_type: Detached 3-4 bed with mountain views near Nose Hill Park.
edgemont.guest_profile: Mountain-bound travellers, outdoor enthusiasts, families visiting NW Calgary.
edgemont.stand_out: Detached homes with mountain views near Nose Hill Park.
edgemont.distinctive_fact: Edgemont sits on the ridge above Nose Hill Park with mountain views — properties with views and park access see stronger bookings from outdoor recreation guests. BNBCalc data for this NW zone shows consistent summer performance.

discovery-ridge.name: Discovery Ridge
discovery-ridge.subtitle: West Calgary Nature Enclave Backing onto Fish Creek Park and the Elbow River
discovery-ridge.str_intro:
Discovery Ridge is a premium west Calgary community located at the city's western edge, bounded by Stoney Trail to the north, 69 Street SW to the east, Rocky View County to the west, and the Elbow River and Tsuut'ina Nation reserve to the south. The community's defining feature is its direct access to both Griffith Woods Park — a 93-hectare natural environment park along the Elbow River within the community — and Fish Creek Provincial Park, which borders the area to the south. Stoney Trail on the northern boundary provides direct ring-road access to Kananaskis Country in under 45 minutes and Banff in under 90 minutes, positioning Discovery Ridge as Calgary's closest established community to the mountain wilderness.

Discovery Ridge's premium landscaping and greenspace are maintained by the New Discovery Homeowners Association (NDHA), which charges a mandatory annual encumbrance fee of $325 per residential property for the maintenance of roundabout features, boulevard plantings, pathway snow clearing, and community greenspaces. The Discovery Ridge Community Association (DRCA) organises recreational programming, sports facilities (ice rink and tennis courts), and community events. Griffith Woods Park at 45 Discovery Ridge Link SW offers riverside hiking, wildlife spotting (the Elbow River corridor is rich in bird life, deer, and small mammals), and multi-use trail networks. Westhills Shopping Centre on Glenmore Trail SW is 10–15 minutes away for retail provisioning.

Discovery Ridge's housing stock is primarily 2000s–2010s single-detached homes on generously sized lots, many backing onto Griffith Woods Park or internal greenspace corridors. Separate entry suite stock is 'Rare', meaning whole-home detached 3-bedroom listings are the primary STR format. Properties backing onto the park provide the strongest listing photography and command the best nightly rate premiums. Guest profile: outdoor recreation visitors, mountain-bound travellers, and families seeking a nature-immersive experience within Calgary city limits. Occupancy skews strongly seasonal (June–September) with a ski-season winter secondary driven by Stoney Trail mountain access.

The Discovery Ridge Community Association (DRCA) organises recreational programming, sports facilities (ice rink and tennis courts), and community events. Griffith Woods Park at 45 Discovery Ridge Link SW offers riverside hiking, wildlife spotting (the Elbow River corridor is rich in bird life, deer, and small mammals), and multi-use trail networks. Westhills Shopping Centre on Glenmore Trail SW is 10–15 minutes away for retail provisioning.

Separate entry suite stock is 'Rare', meaning whole-home detached 3-bedroom listings are the primary STR format. Properties backing onto the park provide the strongest listing photography and command the best nightly rate premiums. Guest profile: outdoor recreation visitors, mountain-bound travellers, and families seeking a nature-immersive experience within Calgary city limits. Occupancy skews strongly seasonal (June–September) with a ski-season winter secondary driven by Stoney Trail mountain access.

renter_intel.title: Griffith Woods Park — 93-Hectare Elbow River Natural Park In the Community
renter_intel.description: Griffith Woods Park (45 Discovery Ridge Link SW) is a 93-hectare natural environment park along the Elbow River within the Discovery Ridge community boundary, maintained by the City of Calgary. The park contains riverside hiking trails, mature cottonwood and spruce forest, and diverse wildlife habitat. Multiple entry points from Discovery Ridge Boulevard allow guests to access the park on foot from the property. This in-community park access is the primary STR differentiator for Discovery Ridge.

renter_intel.title: Fish Creek Provincial Park — Adjacent Southern Boundary
renter_intel.description: Fish Creek Provincial Park, one of North America's largest urban parks, is directly accessible at Discovery Ridge's southern boundary. The park's extensive multi-use trail system, the Bow River and Elbow River corridors, Sikome Lake (supervised swimming), and over 80 km of cycling paths are all reachable within a 10-minute drive or short ride from the community. Fish Creek provides meaningful activity programming for multi-day family stays without leaving the city.

renter_intel.title: Stoney Trail West — Direct Mountain Corridor
renter_intel.description: Stoney Trail runs along Discovery Ridge's northern boundary at the Glenmore Trail interchange, providing the city's most direct mountain access from any established SW community. Kananaskis Country (Peter Lougheed Provincial Park, Barrier Lake, Canmore) is under 45 minutes; Banff National Park entrance is under 90 minutes. This access is a year-round demand driver for mountain-focused guests and a differentiating factor for ski-season winter bookings.

whats_nearby.category: Griffith Woods Park (Elbow River) — In the Community
whats_nearby.items: Griffith Woods Park at 45 Discovery Ridge Link SW is a 93-hectare protected natural area along the Elbow River, managed by the City of Calgary. The park contains river-bottom forest, wetlands, and diverse wildlife including deer, coyotes, great blue herons, and a variety of songbirds. Multiple pathway entry points from Discovery Ridge Boulevard SW provide direct access. Open 5 am–11 pm year-round.

whats_nearby.category: Fish Creek Provincial Park — 5–10 min South
whats_nearby.items: Fish Creek Provincial Park's western access points are within 5–10 minutes of Discovery Ridge. The park extends from the Elbow River confluence to the Bow River across 34 km, containing over 80 km of multi-use trails, picnic sites, equestrian paths, Sikome Lake (supervised swimming), and abundant wildlife. It is one of North America's most visited urban parks annually.

Westhills Shopping Centre at Glenmore Trail and 69 Street SW is the primary retail hub for west Calgary, anchored by Cineplex Odeon, Chapters, Real Canadian Superstore, and a range of restaurants. It is the closest large-format shopping destination for Discovery Ridge residents and provides full provisioning access for extended-stay guests.

discovery-ridge.peak_season: June–September (Elbow River access, Fish Creek Provincial Park proximity). Winter secondary for ski season.
discovery-ridge.best_property_type: Detached home near Fish Creek Provincial Park or the Elbow River.
discovery-ridge.guest_profile: Outdoor enthusiasts, mountain-bound travellers, families visiting west Calgary.
discovery-ridge.stand_out: Homes backing onto Fish Creek Provincial Park or near the Elbow River.
discovery-ridge.distinctive_fact: Discovery Ridge backs onto Fish Creek Provincial Park and is adjacent to the Elbow River — properties with park or river access are the primary STR differentiator in this community.

lake-bonavista.name: Lake Bonavista
lake-bonavista.subtitle: Canada's First Private Lake Community — SE Calgary with Dual-Lake Year-Round Recreation
lake-bonavista.str_intro:
Lake Bonavista holds a singular distinction in Canadian urban history: it was the first community in Canada to be built around a man-made lake, developed by Keith Construction starting in 1967 and serving as the prototype for every lake community in Calgary that followed. Located in the southeast quadrant, bounded by Anderson Road to the north, Macleod Trail to the west, Canyon Meadows Drive to the south, and Bonavista Downs to the east, the community centres on two private lakes: the 52-acre Lake Bonavista and the 35-acre Lake Bonaventure. Access to both lakes is restricted to members of the Lake Bonavista Homeowners Association, whose membership is mandatory for all homeowners in the area.

Lake Bonavista's amenity suite is extensive for a mature SE community. The lakes support swimming from sandy beaches, recreational boating and fishing, kayaking and paddleboarding in summer, and outdoor ice skating and tobogganing from the community's 65-foot hill (built from excavated lake material) in winter. The Lake Bonavista Community Association facility houses two ice arenas, a gymnasium, fitness studio, and multipurpose rooms. The Lake Bonavista Promenade — a small commercial development within the community — provides boutique retail and professional services. Macleod Trail's restaurant corridor, Southcentre Mall, and CF Chinook Centre are all within 5–10 minutes. Fish Creek Provincial Park borders the community to the south.

Lake Bonavista's housing stock ranges from original 1960s–1970s homes to substantially rebuilt or extensively renovated properties on large lots, with many properties commanding lake-frontage premiums. The Lake Bonavista Homeowners Association membership is mandatory and covers upkeep of both lakes — the annual fee is a fixed operating cost for any STR property in the community. Separate entry suite stock is 'Rare' meaning whole-home detached 3-bedroom-plus is the dominant STR format. Lake access is the decisive STR premium; properties without direct lake-frontage or lake view should be marketed honestly on their proximity to Fish Creek and Macleod Trail access rather than the lake itself.

The lakes support swimming from sandy beaches, recreational boating and fishing, kayaking and paddleboarding in summer, and outdoor ice skating and tobogganing from the community's 65-foot hill (built from excavated lake material) in winter. The Lake Bonavista Community Association facility houses two ice arenas, a gymnasium, fitness studio, and multipurpose rooms. The Lake Bonavista Promenade — a small commercial development within the community — provides boutique retail and professional services. Macleod Trail's restaurant corridor, Southcentre Mall, and CF Chinook Centre are all within 5–10 minutes. Fish Creek Provincial Park borders the community to the south.

The Lake Bonavista Homeowners Association membership is mandatory and covers upkeep of both lakes — the annual fee is a fixed operating cost for any STR property in the community. Separate entry suite stock is 'Rare' meaning whole-home detached 3-bedroom-plus is the dominant STR format. Lake access is the decisive STR premium; properties without direct lake-frontage or lake view should be marketed honestly on their proximity to Fish Creek and Macleod Trail access rather than the lake itself.

renter_intel.title: Private 52-Acre Lake — Swimming, Boating, Fishing, Winter Skating
renter_intel.description: Lake Bonavista (52 acres) provides year-round recreation: swimming from private beaches, kayaking, paddleboarding, canoeing, and fishing in summer; outdoor ice skating and tobogganing from the purpose-built 65-foot hill in winter. The Lake Bonaventure second lake (35 acres) is accessible only to homes built directly around it. Lake access privileges come with HOA membership, which is mandatory for all homeowners — confirm the specific property's lake access before marketing.

renter_intel.title: Canada's First Lake Community — Heritage Positioning
renter_intel.description: Lake Bonavista's distinction as Canada's first planned lake community (1967) provides a genuine heritage narrative for premium listing positioning. The community's careful design — incorporating the 65-foot hill, the lake shoreline park, and the Promenade commercial strip — set the standard for all subsequent Calgary lake communities including Mahogany, McKenzie Lake, and Coral Springs. This heritage story resonates with guests seeking a genuinely curated neighbourhood experience.

renter_intel.title: Fish Creek Provincial Park — Southern Boundary Access
renter_intel.description: Fish Creek Provincial Park borders Lake Bonavista to the south, providing direct access to 80+ km of trails, the Bow River natural corridor, and Sikome Lake from the community's southern streets. Properties near Canyon Meadows Drive can walk to Fish Creek trail entry points, providing a second major outdoor amenity anchor alongside the private lake — a genuine two-venue outdoor recreation offer.

whats_nearby.category: Lake Bonavista Community Association (Dual Ice Arenas, Gym) — In the Community
whats_nearby.items: The Lake Bonavista Community Association centre within the neighbourhood houses two NHL-sized ice arenas (supplemented by the adjacent Frank McCool Arena), a gymnasium, fitness studio, and multipurpose programming rooms. Year-round programming includes hockey, skating, fitness classes, and community events including movies in the park and family festivals.

whats_nearby.category: Southcentre Mall and Macleod Trail Corridor — 5 min
whats_nearby.items: Southcentre Mall on Anderson Road and the broader Macleod Trail commercial corridor are within 5 minutes of Lake Bonavista. The corridor provides full retail, dining, cinema, and service access including the Avenida Food Hall and Fresh Market, major department stores, and a range of restaurant chains. CF Chinook Centre is an additional 10 minutes north on Macleod Trail.

whats_nearby.category: Fish Creek Provincial Park — 5 min or Walk from South Lake Bonavista
whats_nearby.items: Fish Creek Provincial Park is accessible on foot from the community's southern boundary streets, or within a 5-minute drive via Canyon Meadows Drive to the park's multiple northern entry points. The park's Bow Valley Ranch visitor centre, heritage site, and interpretive programming make it a half-day family destination alongside the fishing, cycling, and nature immersion activities throughout the park's 34 km length.

lake-bonavista.best_property_type: Detached home with Lake Bonavista access.
lake-bonavista.stand_out: Detached homes with Lake Bonavista access. 3+ bedrooms for family groups.
lake-bonavista.distinctive_fact: Lake Bonavista is one of Calgary's original private lake communities — properties with lake access see strong summer bookings from families who want a lake experience.

schools.name: Dr. E. P. Scarlett High School

belvedere.name: Belvedere
belvedere.subtitle: New-Build East Calgary Community Adjacent to East Hills Shopping and YYC Airport
belvedere.str_intro:
Belvedere is one of Calgary's newest master-planned communities, developed by Tristar Communities on the east side of the city at the corner of 17 Avenue SE and 84 Street SE — directly adjacent to East Hills Shopping Centre, a major retail destination anchored by Costco, Cineplex Odeon, and Walmart. The community's east Calgary location delivers meaningful airport proximity: Calgary International Airport (YYC) is accessible via 17 Avenue SE and Barlow Trail in approximately 15–20 minutes. Downtown Calgary is approximately 15 minutes west on 17 Avenue SE. Belvedere's brand-new housing stock — built from the early 2020s onward — offers the contemporary finishes and open-concept layouts that perform strongly on short-term rental platforms.

Belvedere's most immediate lifestyle advantage is its adjacency to East Hills Shopping Centre, which provides walkable access to major retailers without a long drive. Elliston Park (1827 68 Street SE) — the historic host of GlobalFest's international fireworks festival — is nearby, offering open-space recreation and, during August's festival, a genuine neighbourhood event anchor. The community sits on 17 Avenue SE, a major east-west arterial that connects to Deerfoot Trail (north-south express), the Stoney Trail ring road, and the broader east Calgary grid. New community schools and commercial services are developing in pace with Belvedere's construction phases.

Belvedere's housing stock is new construction (2020s), consisting of townhomes, front-drive detached, and laned homes by multiple builders. Tristar Communities manages the master plan; individual builder HOA covenants on newer lots should be verified before listing for STR. Separate entry suite stock is mixed — some new builds include basement suite rough-ins. The STR case rests primarily on airport proximity and East Hills Shopping Centre access, creating a year-round baseline demand from airport travellers, visiting families, and corporate visitors to east Calgary industrial and commercial zones. July Stampede provides a secondary spike. Verify any developer covenant restrictions on STR activity at the time of purchase.

Elliston Park (1827 68 Street SE) — the historic host of GlobalFest's international fireworks festival — is nearby, offering open-space recreation and, during August's festival, a genuine neighbourhood event anchor. July Stampede provides a secondary spike. Verify any developer covenant restrictions on STR activity at the time of purchase.

renter_intel.title: East Hills Shopping Centre — Adjacent and Walkable
renter_intel.description: East Hills Shopping Centre at 84 Street and 17 Avenue SE (the community's western boundary) provides walkable access to Costco, Cineplex Odeon, Walmart, a range of restaurants, and a large-format retail cluster. For guests arriving without a vehicle, the proximity of this shopping centre eliminates the most common practical friction point of remote suburban accommodation. It is also a strong provisioning hub for extended-stay families.

renter_intel.title: Calgary International Airport — 15–20 min via 17 Avenue SE
renter_intel.description: Calgary International Airport is accessible from Belvedere in approximately 15–20 minutes via 17 Avenue SE east to Barlow Trail north, or via Stoney Trail. This reliable airport proximity creates a consistent, year-round booking base from airline crew accommodation, long-connection travellers, and visiting families who prefer a full home to an airport hotel. The relatively flat 17 Avenue SE route avoids downtown congestion.

renter_intel.title: New-Build Stock — Strong Listing Photography Performance
renter_intel.description: Belvedere's 2020s new-build housing inventory features contemporary open-concept layouts, quartz countertops, luxury vinyl plank flooring, and fresh exteriors with attached or detached garages that photograph exceptionally well. Platform algorithm data consistently shows that newer, well-photographed properties achieve 20–30% higher click-through rates than comparable older stock at similar price points. Belvedere properties can lead with the new-build quality story as their primary visual differentiator.

whats_nearby.items: East Hills Shopping Centre directly adjacent to Belvedere's western boundary hosts Costco, Cineplex, Walmart, a major sport retailer, and multiple fast-food and casual dining options. The centre is Calgary's principal east-side retail hub. Walking access from Belvedere properties on 84 Street SE to the shopping centre is feasible for guests without a vehicle.

Elliston Park at 1827 68 Street SE — the historic host of GlobalFest's annual international fireworks festival — is approximately 10 minutes from Belvedere. The park provides open-space recreation with a lake, walking pathways, and community event infrastructure year-round. During GlobalFest (historically held in August), the Belvedere address provides convenient access with shuttle options available from nearby Sunridge Mall.

whats_nearby.category: Sunridge Mall and Deerfoot City — 10–15 min
whats_nearby.items: Sunridge Mall at 26 Street and 36 Avenue NE provides a traditional enclosed shopping centre approximately 10–15 minutes from Belvedere. Deerfoot City (formerly Deerfoot Meadows) at Deerfoot Trail and Glenmore Trail offers additional big-box retail, the Rec Room entertainment venue, and iFly indoor skydiving — useful activity anchors for guests seeking structured indoor entertainment.

belvedere.peak_season: Year-round stable (airport proximity, new community). Slight summer bump.
belvedere.best_property_type: Newer detached 3-4 bed within 15 minutes of YYC airport.
belvedere.stand_out: Newer 3-4 bed detached homes within 15 minutes of YYC airport.
belvedere.distinctive_fact: Belvedere is one of Calgary's newest east communities, with new-build stock that photographs well and airport proximity. BNBCalc data for newer east communities shows consistent performance.

scarboro-sunalta-west.name: Scarboro Sunalta West
scarboro-sunalta-west.subtitle: Quiet Inner-City Character With 17th Ave at Your Doorstep
scarboro-sunalta-west.str_intro:
Scarboro/Sunalta West is a compact, heritage-rich community tucked west of Crowchild Trail in Calgary's inner-city southwest, occupying the quieter residential remnant above the Beltline. Designed by landscape architect John Charles Olmsted as a "Picturesque Suburb" in Calgary's pre-WWI building boom, the area features curvilinear streets, deep-setback homes, and a dense tree canopy that distinguishes it from the grid-pattern neighbourhoods to the east. Its southern boundary sits along 17th Avenue SW — Calgary's most celebrated entertainment and dining corridor — making this one of the rare inner-city addresses where guests can walk to dozens of restaurants, bars, and boutiques in under ten minutes. The community's intimate scale and distinct character attract guests who specifically want the cultural energy of 17th Ave without the noise and density of the Beltline itself.

Daily life here centres on the walkable stretch of 17th Ave SW between 14th Street and 4th Street, lined with patios, independent restaurants, specialty coffee shops, and local retailers. Scarboro Hill rises above the commercial strip, providing a genuinely residential atmosphere where mature trees shade the streets and most homes sit on generously proportioned lots. Sunalta LRT Station is a short walk from the eastern edge of the area, providing car-free access to downtown and the CTrain network. The Calgary Tennis Club operates within the Scarboro community, and the Sunalta Elementary school grounds provide additional green space within walking distance.

The housing stock is predominantly pre-1965 character homes — a mix of Craftsman, Tudor Revival, Colonial Revival, and Ranch-style bungalows — many of which have been renovated to add legal basement suites while preserving their architectural character. With only 167 assessed properties in the Scarboro/Sunalta West area and an average assessed value of $1.34 million (2025), supply is extremely limited and the separation-entry suite inventory is mixed rather than widespread. The best STR opportunity sits in character homes with legal suites that offer private outdoor space; these attract guests who want the inner-city location but prefer a residential scale over hotel-style density. Operators should verify suite legality carefully as character-home renovations vary widely in compliance.
scarboro-sunalta-west.str_lifestyle: Daily life here centres on the walkable stretch of 17th Ave SW between 14th Street and 4th Street, lined with patios, independent restaurants, specialty coffee shops, and local retailers. Scarboro Hill rises above the commercial strip, providing a genuinely residential atmosphere where mature trees shade the streets and most homes sit on generously proportioned lots. Sunalta LRT Station is a short walk from the eastern edge of the area, providing car-free access to downtown and the CTrain network.
