The Memorial Drive corridor to the south provides rapid access to downtown Calgary by car, supplementing the transit option for guests who drive.

renter_intel.title: Quieter Than Marlborough, Same Transit Premium
renter_intel.description: Marlborough Park occupies a sweet spot in the northeast market: it benefits from the same Marlborough C-Train connection as adjacent Marlborough community but with a quieter, more park-fronting residential character. Guests who prioritise peace and neighbourhood feel over mall-adjacent convenience will rate Marlborough Park properties higher — and operators can lean into this distinction in listing copy.

renter_intel.title: Big Marlborough Park — A Genuine Green Space Differentiator
renter_intel.description: Big Marlborough Park is the community's signature open space, providing playgrounds, sports fields, and open greenery directly accessible from surrounding residential streets. For family groups, active guests, and summer travellers, park proximity translates to strong reviews and repeat bookings. Target properties backing or adjacent to the park for the strongest listing differentiation.

renter_intel.title: Memorial Drive Access Adds a Downtown Driving Route
renter_intel.description: Memorial Drive runs along the community's southern boundary and provides a fast, scenic driving route to downtown Calgary in approximately 15 minutes. Combined with the C-Train option, Marlborough Park offers guests genuine multi-mode flexibility — a meaningful value proposition for guests who travel with a car but prefer transit options for event evenings.

whats_nearby.items: Marlborough Station on 36 Street NE is within cycling or short driving distance of Marlborough Park and provides Blue Line service to downtown Calgary, the Stampede grounds, and connections to both the northeast and south legs of the C-Train system. It is the primary transit anchor for the community's STR value proposition.

The large greenspace at the heart of Marlborough Park provides sports fields, playgrounds, open lawns, and walking circuits accessible directly from neighbourhood streets. It is the community's primary recreational resource and adds meaningful outdoor amenity for families and active guests on multi-night stays.

whats_nearby.category: Memorial Drive and Bow River Pathway
whats_nearby.items: Memorial Drive along the community's southern edge connects to Calgary's Bow River pathway system, offering cyclists and pedestrians access to the river trail network running through northeast, central, and southwest Calgary. For active guests, this is a meaningful amenity addition to the standard northeast Calgary accessibility story.

marlborough-park.peak_season: Year-round stable (C-Train access, airport proximity). Slight summer bump.
marlborough-park.guest_profile: Transit-oriented travellers, airport travellers, visiting families.
marlborough-park.stand_out: Detached homes near the Marlborough C-Train station.
marlborough-park.distinctive_fact: Marlborough Park is adjacent to the Marlborough C-Train station — the same transit premium applies as in Marlborough. BNBCalc data for this NE zone shows consistent performance for well-run whole homes.

martindale.name: Martindale
martindale.subtitle: C-Train Station on the Doorstep, Genesis Centre Around the Corner — Northeast Airport Zone
martindale.str_intro:
Martindale is a well-connected northeast Calgary community established in 1983 and bounded by 80 Avenue NE (north), Falconridge Boulevard (east), 64 Avenue NE (south), and Métis Trail (west) — sitting approximately 7 kilometres from YYC International Airport and within walking distance of Martindale LRT Station on the Blue Line. The combination of a neighbourhood C-Train stop and direct airport road access makes Martindale the most transit-versatile STR node in the northeast quadrant: guests who fly in can take transit, and guests who drive can be at the airport in under 10 minutes.

The Genesis Centre community hub is the defining lifestyle anchor of Martindale, offering a full-scale recreation facility with programs for soccer, karate, badminton, roller hockey, lacrosse, and basketball alongside summer camps and rental spaces for community events. Multiple elementary and secondary schools sit within or adjacent to community boundaries, and the cricket grounds within the community are a nationally recognised amenity that speaks to the neighbourhood's South Asian diaspora character — a demographic detail that creates a welcoming environment for visiting family groups from across Canada and internationally. Saddletowne and Westwinds Corner shopping plazas provide restaurant, grocery, and service retail options within the community.

Martindale's housing stock is a mix of single-detached homes, semi-detached duplexes, and some townhome product from the 1980s and 1990s — well-suited to three-bedroom whole-home STR operations at accessible acquisition prices. The community's average home prices in the high $400,000s maintain strong yield potential for rental operators benchmarking against the $110–$190 nightly range. Properties on streets within walking distance of Martindale LRT Station represent the highest-value STR targets given the transit premium's proven effect on bookings in this market.
martindale.str_lifestyle: The Genesis Centre community hub is the defining lifestyle anchor of Martindale, offering a full-scale recreation facility with programs for soccer, karate, badminton, roller hockey, lacrosse, and basketball alongside summer camps and rental spaces for community events. Multiple elementary and secondary schools sit within or adjacent to community boundaries, and the cricket grounds within the community are a nationally recognised amenity that speaks to the neighbourhood's South Asian diaspora character — a demographic detail that creates a welcoming environment for visiting family groups from across Canada and internationally. Saddletowne and Westwinds Corner shopping plazas provide restaurant, grocery, and service retail options within the community.
martindale.str_housing: Martindale's housing stock is a mix of single-detached homes, semi-detached duplexes, and some townhome product from the 1980s and 1990s — well-suited to three-bedroom whole-home STR operations at accessible acquisition prices. The community's average home prices in the high $400,000s maintain strong yield potential for rental operators benchmarking against the $110–$190 nightly range. Properties on streets within walking distance of Martindale LRT Station represent the highest-value STR targets given the transit premium's proven effect on bookings in this market.

renter_intel.title: Martindale LRT — Rare C-Train Access in the Northeast
renter_intel.description: Martindale LRT Station on the Blue Line is one of the northeast's inner residential C-Train stops, placing guests within a 30-minute car-free ride to downtown Calgary and a direct connection to the Stampede grounds. For guests attending major city events, this transit access is a genuine premium that operators should lead with in listing copy.

renter_intel.title: Genesis Centre — Rare Community Recreation Hub
renter_intel.description: The Genesis Centre is one of Calgary's most comprehensive community recreation facilities, providing indoor sports facilities, programming, and event spaces rarely found at this scale in a residential neighbourhood. For families and active guests on multi-night stays, proximity to the Genesis Centre adds a concrete activity option that supports positive reviews and longer booking windows.

renter_intel.title: Airport in Under 10 Minutes — The Dual Advantage
renter_intel.description: At 7 kilometres from YYC airport, Martindale delivers one of the fastest airport drives in the northeast: Métis Trail or Falconridge Boulevard to Stoney Trail places the terminal in under 10 minutes under normal traffic conditions. This dual advantage — C-Train downtown plus driving access to the airport — gives Martindale the broadest guest appeal of any community in this STR tier.

whats_nearby.items: The Martindale LRT station on the Blue Line provides direct C-Train service to downtown Calgary and across the northeast corridor. Its location within the community boundaries — steps from residential streets — makes it one of the most conveniently placed C-Train stations in northeast Calgary.

The Genesis Centre is a major multi-sport and community facility offering indoor fields, gymnasiums, fitness spaces, and programming for residents of all ages. For guests on extended stays or family groups, it provides genuine recreational infrastructure well beyond what neighbouring northeast communities can offer.

whats_nearby.category: Martindale Off-Leash Park and Two Cricket Grounds
whats_nearby.items: Martindale hosts an off-leash dog park and two cricket pitches — the latter a rare and culturally significant amenity in the Calgary context that reflects the community's South Asian diaspora character. For guests who are visiting family or travelling within this cultural context, these facilities are a meaningful neighbourhood feature worth highlighting in listing copy.

pineridge.name: Pineridge
pineridge.subtitle: Part of 'The Properties' Cluster — Northeast Airport-Zone Community With Consistent Year-Round Demand
pineridge.str_intro:
Pineridge is a northeast Calgary residential community forming part of the locally-known 'Properties' cluster — alongside Whitehorn, Temple, and Rundle — four communities that share a comparable built form, price tier, and northeast plateau location east of the Calgary International Airport. The community's proximity to YYC airport via McKnight Boulevard and Stoney Trail keeps it within 15 minutes of the terminal, generating consistent year-round STR demand from airline crews, business travellers, and visiting families. Pineridge's primarily low-density residential streets deliver the quiet, full-home environment that this guest profile consistently seeks.

Pineridge benefits from the infrastructure of the Properties cluster — shared recreational amenities, pathway connections, and commercial services along the McKnight Boulevard corridor that binds the four communities together. The community association at pineridgeyyc.ca operates local programming and community events, maintaining the neighbourhood's social cohesion. Whitehorn C-Train station, just to the south on 36 Street NE, provides a transit connection to downtown Calgary that Pineridge guests can access by a short drive or bus connection, supplementing the airport-driving access that remains the primary STR demand driver in this quadrant.

Housing in Pineridge is predominantly single-detached and semi-detached 1980s-era residential stock, offering the full-bedroom, garage-included layouts that maximise STR utility for family and business travellers. The community's position within the Properties cluster means it benefits from collective amenity and retail development along McKnight Boulevard and the nearby Sunridge Mall area without the higher pricing of communities closer to the city's inner ring. Three-bedroom detached homes are the optimal property type — they accommodate multiple adult guests (airline crews, business groups, family visits) while offering the quiet residential character that drives strong reviews.
pineridge.str_lifestyle: Pineridge benefits from the infrastructure of the Properties cluster — shared recreational amenities, pathway connections, and commercial services along the McKnight Boulevard corridor that binds the four communities together. The community association at pineridgeyyc.ca operates local programming and community events, maintaining the neighbourhood's social cohesion. Whitehorn C-Train station, just to the south on 36 Street NE, provides a transit connection to downtown Calgary that Pineridge guests can access by a short drive or bus connection, supplementing the airport-driving access that remains the primary STR demand driver in this quadrant.
pineridge.str_housing: Housing in Pineridge is predominantly single-detached and semi-detached 1980s-era residential stock, offering the full-bedroom, garage-included layouts that maximise STR utility for family and business travellers. The community's position within the Properties cluster means it benefits from collective amenity and retail development along McKnight Boulevard and the nearby Sunridge Mall area without the higher pricing of communities closer to the city's inner ring. Three-bedroom detached homes are the optimal property type — they accommodate multiple adult guests (airline crews, business groups, family visits) while offering the quiet residential character that drives strong reviews.

renter_intel.title: Properties Cluster — Collective Amenity Advantage
renter_intel.description: Pineridge's membership in the Properties cluster (with Whitehorn, Temple, and Rundle) means guests can access the commercial and recreation infrastructure of the broader northeast corridor without being limited to a single community's resources. The McKnight Boulevard commercial strip, Sunridge Mall, and Whitehorn C-Train station all serve the cluster, effectively broadening the amenity base guests experience.

renter_intel.title: YYC Airport Under 15 Minutes — Stable Demand Base
renter_intel.description: McKnight Boulevard and Stoney Trail provide routing to YYC airport in under 15 minutes from Pineridge, sustaining year-round demand from airline crews, overnight airport travellers, and business guests working in the northeast logistics corridor. This airport proximity baseline flattens the seasonal demand curve and underpins consistent occupancy even in January and February.

renter_intel.title: Competitive Acquisition Pricing in the Properties Cluster
renter_intel.description: Within the Properties cluster, Pineridge typically offers competitive acquisition pricing relative to the nightly rate ceiling ($110–$190) for the northeast corridor, creating a favourable yield equation for investors. Entry-level detached three-bedroom properties in the community can be acquired at prices that support strong cash-on-cash returns at 48–65% occupancy.

whats_nearby.items: McKnight Boulevard forms the northern boundary of the Properties cluster and hosts commercial plazas, grocery anchors, fast-food and dining options, and service retail that guests use during multi-night stays. The corridor also provides fast routing to Deerfoot Trail, Stoney Trail, and the airport.

Whitehorn Station on 36 Street NE provides Blue Line C-Train access to downtown Calgary and is accessible from Pineridge by a short drive or transit connection. It was the northeast line's terminus until the 2007 extension to McKnight-Westwinds and includes 824 park-and-ride spaces — a convenient option for guests who want transit access without owning a car.

Sunridge Mall — a major enclosed retail centre — is accessible along 36 Street NE from the Properties cluster and provides full-service retail, dining, and entertainment options for guests on multi-day stays. Rundle C-Train station (adjacent to Sunridge Mall) provides an additional Blue Line access point.

rundle.name: Rundle
rundle.subtitle: Blue Line C-Train Access Plus SE Airport Proximity — Northeast's Dual-Connectivity Community
rundle.str_intro:
Rundle is a northeast Calgary community established in 1973, named for Mount Rundle near Banff and bounded by 32 Avenue NE to the north, 52 Street NE to the east, 16 Avenue NE (Trans-Canada Highway) to the south, and 36 Street NE to the west. Its position southwest of YYC International Airport delivers airport access in under 15 minutes via Barlow Trail and the Trans-Canada Highway — a dual routing that makes Rundle one of the better-connected communities for airport-demand STR operations. Rundle C-Train station on the Blue Line at 36 Street NE provides the secondary transit premium that distinguishes Rundle from most competing northeast communities.

Rundle's community infrastructure is notably well-developed for a northeast suburban neighbourhood: multiple public and separate schools at both elementary and junior high levels serve the community, including Rundle Elementary, Cecil Swanson Elementary, Dr. Gordon Higgins Junior High, St. Rupert Elementary, and St. Rose of Lima Elementary and Junior High. The Rundle Community Association at 2409 50 Street NE maintains an active outdoor skating rink, hard-surface multi-use rink for rollerblading and basketball, sports fields, and a licensed community hall with a 225-person capacity — infrastructure that reflects a functioning neighbourhood social environment rather than a purely transient residential zone.

Rundle's housing stock is predominantly low-density residential single-detached homes developed from 1973 onward, with some multi-unit residential dwellings that provide suite and secondary-suite opportunities for STR operators. The regional bike pathway system runs in and adjacent to Rundle, providing guests with active transportation connections across the northeast corridor. Three-bedroom detached properties near the 36 Street NE transit spine represent the strongest STR acquisition target — they capture both the C-Train transit premium and the airport-corridor demand that drives year-round occupancy, while remaining on quiet interior streets away from the Trans-Canada arterial noise.
rundle.str_lifestyle: Rundle's community infrastructure is notably well-developed for a northeast suburban neighbourhood: multiple public and separate schools at both elementary and junior high levels serve the community, including Rundle Elementary, Cecil Swanson Elementary, Dr. Gordon Higgins Junior High, St. Rupert Elementary, and St. Rose of Lima Elementary and Junior High. The Rundle Community Association at 2409 50 Street NE maintains an active outdoor skating rink, hard-surface multi-use rink for rollerblading and basketball, sports fields, and a licensed community hall with a 225-person capacity — infrastructure that reflects a functioning neighbourhood social environment rather than a purely transient residential zone.

The regional bike pathway system runs in and adjacent to Rundle, providing guests with active transportation connections across the northeast corridor. Three-bedroom detached properties near the 36 Street NE transit spine represent the strongest STR acquisition target — they capture both the C-Train transit premium and the airport-corridor demand that drives year-round occupancy, while remaining on quiet interior streets away from the Trans-Canada arterial noise.

renter_intel.title: C-Train Plus Airport — The Northeast Dual Advantage
renter_intel.description: Rundle's C-Train station on the Blue Line puts guests in downtown Calgary without a car, while Barlow Trail and the Trans-Canada provide a fast driving route to YYC airport (under 15 minutes). Few northeast communities combine both access modes at this price tier, and this dual connectivity should be the centrepiece of Rundle listing strategy.

renter_intel.title: Established School Cluster Drives Family-Visit Demand
renter_intel.description: Five schools operate within or immediately adjacent to Rundle's boundaries — both public and Catholic — attracting a dense residential family demographic whose visiting relatives generate consistent multi-night bookings throughout the school year. Target this segment with listing copy that emphasises neighbourhood safety, full-home layouts, and proximity to family amenities.

renter_intel.title: Hard-Surface Rink and Sports Fields — Year-Round Activity Anchor
renter_intel.description: The Rundle Community Association operates a hard-surface multi-use rink used for rollerblading, basketball, and skating depending on season, alongside outdoor sports fields. For families with active children on extended stays, this is a meaningful neighbourhood amenity that exceeds what most comparable northeast communities can offer — reference it explicitly in listing descriptions.

whats_nearby.items: Rundle Station on the Blue Line opened in 1985 and is located at 36 Street NE near Sunridge Mall. It provides direct service to downtown Calgary, Stampede Park, and connections to the full C-Train network. The station serves both Rundle community and Sunridge Mall, with 268 park-and-ride spaces available.

Sunridge Mall is immediately adjacent to the Rundle C-Train station and provides enclosed retail, dining, and service options accessible without a car from Rundle addresses. It serves as the community's primary commercial anchor for day-to-day guest needs.

Barlow Trail intersects with the Trans-Canada Highway at the community's southern edge, providing a direct north–south corridor to YYC International Airport in under 15 minutes and downtown Calgary in approximately 15 minutes by car. The regional bike pathway system in and adjacent to Rundle also connects to the broader northeast pathway network.

temple.name: Temple
temple.subtitle: NE Airport Zone With Dual C-Train Access — Whitehorn and Marlborough Stations Both Within Reach
temple.str_intro:
Temple is a northeast Calgary residential community bounded by McKnight Boulevard to the north, 68 Street E to the east, 32 Avenue NE to the south, and 52 Street E to the west — placing it within 15 minutes of YYC International Airport via McKnight Boulevard and Stoney Trail. As part of the Properties cluster alongside Rundle, Whitehorn, and Pineridge, Temple shares access to some of northeast Calgary's strongest transit infrastructure: both Whitehorn and Marlborough C-Train stations on the Blue Line are accessible by bus or car, giving guests dual C-Train options within the community's travel radius.

The Temple Community Association at templecalgary.ca runs consistent programming including monthly events, fitness classes, and community gatherings from its community centre — maintaining the neighbourhood's active social fabric. Temple's internal street network connects to Calgary's regional pathway system, offering guests cycling and walking routes through the northeast corridor. For shopping and services, the McKnight Boulevard commercial strip to the north and the Sunridge Mall area to the southwest via 36 Street NE provide full retail, grocery, and dining options within a short drive.

Temple's housing stock is predominantly single-detached and semi-detached residential from the 1980s and 1990s, offering the full-home layouts that perform best in the northeast STR market — particularly three-bedroom properties with adequate parking for airport travellers arriving by car. The community's affordable acquisition pricing relative to the $110–$190 nightly rate ceiling supports strong yield potential for investors, especially on properties positioned near McKnight Boulevard for fast airport routing. Major roads — 16 Avenue NE, Stoney Trail, and McKnight Boulevard — provide quick-access commute routing that expands Temple's appeal beyond the pure airport-corridor market to include northeast business district travellers.
temple.str_lifestyle: The Temple Community Association at templecalgary.ca runs consistent programming including monthly events, fitness classes, and community gatherings from its community centre — maintaining the neighbourhood's active social fabric. For shopping and services, the McKnight Boulevard commercial strip to the north and the Sunridge Mall area to the southwest via 36 Street NE provide full retail, grocery, and dining options within a short drive.

The community's affordable acquisition pricing relative to the $110–$190 nightly rate ceiling supports strong yield potential for investors, especially on properties positioned near McKnight Boulevard for fast airport routing. Major roads — 16 Avenue NE, Stoney Trail, and McKnight Boulevard — provide quick-access commute routing that expands Temple's appeal beyond the pure airport-corridor market to include northeast business district travellers.

renter_intel.title: Access to Both Whitehorn and Marlborough C-Train Stations
renter_intel.description: Temple's central position within the Properties cluster puts both Whitehorn Station (to the south) and Marlborough Station (to the west) within a short bus connection or drive. This gives Temple guests genuine C-Train flexibility — they can access downtown via the Blue Line in either direction, and both routes connect to the Stampede grounds. Market this dual-station access explicitly in listings targeting event and downtown travellers.

renter_intel.title: Airport via McKnight Blvd and Stoney Trail — Under 15 Minutes
renter_intel.description: McKnight Boulevard's east–west routing along Temple's northern boundary provides fast access to Stoney Trail, which routes directly to YYC airport in under 15 minutes from most Temple addresses. This is the community's primary demand driver and should anchor year-round pricing strategy regardless of event season.

renter_intel.title: Strong Family-Visit and Business Traveller Mix
renter_intel.description: Temple's demographic profile includes a strong family-oriented resident base, generating consistent visiting-family accommodation demand through school calendars and holiday periods. Combined with the airport-proximity market for business travellers and airline crews, Temple hosts a diversified guest mix that smooths seasonal fluctuations relative to single-market dependent communities.

whats_nearby.items: Whitehorn Station on 36 Street NE provides Blue Line C-Train service to downtown Calgary and the broader transit network. Located south of Temple, it is accessible by bus route 57 or a short drive, and includes 824 park-and-ride spaces. It was the northeast line terminus until 2007 and remains a major transit hub for the Properties cluster communities.

McKnight Boulevard along Temple's northern boundary hosts grocery, pharmacy, fast-food dining, and service retail accessible within minutes from any Temple address. The corridor also routes directly west to Deerfoot Trail and east to Stoney Trail, providing the airport access routing that underpins the community's STR demand profile.

whats_nearby.category: 68 Street E — East Calgary Access Route
whats_nearby.items: 68 Street E forms Temple's eastern boundary and routes southward to Stoney Trail and the east-side ring road system, providing guests with a fast connection to Applewood Park, southeast Calgary, and the Trans-Canada Highway. For guests driving in from eastern Alberta, 68 Street E is the direct route into the Temple/Abbeydale zone.

vista-heights.name: Vista Heights
vista-heights.subtitle: Compact Northeast Community at the Deerfoot-Trans-Canada Interchange — Minimal STR Competition
vista-heights.str_intro:
Vista Heights is a compact residential community in northeast Calgary, established in 1962 and uniquely positioned at the intersection of two of the city's most significant arterials: Deerfoot Trail to the west and the Trans-Canada Highway (16 Avenue NE) to the south. This placement delivers some of the fastest airport routing of any residential community in Calgary — YYC International Airport is within 10 minutes via Deerfoot Trail northbound — while the South Airways Industrial Area to the north and east reflects the community's proximity to Calgary's aviation and logistics employment corridor.

Vista Heights operates under the Crossroads Community Association (shared with Mayland Heights, at Box 37027 RPO Mayland Heights NE), reflecting the area's small resident population and the consolidated service model that characterises smaller northeast communities. The Trans-Canada Highway's proximity provides immediate access to the commercial and recreation infrastructure of the broader northeast corridor — including grocery, dining, and services along Memorial Drive and 16 Avenue NE. Nose Creek Pathway, accessible via the northeast corridor, provides cycling and walking route connections for active guests.

Vista Heights is among Calgary's smaller residential communities, with a low volume of detached residential stock concentrated in the community's interior streets away from the Deerfoot Trail and Trans-Canada Highway traffic corridors. This small supply base means active STR competition in the community will be extremely limited at any given time — a natural competitive advantage for operators who establish a presence here. Three-bedroom detached properties on the community's quieter interior streets offer the combination of airport routing speed (under 10 minutes to YYC) and full-home space that airport travellers and business guests specifically seek.

The Trans-Canada Highway's proximity provides immediate access to the commercial and recreation infrastructure of the broader northeast corridor — including grocery, dining, and services along Memorial Drive and 16 Avenue NE. Nose Creek Pathway, accessible via the northeast corridor, provides cycling and walking route connections for active guests.

Three-bedroom detached properties on the community's quieter interior streets offer the combination of airport routing speed (under 10 minutes to YYC) and full-home space that airport travellers and business guests specifically seek.

renter_intel.title: Fastest Airport Access in the Northeast Residential Market
renter_intel.description: Vista Heights' location directly at the Deerfoot Trail/Trans-Canada intersection means YYC airport is reachable in under 10 minutes northbound on Deerfoot. For airline crew contracts, cargo and logistics employees, and frequent flyers, this is an unmatched proximity advantage among Calgary's residential communities. Lead with this in all listing copy.

renter_intel.title: Minimal STR Supply — Natural Scarcity Premium
renter_intel.description: Vista Heights' small residential population means the total pool of potential STR properties is very limited. Operators who establish a well-run listing here will face minimal direct competition, allowing them to maintain occupancy rates at the upper end of the 48–65% projected band with less price pressure than larger northeast communities experience.

renter_intel.title: Business Traveller Demand From Industrial Corridor
renter_intel.description: The South Airways Industrial Area immediately north and east of Vista Heights generates a discrete demand segment of business travellers — contractors, logistics staff, aviation industry workers — who seek accommodation close to their worksites. Vista Heights' residential streets provide a quieter alternative to downtown hotels at a price point better suited to weekly or multi-night business-travel stays.

whats_nearby.category: Deerfoot Trail — YYC Airport in Under 10 Minutes
whats_nearby.items: Deerfoot Trail forms the western boundary of Vista Heights and provides a direct northbound route to YYC International Airport in under 10 minutes from the community's western streets. Southbound, Deerfoot routes to downtown Calgary in approximately 15 minutes. This is Vista Heights' defining location advantage.

The Trans-Canada Highway borders Vista Heights to the south and provides east–west connectivity across Calgary — connecting guests to Marlborough, the downtown core via Memorial Drive, and westward toward the mountains. The 16 Avenue commercial corridor includes grocery, gas, and service retail options convenient for multi-night guests.

The South Airways Industrial Area directly north and east of Vista Heights is a major employment zone serving the aviation, logistics, and light manufacturing sectors. Its proximity to Vista Heights generates a year-round business-travel demand segment that can supplement airport-corridor bookings for operators whose listings target the business travel profile.

whitehorn.name: Whitehorn
whitehorn.subtitle: Whitehorn C-Train Station — Northeast's Transit Hub With 824-Space Park-and-Ride and Airport Proximity
whitehorn.str_intro:
Whitehorn is a northeast Calgary community anchored by one of the Blue Line's most strategically located C-Train stations: Whitehorn Station on 36 Street NE, 9.8 kilometres from the City Hall Interlocking and formerly the northeast terminus of the line until its 2007 extension to McKnight-Westwinds. The station includes 824 park-and-ride spaces and serves as a connection hub for inter-city bus services to Airdrie, Edmonton, and Red Deer — a designation that signals Whitehorn's role as a genuine regional transit node rather than a simple local stop. For STR operators, this transit significance creates a guest profile that extends beyond Calgary residents to include inter-city business travellers and regional visitors.

Whitehorn is part of the Properties cluster alongside Rundle, Temple, and Pineridge — a cohesive group of northeast communities sharing recreational, commercial, and transit infrastructure along the 36 Street NE corridor. Horizon Industrial Park to the east of the station provides a business employment context for the community, generating weekday business-traveller demand from contractors and workers who prefer residential accommodation over hotel blocks. The McKnight Boulevard commercial corridor to the north provides grocery, dining, and retail services, while the Sunridge Mall area to the south completes the community's service geography.

Whitehorn's housing stock is primarily single-detached and semi-detached residential from the 1980s and 1990s, with some multi-unit residential development in proximity to the station node. Three-bedroom detached homes on quiet interior streets — within a five-to-ten-minute walk of Whitehorn Station — represent the most competitive STR acquisition targets, delivering the transit premium alongside the residential character that drives high guest satisfaction. McKnight Boulevard provides fast routing to YYC airport (under 15 minutes) for guests who need the driving option alongside the C-Train access.
whitehorn.str_lifestyle: Whitehorn is part of the Properties cluster alongside Rundle, Temple, and Pineridge — a cohesive group of northeast communities sharing recreational, commercial, and transit infrastructure along the 36 Street NE corridor. Horizon Industrial Park to the east of the station provides a business employment context for the community, generating weekday business-traveller demand from contractors and workers who prefer residential accommodation over hotel blocks. The McKnight Boulevard commercial corridor to the north provides grocery, dining, and retail services, while the Sunridge Mall area to the south completes the community's service geography.
whitehorn.str_housing: Whitehorn's housing stock is primarily single-detached and semi-detached residential from the 1980s and 1990s, with some multi-unit residential development in proximity to the station node. Three-bedroom detached homes on quiet interior streets — within a five-to-ten-minute walk of Whitehorn Station — represent the most competitive STR acquisition targets, delivering the transit premium alongside the residential character that drives high guest satisfaction. McKnight Boulevard provides fast routing to YYC airport (under 15 minutes) for guests who need the driving option alongside the C-Train access.

renter_intel.title: 824-Space Park-and-Ride — The Guest Logistics Advantage
renter_intel.description: Whitehorn Station's 824-space park-and-ride is one of the largest on the Blue Line northeast leg. For guests who drive to Calgary but want transit access to downtown, this arrangement eliminates the need to navigate downtown parking. Operators should highlight this in listing copy — it is a concrete logistical benefit that converts undecided guests when they compare Whitehorn listings to alternatives without transit access.

renter_intel.title: Inter-City Bus Hub Expands Guest Origin Market
renter_intel.description: Whitehorn Station connects inter-city bus services running to Airdrie, Red Deer, and Edmonton — meaning guests travelling between Calgary and other Alberta cities can use Whitehorn as their transit hub without downtown hotel pricing. This discrete demand segment is not captured by pure airport-proximity marketing and should be addressed with listing copy targeting road-trip and multi-city Alberta travellers.

renter_intel.title: Properties Cluster — Shared Commercial Infrastructure
renter_intel.description: As part of the Properties cluster, Whitehorn benefits from collective commercial development along 36 Street NE and the McKnight Boulevard corridor. Guests can access grocery, dining, and services in the Sunridge Mall area to the south and the McKnight commercial strip to the north — a convenience profile that supports multi-night stays without requiring car-dependent errands.

whats_nearby.category: Whitehorn C-Train Station — Transit and Inter-City Hub
whats_nearby.items: Whitehorn Station at 36 Street NE provides Blue Line C-Train service to downtown Calgary, Stampede Park, and connections across the system. With 824 park-and-ride spaces and inter-city bus connections to Airdrie, Red Deer, and Edmonton, it is the northeast's most significant transit hub for both local and regional travellers.

Adjacent to Whitehorn Station on the east side of 36 Street NE, Horizon Industrial Park is a substantial employment zone generating business-travel demand from contractors, logistics workers, and corporate visitors who prefer quiet residential accommodation near their worksites over downtown hotel pricing.

whats_nearby.category: Sunridge Mall and Rundle C-Train Station
whats_nearby.items: Sunridge Mall is accessible south on 36 Street NE from Whitehorn and provides enclosed retail, dining, and services for guest daily needs. Rundle Station (adjacent to Sunridge) offers an additional C-Train access point on the same Blue Line for guests preferring a shorter walk from certain Whitehorn streets.

chaparral.name: Chaparral
chaparral.subtitle: Private Lake Living in SE Calgary — 32-Acre Man-Made Lake With Year-Round Recreation
chaparral.str_intro:
Chaparral is a southeast Calgary residential community established in 1995 and built around a 32-acre man-made lake that serves as the community's defining amenity — one of Calgary's original and most established private lake communities. The lake complex includes a 21-acre park with two waterfalls, a full-time recreation director, and managed facilities for swimming, boating, fishing, kayaking, paddleboarding, and winter skating — all governed by the Lake Chaparral Residents Association (LCRA) and accessible exclusively to homeowners who hold valid lake access membership. For STR operators, this lake access is the primary listing differentiator and the driver of strong June–August bookings.

Chaparral occupies the far southeast of Calgary, bounded by Stoney Trail to the north, the Bow River valley to the east, 194 Avenue SE to the south, and Macleod Trail to the west — with easy connections to Deerfoot Trail for citywide access and to the South Health Campus hospital complex in adjacent Seton. Fish Creek Provincial Park borders the community's eastern edge, adding 2,300 acres of urban park and pathway to the lifestyle equation alongside the private lake amenity. The nearby Gates of Walden shopping district, Shawnessy Town Centre, and the Seton commercial area provide restaurants, grocery, and retail within a short drive, while Blue Devil Golf Club along the Bow River rounds out the outdoor recreation profile.

Chaparral's housing stock spans single-family detached homes, some backed directly onto the lake or park system, through to standard interior lots, with pricing reflecting the lake access premium. Properties with direct lake-view or lake-access backing command both higher acquisition prices and significantly stronger STR nightly rates — the lake experience is the product, and guests booking in summer specifically seek properties that deliver it. Investors must verify that any target property carries active lake access membership through the LCRA before listing; properties without confirmed lake privileges cannot credibly market the community's core differentiator and will underperform against the $130–$215 nightly rate band.

Fish Creek Provincial Park borders the community's eastern edge, adding 2,300 acres of urban park and pathway to the lifestyle equation alongside the private lake amenity. The nearby Gates of Walden shopping district, Shawnessy Town Centre, and the Seton commercial area provide restaurants, grocery, and retail within a short drive, while Blue Devil Golf Club along the Bow River rounds out the outdoor recreation profile.

renter_intel.title: Lake Access Verification Is a Pre-Purchase Requirement
renter_intel.description: Lake Chaparral Residents Association (LCRA) manages all lake access privileges through annual membership fees (approximately $350 per year per household). Not all Chaparral properties automatically include lake access — the access right must be verified through the LCRA before listing. Properties without confirmed lake access should be priced and marketed on their Fish Creek proximity alone, which reduces yield potential meaningfully versus lake-access listings.

renter_intel.title: Summer Bookings Peak in June–August — Dynamic Pricing Is Essential
renter_intel.description: Chaparral Lake's swimming, boating, and beach experience drives a pronounced June–August peak that can support nightly rates well above the $130–$215 band floor for lake-access properties. Operators using dynamic pricing tools should set an aggressive summer ceiling — guests who want a lake experience in Calgary without driving to Canmore will pay a meaningful premium. The winter skating and ice fishing season provides a secondary, smaller demand bump from January through February.

renter_intel.title: Fish Creek Access for Non-Lake-Fronting Properties
renter_intel.description: Interior Chaparral properties that do not carry direct lake access can still market proximity to the Fish Creek Provincial Park trail system, which enters the community via the Bow River valley on the eastern edge. This is a weaker differentiator than lake access but still positions listings above generic suburban southeast Calgary alternatives for outdoor-active guest segments.

whats_nearby.items: The 32-acre man-made Lake Chaparral and adjacent 21-acre park are the community's centrepiece, managed by the Lake Chaparral Residents Association at lakechap.ca. Facilities include swimming areas, boat launches for kayaks and canoes, paddleboard use, fishing, and winter skating. Summer programming is managed by a full-time recreation director.

whats_nearby.category: Fish Creek Provincial Park — Eastern Edge Access
whats_nearby.items: Fish Creek Provincial Park borders Chaparral's eastern edge via the Bow River valley, providing trail access to 2,300 acres of urban parkland. Pathways connect to the park's broader system running through south and southwest Calgary, offering hiking, cycling, and wildlife viewing options accessible within minutes of most Chaparral addresses.

whats_nearby.category: South Health Campus and Seton Urban District
whats_nearby.items: The South Health Campus — one of Calgary's largest hospital complexes — is located in adjacent Seton, a short drive from Chaparral. The Seton urban district is also home to a major YMCA, Cineplex theatre, grocery anchors, and an expanding commercial corridor that provides guests with full-service urban amenities within the south Calgary corridor.

chaparral.best_property_type: Detached home with Chaparral Lake access.
chaparral.stand_out: Detached homes with Chaparral Lake access. 3+ bedrooms for family groups.
chaparral.distinctive_fact: Chaparral Lake is one of Calgary's original private lake communities. Properties with lake access see strong summer bookings, but the community is well-established and competition among lake-access listings is higher than in newer lake communities.

evergreen.name: Evergreen
evergreen.subtitle: Fish Creek Provincial Park on the Doorstep — SW Calgary With Direct Trailhead Access
evergreen.str_intro:
Evergreen is a southwest Calgary residential community with a defining geographical advantage: Fish Creek Provincial Park — one of Canada's largest urban provincial parks at approximately 2,300 acres — borders the community directly to the north and east. Established in two phases (Evergreen Estates from 1986 and the newer Evergreen from 1999), the community offers a mature streetscape in the north section and contemporary homes in the south, with the park system accessible from multiple trailhead entry points within walking distance of most properties. This park adjacency is the primary STR differentiator for Evergreen and the lead asset in any listing strategy.

Evergreen's southern position in Calgary's southwest — bounded by Fish Creek Provincial Park (north), 162 Avenue SW (south), 37 Street SW (west), and Fish Creek Boulevard through to James McKevitt Road (east) — gives residents and guests quick access to Macleod Trail and Stoney Trail for city-wide connectivity. The Spruce Meadows equestrian complex, internationally recognised for its show-jumping events, sits just south of the community and provides a distinctive seasonal event draw. Shawnessy Town Centre, Evergreen Village, and the broader Shawnessy commercial corridor provide extensive dining, grocery, and entertainment options within a short drive, while CardelRec South offers year-round fitness and swimming programming nearby.

Evergreen's housing stock ranges from entry-level townhomes and condos through to three- and four-bedroom detached homes on mature lots, with Evergreen Estates' 1990s-era properties tending toward larger lots with established tree cover. The most compelling STR acquisition targets are detached homes in Evergreen Estates that back or directly access Fish Creek Provincial Park — these properties can market genuine park views and immediate trail access as premium listing features that distinguish them from the broader south Calgary STR market. Legal suite configurations exist in the community and provide an alternative to whole-home operations for investors seeking different risk profiles.
evergreen.str_lifestyle: Evergreen's southern position in Calgary's southwest — bounded by Fish Creek Provincial Park (north), 162 Avenue SW (south), 37 Street SW (west), and Fish Creek Boulevard through to James McKevitt Road (east) — gives residents and guests quick access to Macleod Trail and Stoney Trail for city-wide connectivity. The Spruce Meadows equestrian complex, internationally recognised for its show-jumping events, sits just south of the community and provides a distinctive seasonal event draw. Shawnessy Town Centre, Evergreen Village, and the broader Shawnessy commercial corridor provide extensive dining, grocery, and entertainment options within a short drive, while CardelRec South offers year-round fitness and swimming programming nearby.

Legal suite configurations exist in the community and provide an alternative to whole-home operations for investors seeking different risk profiles.

renter_intel.title: Park-Backing Properties — The Premium Acquisition Target
renter_intel.description: Homes backing onto Fish Creek Provincial Park in Evergreen Estates command the strongest STR yields in the community. The ability to step from the back yard directly onto Fish Creek trail system is a powerful listing narrative for outdoor-active guests, families, and nature enthusiasts visiting Calgary. These properties photograph exceptionally well and justify pricing at the $130–$215 upper band.

renter_intel.title: Year-Round Trail Access Extends Seasonality
renter_intel.description: Fish Creek Provincial Park's trail network operates year-round — summer hiking and cycling, autumn foliage walks, and winter cross-country skiing and snowshoeing all generate guest interest outside the typical June–August peak. Operators who market park trail access as a four-season amenity can extend booking windows beyond the summer peak that dominates south Calgary STR seasonality.

renter_intel.title: Spruce Meadows Event Draw
renter_intel.description: Spruce Meadows hosts multiple major equestrian events annually including the Masters tournament, attracting international competitors, spectators, and corporate guests. Evergreen's proximity to Spruce Meadows — a short drive south — creates a secondary event-driven booking segment layered over the park-access primary demand. These bookings tend to be multi-night and command premium nightly rates.

whats_nearby.category: Fish Creek Provincial Park — Direct Trailhead Access
whats_nearby.items: Fish Creek Provincial Park borders Evergreen's northern and eastern edges, providing immediate access to approximately 2,300 acres of urban parkland with paved and gravel trails for hiking, cycling, and wildlife watching. Multiple trailhead entry points are accessible within walking distance of Evergreen Estates addresses.

Spruce Meadows, located just south of Evergreen, is internationally recognized for its show-jumping events and annually draws competitors and spectators from across the world. Major events including the Masters and Spruce Meadows Nationals generate discrete accommodation demand blocks that Evergreen operators can capture with appropriate dynamic pricing.

Shawnessy Town Centre is a major retail and lifestyle hub west of Evergreen along Shawnessy Boulevard SW, offering major grocery anchors, restaurants, coffee shops, fitness facilities, and a Landmark Cinemas location. It is the closest full-service commercial hub for Evergreen guests and covers all daily convenience needs.

evergreen.peak_season: June–August (Fish Creek Provincial Park proximity). Long weekends.
evergreen.best_property_type: Detached home near Fish Creek Provincial Park.
evergreen.guest_profile: Families visiting south Calgary, outdoor enthusiasts.
evergreen.stand_out: Homes backing onto Fish Creek Provincial Park with legal suites or separate-entry configurations.
evergreen.distinctive_fact: Evergreen borders Fish Creek Provincial Park — properties with park access or backing are the primary STR differentiator in this community.

mckenzie-towne.name: Mckenzie Towne
mckenzie-towne.subtitle: Neo-Traditional Village Design in SE Calgary — High Street, Front Porches, and Walkable Character
mckenzie-towne.str_intro:
McKenzie Towne is a master-planned neotraditional community in southeast Calgary, designed from the ground up to feel like a small town within the city — complete with front porches, narrow lots, alley-accessed garages, street-facing retail, and a pedestrian-oriented High Street commercial village at 20 McKenzie Towne Avenue SE. Bounded by Deerfoot Trail to the west, Stoney Trail to the south, 52 Street SE to the east, and the 130 Avenue SE commercial corridor to the north, the community offers an intentional departure from standard Calgary suburban design that translates directly into a differentiated STR guest experience. Listings that market McKenzie Towne's architectural character and walkable village access outperform generic suburban listings in the same price tier.

McKenzie Towne's High Street is the community's social and commercial spine — a traditional main-street commercial strip anchored by independent cafés, restaurants, boutiques, and service businesses that creates a walkable neighbourhood experience rare in Calgary's southeast. The 302 Bus Rapid Transit (BRT) route connects McKenzie Towne to downtown Calgary, supplementing the community's road access via Deerfoot Trail. Fish Creek Provincial Park is accessible from the community's northern edge, adding trail access to the guest experience alongside the village character. The 130th Avenue commercial corridor immediately north provides the big-box retail, restaurant chains, and entertainment anchors (including the major retail power centre) that residents use for larger shopping trips.

McKenzie Towne's architectural diversity — spanning single-family detached homes with traditional front porches, semi-detached properties, townhomes, apartments, and seniors care facilities — creates a varied STR opportunity set. For the strongest yields, target detached properties near the High Street commercial village: these listings can emphasise walkable dining, the neighbourhood's distinct architectural character, and the community's story as Calgary's leading neotraditional development. Properties with front porch design features photograph exceptionally well and produce distinctive listing visuals that stand out in the southeast Calgary STR market.

The 302 Bus Rapid Transit (BRT) route connects McKenzie Towne to downtown Calgary, supplementing the community's road access via Deerfoot Trail. Fish Creek Provincial Park is accessible from the community's northern edge, adding trail access to the guest experience alongside the village character. The 130th Avenue commercial corridor immediately north provides the big-box retail, restaurant chains, and entertainment anchors (including the major retail power centre) that residents use for larger shopping trips.

Properties with front porch design features photograph exceptionally well and produce distinctive listing visuals that stand out in the southeast Calgary STR market.

renter_intel.title: Marketing the Character, Not Just the Location
renter_intel.description: McKenzie Towne's built form is its most powerful STR differentiator. Listings that lead with the village-style High Street, the front-porch streetscapes, and the walkable neighbourhood design will consistently outperform generic suburban listings in the same price tier. Guest reviews for McKenzie Towne consistently reference the neighbourhood's character as a positive — this is an earned authenticity that should drive listing copy, not just photography.

renter_intel.title: High Street Walkability — Dining Without a Car
renter_intel.description: The High Street commercial village at 20 McKenzie Towne Avenue SE puts independent restaurants, cafés, and services within walking distance of most community properties. For guests who do not want to drive for every meal — a common preference among travelling couples and short-break guests — this walkable dining access is a meaningful booking driver and a reliable source of five-star guest reviews.

renter_intel.title: Deerfoot Trail Access — Fast City Routing
renter_intel.description: Deerfoot Trail on McKenzie Towne's western boundary provides fast north–south routing to downtown Calgary (approximately 20–25 minutes), the Stampede grounds, and cross-city connections. Combined with the 302 BRT route, McKenzie Towne guests have genuine multi-mode access to Calgary's broader event and commercial landscape without being dependent on private vehicle transport.

whats_nearby.items: The High Street at 20 McKenzie Towne Avenue SE is the community's pedestrian-oriented commercial village — a traditional main street with independent cafés, restaurants, boutiques, clinics, and service businesses. It is the defining lifestyle amenity of the community and should be featured prominently in listing copy and photography for properties within walking distance.

The 130 Avenue SE retail power centre immediately north of McKenzie Towne provides big-box retail anchors, restaurant chains, grocery, entertainment (including cinema), and full-service commercial infrastructure. It handles the high-volume shopping needs that complement the village-scale High Street experience.

Fish Creek Provincial Park is accessible from McKenzie Towne's northern edge, adding 2,300 acres of urban parkland and trail access to the community's lifestyle proposition. The Bow Valley Ranch Visitor Centre and Sikome Lake aquatic facility within the park are accessible within a short drive for outdoor-active guests.

mckenzie-towne.peak_season: June–August (family-friendly character, proximity to Fish Creek). Long weekends.
mckenzie-towne.best_property_type: Detached home near the McKenzie Towne commercial village.
mckenzie-towne.guest_profile: Families visiting south Calgary, guests attending events at the McKenzie Towne commercial village.
mckenzie-towne.stand_out: Homes near the McKenzie Towne commercial village with legal suites or separate-entry configurations.
mckenzie-towne.distinctive_fact: McKenzie Towne's neo-traditional design — walkable village centre, front porches, narrow lots — creates a guest experience that feels different from standard Calgary suburbs. Listings that market this character see stronger bookings than generic suburban listings.

sundance.name: Sundance
sundance.subtitle: Private 33-Acre Lake in South Calgary — Lakeside Resort Living With Fish Creek on the Border
sundance.str_intro:
Sundance is a south Calgary residential community built around a 33-acre man-made private lake managed by the Sundance Lake Residents Association (SLRA) — one of Calgary's most celebrated private lake communities and, by some resident accounts, the most visually striking of the city's lake neighbourhoods. Established in the early 1980s and positioned east of Macleod Trail with Fish Creek Provincial Park bordering it to the north and east, Sundance offers guests a rare dual lifestyle asset: a private beach and lake for summer recreation, and immediate access to one of Canada's largest urban provincial parks. This combination drives the community's pronounced June–August STR peak and supports the upper end of the $130–$215 nightly rate band for lake-access properties.

The SLRA actively manages the 33-acre Lake Sundance with a full suite of summer amenities — swimming, paddleboarding, sailing, kayaking, fishing, disc golf, tennis and pickleball courts, basketball courts, volleyball courts, and soccer pitches — plus winter skating, ice fishing, and holiday events. The lake office operates seasonally with monitoring of water temperature and ice conditions, maintaining a resort-level standard of amenity management that is directly reflected in the guest experience for STR listings that carry lake access. Shawnessy Town Centre to the west provides full-service retail, restaurant, and entertainment infrastructure, while Southcentre Mall and the South Health Campus at Seton are accessible via Macleod Trail within 10 minutes.

Sundance's housing stock consists primarily of well-maintained single-family homes on mature lots with established tree cover and some properties fronting directly onto the lake. Lake-fronting and lake-view properties command significant STR premiums in summer, when the lake experience is the product. Operators must verify active SLRA membership for any target property before listing — guests arriving to find they cannot access the lake will generate negative reviews regardless of property quality. Properties without lake access can still leverage Fish Creek Provincial Park's trail network from the community's eastern boundary as an alternative outdoor amenity, though this is a meaningfully weaker positioning than lake access.
sundance.str_lifestyle: The SLRA actively manages the 33-acre Lake Sundance with a full suite of summer amenities — swimming, paddleboarding, sailing, kayaking, fishing, disc golf, tennis and pickleball courts, basketball courts, volleyball courts, and soccer pitches — plus winter skating, ice fishing, and holiday events. The lake office operates seasonally with monitoring of water temperature and ice conditions, maintaining a resort-level standard of amenity management that is directly reflected in the guest experience for STR listings that carry lake access. Shawnessy Town Centre to the west provides full-service retail, restaurant, and entertainment infrastructure, while Southcentre Mall and the South Health Campus at Seton are accessible via Macleod Trail within 10 minutes.

Lake-fronting and lake-view properties command significant STR premiums in summer, when the lake experience is the product. Operators must verify active SLRA membership for any target property before listing — guests arriving to find they cannot access the lake will generate negative reviews regardless of property quality. Properties without lake access can still leverage Fish Creek Provincial Park's trail network from the community's eastern boundary as an alternative outdoor amenity, though this is a meaningfully weaker positioning than lake access.

renter_intel.title: SLRA Lake Membership — Verify Before Purchase
renter_intel.description: The Sundance Lake Residents Association strictly controls lake access through membership, which is property-tied but must be maintained current through annual fees. Guest access rules allow up to 12 guests per household per day (member must be present), which accommodates group stays but requires STR operators to understand guest logistics. Verify SLRA membership status before any acquisition and confirm the extent of guest access rights applicable to non-owner operators.

renter_intel.title: Summer Peak June–August — The Revenue Concentration Opportunity
renter_intel.description: Sundance Lake's swimming, boating, disc golf, tennis, pickleball, and beach experience creates a concentrated June–August STR revenue opportunity. Dynamic pricing during peak summer — particularly long weekends, Stampede week, and August — can materially increase annual yield on a lake-access property. Operators should set rate floors consistent with the premium lake-access positioning and not discount below the market mid-point during peak weeks.

renter_intel.title: Fish Creek Access Supplements Off-Season Bookings
renter_intel.description: Fish Creek Provincial Park's trail network borders Sundance to the north and east, providing year-round outdoor access that supports shoulder-season and winter bookings from hikers, cyclists, and families seeking an active base in south Calgary. The park's winter trail system (snowshoeing, cross-country skiing) is a secondary marketing angle for November–February periods when the lake is in low-activity mode.

whats_nearby.items: Managed by the Sundance Lake Residents Association at lakesundance.org, the 33-acre Lake Sundance provides swimming, paddleboarding, sailing, kayaking, fishing, disc golf, tennis, pickleball, volleyball, and basketball facilities seasonally, with winter skating and ice fishing in the colder months. It is the community's defining lifestyle and STR differentiator.

Fish Creek Provincial Park shares Sundance's northern and eastern boundaries, providing direct trail access to 2,300 acres of urban parkland. The Sikome Lake aquatic facility within the park (a public lake and beach) offers a free supplementary aquatic recreation option for guests and residents beyond the private lake.

Shawnessy Town Centre is a major retail and commercial hub directly west of Sundance along Shawnessy Boulevard SW, with grocery anchors, restaurants, a Landmark Cinemas location, fitness centres, and the Shawnessy YMCA. It handles all daily commercial needs for Sundance residents and guests within minutes.

sundance.best_property_type: Detached home with Sundance Lake access.
sundance.stand_out: Detached homes with Sundance Lake access. 3+ bedrooms for family groups.
sundance.distinctive_fact: Sundance Lake is a private lake community — properties with lake access see strong summer bookings from families who want a lake experience without driving to Banff.

midnapore.name: Midnapore
midnapore.subtitle: One of Calgary's Original Lake Communities — Fish Creek on the Doorstep, Private 30-Acre Lake
midnapore.str_intro:
Midnapore holds a distinctive position in Calgary's south residential market as one of the city's original private lake communities — the 12-hectare (approximately 30-acre) Midnapore Lake was dredged in 1976 and has been a resident amenity for half a century. Bounded by Fish Creek Provincial Park to the north and east, Sun Valley Boulevard to the south, and Macleod Trail to the west, Midnapore combines private lake access with direct Fish Creek park adjacency — a dual outdoor-amenity profile that no other single south Calgary community matches. Some properties back directly onto the lake while others border onto Fish Creek, creating distinct premium lot categories within the community.

Midnapore's lake is a genuine four-season amenity: in summer, it supports swimming, boating, and fishing; in winter, a dedicated skating circuit is maintained around the lake, and ice fishing and tobogganing draw both residents and visiting guests to the park adjacent to the lake. The Midnapore Community Association manages lake programming alongside a broader year-round events calendar, and drone-assisted ice condition monitoring ensures guest safety — a level of operational detail that reflects the mature community management infrastructure. Macleod Trail to the west provides immediate access to Shawnessy Town Centre, the Fish Creek–Lacombe C-Train station, and the broader Macleod Trail commercial corridor for daily shopping and dining needs.

Midnapore's housing stock is predominantly single-detached and is classified as 'Rare' for separate-entry configurations in the source data — meaning STR operators will primarily find whole-home detached opportunities here rather than legal suite options. Properties backing onto the lake or Fish Creek park system command the strongest nightly rates and occupancy in the community, while interior lots still benefit from the general community premium of lake access membership. The lake maintenance is funded by property encumbrances rather than optional association fees — confirm with a solicitor that any target property's encumbrance is current and includes lake access rights.
midnapore.str_lifestyle: Midnapore's lake is a genuine four-season amenity: in summer, it supports swimming, boating, and fishing; in winter, a dedicated skating circuit is maintained around the lake, and ice fishing and tobogganing draw both residents and visiting guests to the park adjacent to the lake. The Midnapore Community Association manages lake programming alongside a broader year-round events calendar, and drone-assisted ice condition monitoring ensures guest safety — a level of operational detail that reflects the mature community management infrastructure. Macleod Trail to the west provides immediate access to Shawnessy Town Centre, the Fish Creek–Lacombe C-Train station, and the broader Macleod Trail commercial corridor for daily shopping and dining needs.

Properties backing onto the lake or Fish Creek park system command the strongest nightly rates and occupancy in the community, while interior lots still benefit from the general community premium of lake access membership. The lake maintenance is funded by property encumbrances rather than optional association fees — confirm with a solicitor that any target property's encumbrance is current and includes lake access rights.

renter_intel.title: Lake Backed by a 50-Year Management Track Record
renter_intel.description: Unlike newer lake communities where management quality is still being established, Midnapore's lake has been in continuous operation since 1976 with mature infrastructure, dedicated winter programming, and drone-assisted ice safety monitoring. This operational depth means operators can market the lake experience with confidence and expect consistent amenity quality that supports strong reviews across seasons.

renter_intel.title: Property Encumbrance Covers Lake Maintenance — Not Optional
renter_intel.description: Midnapore lake maintenance is funded through encumbrances on property titles rather than optional association memberships, meaning the cost is built into property ownership rather than requiring annual sign-up. For STR operators, this provides certainty that lake access is maintained — but requires legal confirmation that any target property's encumbrance is active before listing.

renter_intel.title: Fish Creek Backs Some Lots — Dual Amenity Premium
renter_intel.description: Northern Midnapore properties border Fish Creek Provincial Park directly, meaning some homes offer both lake access and Fish Creek park proximity from the same address. These dual-amenity lots command the community's highest STR premiums and should be prioritised in acquisition searches — they combine the summer lake experience with year-round trail access for the broadest guest appeal.

whats_nearby.items: The 12-hectare (30-acre) Midnapore Lake has been the community's core amenity since 1976, managed by the Midnapore Community Association with swimming, boating, fishing, winter skating, and ice fishing programming. A toboggan hill adjacent to the lake and a skating circuit around the lake provide structured winter recreation beyond the summer swimming season.

Fish Creek Provincial Park borders Midnapore to the north and east, providing trail access to 2,300 acres of urban parkland. Several Midnapore properties back directly onto the park, delivering immediate trailhead access without requiring a drive to a separate park entrance.

Macleod Trail to the west of Midnapore provides access to Shawnessy Town Centre, the Fish Creek–Lacombe C-Train station (Red Line), and the broader south Calgary commercial strip. Guests on multi-night stays can commute downtown by C-Train from Fish Creek–Lacombe in under 20 minutes.

midnapore.best_property_type: Detached home with Midnapore Lake access.
midnapore.stand_out: Detached homes with Midnapore Lake access. 3+ bedrooms for family groups.
midnapore.distinctive_fact: Midnapore Lake is one of Calgary's original private lake communities — properties with lake access see strong summer bookings.

shawnee-slopes.name: Shawnee Slopes
shawnee-slopes.subtitle: Fish Creek Trailhead Access and C-Train — SW Calgary's Nature-Transit Combination
shawnee-slopes.str_intro:
Shawnee Slopes is a southwest Calgary residential community occupying the corridor between Fish Creek Provincial Park to the north and James McKevitt Road to the south, with Macleod Trail forming the eastern boundary and Evergreen Street SW to the west. The community's primary STR differentiator is a combination that is rare in this price tier: direct Fish Creek Provincial Park access on the northern boundary and walkable proximity to the Fish Creek–Lacombe C-Train station (Red Line) on the eastern edge. This nature-transit pairing creates a guest profile that spans outdoor enthusiasts, downtown event travellers, and families seeking a quiet south Calgary base.

Fish Creek Provincial Park runs along Shawnee Slopes' entire northern edge, providing approximately 19 kilometres of trails within the park system alongside dozens of pathway connections for cycling, hiking, dog-walking, and wildlife viewing — a genuinely world-class urban natural asset accessible on foot from most community addresses. The Fish Creek–Lacombe C-Train station at the community's eastern edge provides Red Line service to downtown Calgary in under 20 minutes, while Macleod Trail connects southward to Shawnessy Town Centre, CardelRec South, the Shawnessy YMCA, and a Landmark Cinemas location. Blue Devil Golf Club along the Bow River and the Sikome Lake aquatic facility within Fish Creek Provincial Park complete the outdoor recreation picture.

Shawnee Slopes' housing stock is primarily single-detached, with a combination of mature 1980s and 1990s homes on the community's established streets and newer development from the Shawnee Park phase (Cardel Homes) on the community's southern streets. Properties on the northern streets backing onto Fish Creek Provincial Park deliver the most compelling STR experience — direct park access from the rear of the property is a premium listing feature that drives both nightly rate and occupancy. Source data classifies separate-entry stock as 'Rare,' meaning operators should focus on whole-home detached listings, which are the community's primary STR product type.

The Fish Creek–Lacombe C-Train station at the community's eastern edge provides Red Line service to downtown Calgary in under 20 minutes, while Macleod Trail connects southward to Shawnessy Town Centre, CardelRec South, the Shawnessy YMCA, and a Landmark Cinemas location. Blue Devil Golf Club along the Bow River and the Sikome Lake aquatic facility within Fish Creek Provincial Park complete the outdoor recreation picture.

Properties on the northern streets backing onto Fish Creek Provincial Park deliver the most compelling STR experience — direct park access from the rear of the property is a premium listing feature that drives both nightly rate and occupancy. Source data classifies separate-entry stock as 'Rare,' meaning operators should focus on whole-home detached listings, which are the community's primary STR product type.

renter_intel.title: Park-Backing Lots — The Premium Target
renter_intel.description: Homes backing onto Fish Creek Provincial Park are Shawnee Slopes' highest-performing STR properties. The ability to walk directly from the property into the park — past a private gate or through a rear access point — is a feature that generates disproportionate guest interest and supports nightly rates at the top of the $130–$215 band. Target park-backing lots explicitly in acquisition searches and factor the park-access premium into valuation models.

renter_intel.title: Fish Creek–Lacombe C-Train Extends Guest Appeal
renter_intel.description: The Fish Creek–Lacombe C-Train station (Red Line) is within walking distance of parts of Shawnee Slopes — 5 to 20 minutes depending on the specific address. This transit access places guests downtown in under 20 minutes without a car, making Shawnee Slopes competitive for Stampede week, downtown event bookings, and business travellers who prefer a quiet residential base over downtown accommodation.

renter_intel.title: Shawnee Park New Development — Modern Stock in the Same Zone
renter_intel.description: Cardel Homes' Shawnee Park development within the Shawnee Slopes area provides newer-build detached homes and paired homes on a 20-metre greenbelt, with Fish Creek park access within a 10–15-minute walk and the C-Train station 4 minutes by car. For investors who prefer newer construction with modern layouts, Shawnee Park inventory represents an alternative to the community's older housing stock.

whats_nearby.category: Fish Creek Provincial Park — Northern Boundary
whats_nearby.items: Fish Creek Provincial Park runs along Shawnee Slopes' northern edge, providing approximately 19 km of trails within the accessible park segment and connections to the broader 2,300-acre park system. Multiple residential trailheads allow residents and guests to enter the park directly from neighbourhood streets, eliminating the need to drive to a park entrance.

The Fish Creek–Lacombe C-Train station sits at the eastern edge of Shawnee Slopes and provides Red Line service to downtown Calgary in under 20 minutes. For guests who want a quiet south Calgary base with event-night transit access, this station is the key differentiator that positions Shawnee Slopes above most south Calgary alternatives.

whats_nearby.category: Shawnessy Town Centre and CardelRec South
whats_nearby.items: Shawnessy Town Centre provides major grocery anchors, restaurants, a Landmark Cinemas, and service retail within a short drive west of Shawnee Slopes. CardelRec South adjacent to the shopping area offers a full recreation centre with pool, fitness, and sports programming, supported by the Shawnessy YMCA nearby.

shawnee-slopes.peak_season: June–August (Fish Creek Provincial Park proximity). Long weekends.
shawnee-slopes.best_property_type: Detached home near Fish Creek Provincial Park.
shawnee-slopes.guest_profile: Families visiting south Calgary, outdoor enthusiasts.
shawnee-slopes.stand_out: Homes backing onto Fish Creek Provincial Park.
shawnee-slopes.distinctive_fact: Shawnee Slopes backs onto Fish Creek Provincial Park — properties with park access are the primary STR differentiator in this community.

schools.name: Dr. E. P. Scarlett High School

applewood-park.name: Applewood Park
applewood-park.subtitle: East Calgary Airport Corridor — Elliston Park on the Doorstep, YYC in 15 Minutes
applewood-park.str_intro:
Applewood Park is a residential community on Calgary's eastern edge, established in 1982 and bounded by the Trans-Canada Highway (16 Avenue) to the north, the city's east boundary to the east, 17 Avenue SE to the south, and 68 Street to the west — approximately 10 kilometres from downtown Calgary via either 17 Avenue SE or Memorial Drive. Its east-edge positioning places it within 15 minutes of YYC International Airport via 16 Avenue NE and Stoney Trail, establishing it firmly within the airport-corridor demand zone that sustains consistent year-round STR occupancy. A distinctive local landmark — Elliston Park, the site of Calgary's annual GlobalFest international fireworks festival — sits immediately southwest of the community and provides a genuine event-booking anchor not shared by other east Calgary communities.

Elliston Park's GlobalFest fireworks festival — a major annual event typically held over multiple evenings in August — drives a concentrated burst of accommodation demand for Applewood Park listings that are within walking or cycling distance of the venue. For STR operators, this event represents a high-yield booking window to target with dynamic pricing well above the community's standard $110–$185 nightly band. Beyond GlobalFest, Elliston Park functions as a year-round green space and lake feature for the community, providing recreational access that supports multi-season listing appeal. Memorial Drive and 17 Avenue SE connect guests to downtown Calgary in approximately 20 minutes by car, while the Trans-Canada provides fast routing to the airport.

Applewood Park's housing stock is primarily single-detached and semi-detached residential developed in the 1980s and early 1990s, with an owner-occupancy rate of approximately 88.6% — meaning the available STR supply pool will be limited and dominated by whole-home listings rather than suites or secondary units. This natural scarcity is a competitive advantage for well-positioned operators. Properties between Elliston Park and the community's residential interior are the strongest acquisition targets: they benefit from park-adjacent green space as a listing feature, proximity to the GlobalFest event draw, and the quiet residential character that airport-corridor travellers consistently rate highly. The community's 10-kilometre east-of-downtown positioning keeps acquisition prices accessible relative to nightly rate potential.
applewood-park.str_lifestyle: Elliston Park's GlobalFest fireworks festival — a major annual event typically held over multiple evenings in August — drives a concentrated burst of accommodation demand for Applewood Park listings that are within walking or cycling distance of the venue. For STR operators, this event represents a high-yield booking window to target with dynamic pricing well above the community's standard $110–$185 nightly band. Beyond GlobalFest, Elliston Park functions as a year-round green space and lake feature for the community, providing recreational access that supports multi-season listing appeal. Memorial Drive and 17 Avenue SE connect guests to downtown Calgary in approximately 20 minutes by car, while the Trans-Canada provides fast routing to the airport.
