renter_intel.title: Heritage Park Seasonal Visitor Draw is Thin
renter_intel.description: Heritage Park Historical Village draws significant annual visitation to the Glenmore corridor, but this demand is heavily seasonal (May–September) and does not generate the concentrated short-stay accommodation volume needed to support a viable STR operation across a full calendar year.

renter_intel.title: Hospital Proximity Creates a Niche Demand Segment
renter_intel.description: Rockyview General Hospital is accessible via Glenmore Trail within approximately five minutes. Families of long-stay patients and medical professionals on locum or rotation contracts represent a small but consistent demand segment for furnished accommodation in this part of the southwest.

renter_intel.title: Glenmore Pathway Access Supports Active-Lifestyle Listings
renter_intel.description: STR listings in Mayfair can credibly market access to the Glenmore Reservoir pathway — a premium cycling and running route with reservoir views — as a differentiating amenity for leisure guests, though this alone is insufficient to drive occupancy rates above the viability threshold.

whats_nearby.items: The Glenmore Reservoir's eastern shoreline pathway runs adjacent to Mayfair's western boundary, offering a scenic, traffic-free route for cyclists, runners, and walkers connecting to the broader southwest pathway network.

Located on Heritage Drive SW just south of Glenmore Trail, Heritage Park is one of Canada's largest living-history museums and a major seasonal tourism destination, bringing visitors to the broader Glenmore corridor from May through September.

A short drive north along Elbow Drive SW leads to Britannia Plaza's independent retailers, Sunterra Market, Lina's Italian Mercato, and neighbourhood cafés — providing Mayfair residents with a premium grocery and dining alternative to big-box retail on Macleod Trail.

queens-park-village.name: Queens Park Village
queens-park-village.subtitle: Compact NW Townhome Enclave on Queens Park Hill Near Confederation Park
queens-park-village.str_intro:
Queens Park Village is a small, largely residential community situated in northwest Calgary on the rolling terrain of Queens Park Hill, near the intersection of 40 Avenue NW and 5 Street NW. The community falls within Calgary's northwest quadrant and is proximate to Highwood to the north and the Confederation Park corridor to the west. While the City of Calgary maintains a formal community profile for Queens Park Village, it is a notably compact enclave with a limited residential footprint — primarily characterised by townhome complexes and low-rise rental buildings rather than single-family detached homes. Access to the broader city is facilitated by proximity to Deerfoot Trail to the east and 14 Street NW to the west.

The community's position near Queens Park Hill provides some of the elevated terrain and treed character common to northwest Calgary. Highwood Park, a short walk to the north, offers outdoor swimming in summer and a skating rink in winter. Confederation Park's extensive pathway system and the McCall Lake Golf Course are accessible within a brief drive or bike ride to the southwest. James Fowler High School and Buchanan School serve the area's school-age population, and a local Co-op or Safeway grocery node is within a short drive along 14 Street NW or 16 Avenue NW. Transit options run directly outside the community on nearby arterials.

Queens Park Village's housing stock is dominated by townhome complexes and multi-unit rental buildings — notably the 188-unit Queen's Park Townhomes complex at 501 40 Avenue NW, which consists primarily of two- and three-bedroom multi-level units averaging approximately 970 square feet. These units include in-suite laundry, fireplaces, and private patios, providing comfortable self-contained accommodation, though the buildings were constructed in 1978 and reflect that era's construction standards. The community's STR viability is Not recommended due to the nature of the strata ownership structure, tenant density, and the absence of the private-entry detached housing format most conducive to compliant short-term rental operations in Calgary.

renter_intel.title: Strata and Multi-Unit Buildings Limit STR Options
renter_intel.description: The dominant housing form in Queens Park Village — older townhome complexes and multi-unit buildings — is generally subject to strata bylaws that restrict or prohibit STR operations. Individual unit owners would need to confirm STR permissions with their strata council before proceeding.

renter_intel.title: Highwood Park as Nearby Seasonal Amenity
renter_intel.description: Highwood Park's seasonal outdoor pool and winter skating rink, a short walk north of Queens Park Village, can serve as a lifestyle amenity mention in rental listings, particularly for families seeking summer or winter recreational access without driving distance.

renter_intel.title: University of Calgary Proximity for Shoulder Demand
renter_intel.description: The University of Calgary campus is approximately 12 minutes by car, providing a potential secondary demand source from visiting academics, alumni families, and conference attendees particularly during the spring and fall semesters.

whats_nearby.items: The community park directly north on Harlow Avenue NW features an outdoor swimming pool open in summer and a skating rink in winter, serving as the neighbourhood's primary recreation facility and a gathering point for families.

A short drive or cycle ride west, Confederation Park's 107 hectares include the McCall Lake Golf Course, tennis courts, and an extensive pathway network connecting to the northwest's green corridor system.

James Fowler High School is located across the street from the Queen's Park Townhomes complex on 40 Avenue NW, providing immediate secondary school access for families with teenagers — one of the practical conveniences that makes the area functional for long-stay family visitors.

rideau-park.name: Rideau Park
rideau-park.subtitle: Elbow River Bend Community Adjacent to Sandy Beach and River Park
rideau-park.str_intro:
Rideau Park is a quiet, leafy residential neighbourhood in the southwest quadrant of Calgary, tucked into a bend of the Elbow River and bounded to the east by 4 Street SW and the escarpment, to the south by 33 Avenue SW, and to the north and west by the Elbow River itself. Established in 1911 and named after Rideau Road — which in turn derives from the Ottawa streets near Rideau Hall, the Governor General's residence — the neighbourhood was originally designed by planner Maurice Yelland Brown with a series of crescents and cul-de-sacs intended to create a sequence of connected residential islands. This Edwardian design philosophy remains visible in the neighbourhood's non-grid street pattern and contributes to the tucked-away quality that long-term residents prize. The Rideau-Roxboro Community Association at 101 – 30 Avenue SW manages programming for both communities.

Sandy Beach Park, River Park, and the Riverdale Pathway system define Rideau Park's recreational identity — providing Elbow River access, off-leash dog areas, and connection to the city-wide cycling and pedestrian trail network within walking distance of virtually every address in the community. Mission's restaurants and 4th Street SW's boutique commercial strip lie less than two kilometres north, making it straightforward for residents and guests to access Calgary's most vibrant inner-city hospitality corridor without requiring a car. Rideau Park School serves the community and has strong academic standing within the CBE system. The Glencoe Club on 37 Avenue SW provides an elite private sports and social facility option for affluent residents.

Rideau Park's housing stock is primarily composed of single-family detached homes, many dating from the 1910s–1940s original development period alongside later infills and rebuilds. Home values are high — the community's inner-city location, river-bend positioning, and design heritage command prices well above the Calgary average — and the owner-occupancy rate is correspondingly elevated. The Not recommended STR viability reflects both the acquisition cost relative to achievable nightly rates in this part of the southwest and the community's predominantly owner-occupied, low-density residential character. While individual whole-home listings at premium rates are possible, consistent annual revenue sufficient to justify investment at current Rideau Park prices is not well-supported by demand data.

Mission's restaurants and 4th Street SW's boutique commercial strip lie less than two kilometres north, making it straightforward for residents and guests to access Calgary's most vibrant inner-city hospitality corridor without requiring a car. Rideau Park School serves the community and has strong academic standing within the CBE system. The Not recommended STR viability reflects both the acquisition cost relative to achievable nightly rates in this part of the southwest and the community's predominantly owner-occupied, low-density residential character. While individual whole-home listings at premium rates are possible, consistent annual revenue sufficient to justify investment at current Rideau Park prices is not well-supported by demand data.

renter_intel.title: Premium Whole-Home Listings Face Thin Demand
renter_intel.description: While an elegant detached home in Rideau Park could command premium nightly rates, the community's location in inner southwest Calgary attracts a narrower short-stay audience than high-demand areas like the Beltline, East Village, or Inglewood. Year-round occupancy is the primary challenge.

renter_intel.title: Mission District Walkability Adds Guest Appeal
renter_intel.description: Rideau Park's northern edge is within reasonable walking distance of the Mission retail and restaurant corridor on 4th Street SW — a genuine lifestyle differentiator that can feature in listing copy for guests seeking immersive neighbourhood experiences rather than generic accommodation.

renter_intel.title: River Pathway System is a Unique Marketing Anchor
renter_intel.description: Direct neighbourhood access to Sandy Beach Park and the Elbow River pathway system is a meaningful and locally grounded amenity that distinguishes a Rideau Park listing from comparable inner-southwest options, particularly for outdoor-recreation-oriented guests visiting in warmer months.

whats_nearby.category: Sandy Beach Park and Elbow River Pathway
whats_nearby.items: Sandy Beach Park lies along the Elbow River just west of Rideau Park, offering river access, an off-leash dog area, and connection to a linear pathway corridor running through River Park and onward to 17 Avenue SW. This green network is one of inner Calgary's most-used recreational corridors.

Mission's dense restaurant, café, fitness, and retail strip along 4th Street SW between 17 Avenue SW and 25 Avenue SW is accessible by foot or bike from northern Rideau Park addresses, providing guests with immediate access to inner-city dining and entertainment without requiring transit.

Rideau Park School, serving the combined Rideau Park and Roxboro catchment, is a well-regarded CBE elementary and junior high school that is one of the primary reasons families pursue ownership in this part of the inner southwest.

roxboro.name: Roxboro
roxboro.subtitle: Tiny Elbow River Enclave — One of Calgary's Wealthiest Communities by Household Income
roxboro.str_intro:
Roxboro is one of Calgary's smallest and most affluent residential communities, occupying a 0.3-square-kilometre peninsula of land defined on its north and west sides by the Elbow River and on the south by 33 Avenue SW — placing it within a dramatic natural bend of the river approximately 2.5 kilometres south of downtown via 4th Street SW. Established in 1923 — a decade after neighbouring Rideau Park — Roxboro retains original estate homes from the 1920s and 1930s alongside contemporary custom rebuilds, creating a built environment that mixes Craftsman and period Revival architecture with contemporary luxury. The community is administered jointly with Rideau Park through the Rideau-Roxboro Community Association at 101–30 Avenue SW. Population as of the 2021 Census was recorded at approximately 415 residents in 161 dwellings — an exceptionally low density that reinforces the enclave quality.

Despite its tiny footprint, Roxboro's position offers inner-city lifestyle access that few communities of any size can replicate: the Elbow River pathway system wraps three sides of the community, 4th Street SW provides direct walking and cycling access to Mission's restaurant and café corridor to the north, and the Glencoe Club — one of Calgary's most prestigious private athletic and social clubs — is within minutes on 37 Avenue SW. The Stampede grounds and Saddledome are approximately a ten-minute drive northeast via Macleod Trail. As noted in the 2012 census data, 100% of Roxboro's housing was owner-occupied and none was recorded as rental, underlining the community's exclusive ownership culture.

Roxboro's housing stock is composed entirely of single-family detached homes, with no multi-unit, condominium, or apartment buildings recorded within the community boundaries. The median household income in 2000 was recorded at $153,667 — significantly higher than the Calgary average — with more recent estimates placing current values considerably above that figure given the trajectory of Calgary's luxury market. Home prices for Roxboro properties regularly exceed $2 million, and custom builds on premium river-facing lots can approach $5 million or more. The Not recommended STR viability reflects the prohibitive entry cost relative to achievable short-stay revenue, the 0% historical rental rate indicating near-zero suite stock, and a community culture entirely oriented toward owner-occupancy and long-term stewardship.
roxboro.str_lifestyle: Despite its tiny footprint, Roxboro's position offers inner-city lifestyle access that few communities of any size can replicate: the Elbow River pathway system wraps three sides of the community, 4th Street SW provides direct walking and cycling access to Mission's restaurant and café corridor to the north, and the Glencoe Club — one of Calgary's most prestigious private athletic and social clubs — is within minutes on 37 Avenue SW. The Stampede grounds and Saddledome are approximately a ten-minute drive northeast via Macleod Trail. As noted in the 2012 census data, 100% of Roxboro's housing was owner-occupied and none was recorded as rental, underlining the community's exclusive ownership culture.

The median household income in 2000 was recorded at $153,667 — significantly higher than the Calgary average — with more recent estimates placing current values considerably above that figure given the trajectory of Calgary's luxury market. Home prices for Roxboro properties regularly exceed $2 million, and custom builds on premium river-facing lots can approach $5 million or more. The Not recommended STR viability reflects the prohibitive entry cost relative to achievable short-stay revenue, the 0% historical rental rate indicating near-zero suite stock, and a community culture entirely oriented toward owner-occupancy and long-term stewardship.

renter_intel.title: Zero Historical Rental Activity
renter_intel.description: The 2012 municipal census recorded 0% of Roxboro's housing as renter-occupied — an extraordinary finding that reflects the community's exclusivity and owner-occupant culture. There is effectively no existing STR or rental supply from which to benchmark occupancy expectations.

renter_intel.title: River Pathway Access as a Premium Differentiator
renter_intel.description: For any whole-home listing that does come to market, the Elbow River pathway on three sides of the community is an exceptional lifestyle anchor — walkable to Mission, dog-friendly, and connected to the city-wide trail network. This is genuine value that very few inner-city addresses can claim.

renter_intel.title: Acquisition Costs are Prohibitive for STR Returns
renter_intel.description: With home prices regularly exceeding $2 million and no secondary suite stock available, generating STR revenue adequate to service acquisition debt is not realistic under current market conditions. Roxboro is a long-term capital appreciation play, not an income-generating STR asset.

whats_nearby.items: The Elbow River wraps Roxboro's north and west boundaries, with the riverside pathway providing direct walking and cycling access to Sandy Beach Park, River Park, and the pathway network that eventually connects to Glenmore Reservoir to the south and downtown to the north.

A short walk north along 4th Street SW leads to Mission's restaurant and café strip, widely considered one of Calgary's top urban dining and lifestyle destinations. The commute for residents is counted in minutes on foot, not minutes by car.

One of Calgary's most exclusive private clubs, the Glencoe Club on 37 Avenue SW offers tennis, racquet sports, fitness, swimming, curling, and a range of social events. Membership is a significant draw for the affluent professional demographic that Roxboro attracts.

upper-mount-royal.name: Upper Mount Royal
upper-mount-royal.subtitle: Calgary's Most Prestigious Inner-City Neighbourhood — Estate Homes on the Southwest Escarpment
upper-mount-royal.str_intro:
Upper Mount Royal occupies the high escarpment terrain of southwest Calgary, positioned south of 17 Avenue SW, west of College Lane and Hope Street, north of 10 Street and Premier Way, and east of 14 Street SW — sitting above and distinctly separate from Lower Mount Royal on the flat terrain to the north. The community is bounded by some of Calgary's most coveted residential streets and is home to approximately 3,100 residents in large lots with elevated views toward downtown, the Bow River valley, and in some locations the Rocky Mountains. It is routinely cited as Calgary's most expensive and prestigious neighbourhood, with an average home price above $2.4 million and listings ranging from $1.5 million to $5.75 million as of recent market data. The neighbourhood encompasses schools including Earl Grey Elementary, William Reid Elementary, Mount Royal Junior High, and Western Canada High School within its catchment.

Upper Mount Royal's lifestyle is defined by wide, tree-lined streets, architectural diversity ranging from century-old character homes built by early Calgary industrialists to contemporary custom builds, and immediate walkability to the 17 Avenue SW and 4 Street SW commercial corridors. The community's elevation above the Beltline provides quiet residential character while placing residents within minutes of the city's densest concentration of restaurants, galleries, and boutiques. Mount Royal University is approximately ten minutes south via 14 Street SW, providing cultural and educational proximity. The Glencoe Club, one of Western Canada's premier private athletic clubs, is directly adjacent to the community boundary and accessible to member residents on foot.

Upper Mount Royal's housing stock is one of the most architecturally varied in Calgary, spanning original 1910–1930s character homes with period craftsmanship details, mid-century custom builds, and contemporary luxury infills on lots that frequently exceed 1,200 square metres. The community has no rental stock and an ownership culture that strongly opposes commercialisation — no multi-unit buildings exist within its boundaries, and the neighbourhood association actively monitors development compliance. The Not recommended STR viability is categorical: acquisition costs are among the highest in the city, there is zero suite stock compatible with STR licensing, community culture is hostile to commercial short-stay use, and the demand profile for luxury whole-home rentals in this part of the city does not generate the occupancy rates needed to service investment at these price points.

The community's elevation above the Beltline provides quiet residential character while placing residents within minutes of the city's densest concentration of restaurants, galleries, and boutiques. Mount Royal University is approximately ten minutes south via 14 Street SW, providing cultural and educational proximity. The Glencoe Club, one of Western Canada's premier private athletic clubs, is directly adjacent to the community boundary and accessible to member residents on foot.

The community has no rental stock and an ownership culture that strongly opposes commercialisation — no multi-unit buildings exist within its boundaries, and the neighbourhood association actively monitors development compliance. The Not recommended STR viability is categorical: acquisition costs are among the highest in the city, there is zero suite stock compatible with STR licensing, community culture is hostile to commercial short-stay use, and the demand profile for luxury whole-home rentals in this part of the city does not generate the occupancy rates needed to service investment at these price points.

renter_intel.title: Highest Average Home Price in Batch
renter_intel.description: With an average asking price above $2.4 million and no secondary suite stock, Upper Mount Royal represents the highest acquisition barrier of any community in this batch. Even at premium STR nightly rates, annual revenue cannot approach the carrying costs required at these price points.

renter_intel.title: Character Home Restrictions
renter_intel.description: Many Upper Mount Royal properties fall within heritage or architectural guideline overlays that restrict renovation scope, suite additions, and exterior modifications. Investors considering any infill or conversion strategy must conduct thorough regulatory due diligence before acquisition.

renter_intel.title: Walkability to 17 Avenue is a Genuine Lifestyle Asset
renter_intel.description: For any whole-home listing brought to market, the walkability to 17 Avenue SW's high-density restaurant, bar, and retail strip is an authentic and compelling amenity. The street is within a ten-to-fifteen minute walk from most Upper Mount Royal addresses and gives the neighbourhood a genuine urban edge that suburban luxury communities lack.

whats_nearby.items: One of Calgary's most vibrant commercial streets, 17 Avenue SW between 4 Street SW and 14 Street SW offers an exceptionally dense concentration of restaurants, cocktail bars, boutique retail, fitness studios, and cafés accessible on foot from Upper Mount Royal's northern edge.

Adjacent to the Upper Mount Royal boundary, the Glencoe Club on 37 Avenue SW provides member residents with access to tennis, squash, racquetball, curling, fitness, swimming, and social programming — one of Western Canada's premier private clubs.

Mount Royal University on 4825 Mount Royal Gate SW is approximately ten minutes south via 14 Street SW, providing cultural programming, continuing education, and a degree of institutional vitality to the southwest corridor that benefits the broader community.

huxley.name: Huxley
huxley.subtitle: Emerging East Calgary Master-Planned Community in the Belvedere Growth Corridor
huxley.str_intro:
Huxley is a new master-planned residential community in east Calgary, located within the Belvedere Area Structure Plan near the intersection of 17 Avenue SE and the East Hill Shopping Centre — approximately 15 minutes from downtown via Glenmore Trail or Deerfoot Trail. The community was developed by Genesis Land Development Corp. on 160 acres of residential land, approved by the City of Calgary for approximately 1,400 homes when fully built out. In May 2024, Genesis announced the sale of a 40% ownership stake to two Calgary-based home builders for $21.4 million, accelerating the development program. Truman Homes has active construction underway on single-family and duplex product, with completion phases running through late 2025 and into 2027.

Huxley's design emphasises sustainability and community connectivity, with over 13 acres of parks, open spaces, and walking trails integrated into the community plan. Many homes include solar panels and EV charging infrastructure as standard features. The community is positioned on the future 17 Avenue Bus Rapid Transit (BRT) line, which will provide enhanced transit connectivity once the route is operational. East Hill Shopping Centre, immediately adjacent, provides immediate access to retail, grocery, dining, and services — an advantage that most new greenfield communities lack for years after opening. Jack James High School, Forest Lawn High School, and Father Lacombe High School serve the area's secondary school-age population.

Huxley's housing mix includes detached single-family homes, duplexes, and future multi-family formats, with many units including legal basement income suites as a standard or optional feature — reflecting Calgary's growing builder awareness of the secondary suite market. Home prices in early phases have been documented in the $640,000–$960,000 range. The Not applicable viability designation reflects the community's early-stage development status: STR demand data is not yet available, HOA structures and bylaws are still being established, and the surrounding commercial and institutional amenity base is only partially complete. Investors with a longer horizon and an appetite for new-construction positioning should monitor this community as it matures.

Investors with a longer horizon and an appetite for new-construction positioning should monitor this community as it matures.

renter_intel.title: Income Suites Built Into New Construction
renter_intel.description: A meaningful proportion of Huxley's new detached and duplex homes are being constructed with purpose-built legal basement suites, providing income-generating potential from day one. Investors acquiring in the early phases may benefit from builder incentives including assignment provisions and rental management programs.

renter_intel.title: 17 Avenue BRT Line is a Future Transit Anchor
renter_intel.description: Huxley is positioned on the planned 17 Avenue SE Bus Rapid Transit corridor, which when operational will provide frequent, high-capacity transit service connecting the community to downtown, the Beltline, and East Village. Transit access is a significant medium-term demand driver for STR guests without vehicles.

renter_intel.title: East Hill Shopping Centre Provides Immediate Commercial Access
renter_intel.description: Unlike most greenfield communities that operate without retail amenities for the first several years, Huxley benefits from the adjacent East Hill Shopping Centre providing grocery, pharmacy, restaurants, and services from the community's first occupied phases.

whats_nearby.items: Immediately adjacent to Huxley, East Hill Shopping Centre provides grocery, retail, restaurants, and services — an uncommonly direct amenity connection for a greenfield community in early phases of development.
Huxley's proximity to both Deerfoot Trail and Glenmore Trail provides efficient arterial access to downtown (approximately 15 minutes), the airport, and southeast Calgary's employment nodes.

The planned 17 Avenue Bus Rapid Transit line runs through the Belvedere corridor, and Huxley's position on this alignment will provide significantly enhanced transit access when the route reaches full operational status.

lewisburg.name: Lewisburg
lewisburg.subtitle: Genstar's New 100-Acre Northeast Community with Show Homes Now Open
lewisburg.str_intro:
Lewisburg is a brand-new master-planned community in northeast Calgary, developed by Genstar Development Co. on approximately 100 acres of land. The community launched its first show homes along Lewisburg Close NE in 2025, offering a diverse range of architectural styles and residential formats including single-family detached, semi-detached, townhomes, and future apartment formats to serve a variety of household sizes and price points. The land was originally settled in the late 1880s by the Lewisburg family — a pioneering clan with deep community ties — and the community name honours that heritage. Planned community features include three neighbourhood parks, multi-use pathway connections, and future commercial space to create a connected, family-friendly environment.

Lewisburg's northeast location provides convenient proximity to Calgary International Airport (a primary draw for both residents and visiting guests), major shopping and services along the 128 Avenue NE and Country Hills Boulevard corridors, and rapid arterial access via Stoney Trail NE and Deerfoot Trail. The Calgary Airport and adjacent employment nodes in the northeast represent a meaningful demand source for furnished accommodation, particularly for contractors, logistics professionals, and airline crew on extended stays. The community's pathway network and planned parks are designed to prioritise pedestrian connectivity and active outdoor lifestyles from the earliest phases of development.

Lewisburg's housing product includes a mix of detached homes with legal basement income suites — reflecting builder recognition of Calgary's secondary suite market — alongside semi-detached and townhome formats suitable for both owner-occupants and investors. Cardel Homes is among the active builders, offering floor plans such as the Aspire (2,301 sq ft, 4 bed) with flexible suite configurations. Average list prices from early phases range from approximately $639,000 to $802,000 for detached product. The Not applicable designation reflects the community's earliest-stage status: insufficient occupancy or revenue data exists to assess STR performance, and community bylaws are still being established.

renter_intel.title: Airport Proximity Creates Built-In Demand
renter_intel.description: Lewisburg's northeast location places it within approximately 10–12 minutes of Calgary International Airport, creating a natural demand pool among travellers, airline crew on extended layovers, and contractors working in the northeast industrial and logistics corridor.

renter_intel.title: Genstar Community Design Prioritises Walkability
renter_intel.description: The community plan includes three dedicated neighbourhood parks and a multi-use pathway network built into the plan from the outset — infrastructure that supports active lifestyle marketing for STR listings in a way that ad-hoc suburban subdivisions typically cannot.

renter_intel.title: Early-Phase Acquisition May Offer Suite Product at Pre-Appreciation Pricing
renter_intel.description: Purchasing in Lewisburg's earliest phases may provide investors with access to purpose-built suite product at pre-development appreciation prices, before the community's commercial and transit infrastructure is fully established. The trade-off is a longer runway to optimised STR performance.

whats_nearby.items: Calgary International Airport is approximately 10–12 minutes southwest via Stoney Trail NE and Deerfoot Trail, making Lewisburg one of the northeast communities with the most direct airport connectivity — a meaningful differentiator for guests arriving with luggage who need to minimise transfer time.

whats_nearby.category: Country Hills Boulevard and 128 Avenue NE Retail
whats_nearby.items: The Country Hills Boulevard commercial corridor provides grocery, pharmacy, restaurants, and big-box retail within a short drive, while 128 Avenue NE connects to Costco, Walmart, and the broader northeast commercial infrastructure serving Calgary's growing north communities.

Stoney Trail NE is the primary access route for Lewisburg residents, connecting within minutes to Deerfoot Trail, Country Hills Boulevard, and the broader ring-road network that circumnavigates the city — a critical infrastructure asset for commuters and guests navigating the northeast.

manchester.name: Manchester
manchester.subtitle: Inner-South Community in Active Transition Under Calgary's Chinook Redevelopment Plan
manchester.str_intro:
Manchester is a residential community in the south-central area of Calgary, straddling both the southwest and southeast quadrants, and bounded by 42 Avenue South to the north, 58 Avenue South to the south, Macleod Trail to the west, and the Canadian Pacific railway right-of-way to the east. Established in 1914 as a workers' community within walking distance of nearby industrial employment, Manchester is one of Calgary's oldest residential neighbourhoods and has historically recorded the lowest median household income of any Calgary community. The community is surrounded on three sides — north, south, and east — by Manchester Industrial, reinforcing its location within a primarily industrial corridor. Heritage Calgary added Manchester's Edwardian workers' cottages on 1 Street SW to its heritage sites list in December 2014, though these buildings currently have no formal legal protection.

Manchester's daily life infrastructure is largely defined by its adjacency to Macleod Trail's commercial strip and the Manchester Industrial area, rather than by dedicated community amenities. The Windsor Park Community Association serves Manchester's residential population. The broader Chinook Macleod corridor along Macleod Trail SW provides transit access — the Chinook LRT station on the Red Line is nearby — as well as access to CF Chinook Centre shopping mall, grocery chains, and medical services along the arterial. The Elbow River pathway system is accessible via 4 Street SW to the west, connecting to the broader southwest greenway.

Manchester's housing stock is concentrated in low-income multi-unit residential buildings and housing projects, with a significant portion of subsidised housing constructed in 2003 in the community's southern portion. The community's historical median household income has been the lowest in Calgary by a substantial margin, and housing values remain at the lower end of the city's spectrum. The Not applicable viability designation reflects the absence of meaningful STR demand in this location, the community's social and economic profile, and the significant physical constraints imposed by surrounding industrial uses. However, the Chinook Communities Local Area Planning process now underway envisions substantial future mixed-use redevelopment along Macleod Trail, which could materially alter Manchester's land-use profile over a five-to-fifteen year horizon.

The Windsor Park Community Association serves Manchester's residential population. The broader Chinook Macleod corridor along Macleod Trail SW provides transit access — the Chinook LRT station on the Red Line is nearby — as well as access to CF Chinook Centre shopping mall, grocery chains, and medical services along the arterial. However, the Chinook Communities Local Area Planning process now underway envisions substantial future mixed-use redevelopment along Macleod Trail, which could materially alter Manchester's land-use profile over a five-to-fifteen year horizon.

renter_intel.title: Chinook Communities Plan Signals Long-Term Redevelopment
renter_intel.description: The City of Calgary's Chinook Communities Local Area Plan designates Manchester as an area for industrial transition and mixed-use redevelopment, particularly east of Macleod Trail. For long-horizon investors, land assemblies in Manchester's western portion may capture future density value as the plan is implemented.

renter_intel.title: Heritage Cottages Have Protection Gap
renter_intel.description: The Edwardian workers' cottages on 1 Street SW were recognised by Heritage Calgary but have not received formal legal protection. This creates a renovation window that could permit sympathetic adaptive reuse — though the community's current income profile and location limit the commercial upside of heritage conversion projects.

renter_intel.title: Chinook LRT Station is the Area's Primary Transit Asset
renter_intel.description: The Chinook LRT station on the Red Line provides connections to downtown, South Health Campus, and the south suburban corridor — a transit asset that will become more significant as Macleod Trail redevelopment increases residential density along the corridor.

whats_nearby.items: The Chinook station on the Red Line CTrain provides direct transit access to downtown Calgary and south suburban destinations, located on Macleod Trail SW adjacent to the Manchester neighbourhood boundary.

One of Calgary's largest enclosed shopping centres, CF Chinook Centre on Macleod Trail SW is a short distance from Manchester and provides major retail, dining, cinema, and services to the south-central community.

The Manchester Industrial area surrounding the residential community hosts a range of light industrial, commercial, and logistics businesses — the primary employment base for the area and part of the future mixed-use redevelopment zone identified in the Chinook Communities plan.

medicine-hill.name: Medicine Hill
medicine-hill.subtitle: New West Calgary Village Community on the Paskapoo Slopes Adjacent to Canada Olympic Park
medicine-hill.str_intro:
Medicine Hill is one of Calgary's newest and most distinctive communities, situated in the west quadrant on the Paskapoo Slopes — a culturally significant natural landscape within the ancestral territories of the Blackfoot Confederacy, the Îethka Nakoda Wîcastabi First Nations, and the Tsuut'ina First Nation. The community is bounded by the Trans-Canada Highway (Highway 1) to the north, Canada Olympic Park (WinSport) to the east, Sarcee Trail to the south and east, and the communities of Cougar Ridge, Patterson Heights, and West Springs to the south. Approximately 350 acres of the Paskapoo Slopes are managed as a natural park area, with a City of Calgary Park Plan currently under development to guide ecological health, cultural stewardship, and public access. The community's hillside positioning provides spectacular views of the Bow River valley, downtown Calgary, and the Rocky Mountains.

Medicine Hill's lifestyle is fundamentally shaped by its adjacency to WinSport Canada Olympic Park — one of Canada's premier year-round sports and recreation facilities offering skiing, snowboarding, luge, bobsleigh, mountain biking, ropes courses, skating, and sports camps. Ravine pathways and the Paskapoo Slopes trail network provide direct access to natural terrain for hiking, cycling, and wildlife observation. Trinity Hills Shopping Centre and the Calgary Farmers' Market West (relocated to nearby West Springs Drive SW) offer retail and dining access within a short drive. The Trans-Canada Highway at the community's northern edge provides unobstructed westward access toward Banff National Park and the mountain resorts in under an hour.

Medicine Hill's housing product is being delivered by Homes by Avi, Lupi Luxury Homes, Bedrock Homes, and Renova, offering a range of detached homes, no-condo-fee townhomes, and walkout models designed with secondary suite options. Early-phase pricing has been reported from the low-$400,000s for condos to $800,000+ for townhomes and detached product. The Not applicable viability designation reflects the community's very early development stage, with an insufficient base of completed homes and no established short-stay rental market from which to draw performance data. The proximity to WinSport, Banff highway access, and the mountain recreation corridor represents a legitimate future demand driver for STR that merits monitoring as the community matures.

The proximity to WinSport, Banff highway access, and the mountain recreation corridor represents a legitimate future demand driver for STR that merits monitoring as the community matures.

renter_intel.title: WinSport Adjacency is a Distinctive STR Demand Driver
renter_intel.description: Canada Olympic Park/WinSport is a year-round sports facility with skiing, mountain biking, bobsleigh, and sports camps that draws visitors from across Alberta and beyond. A well-positioned Medicine Hill property could credibly market ski-in-adjacent accommodation — a positioning that commands premium nightly rates and distinguishes the listing from conventional Calgary suburban product.

renter_intel.title: Mountain Highway Access Extends the Demand Pool
renter_intel.description: Medicine Hill sits directly off Highway 1 (Trans-Canada) toward Banff and Lake Louise, positioning it as a natural waypoint for mountain visitors who prefer the infrastructure and services of the city to staying in resort towns. This secondary demand pool — particularly for groups and families — is an underappreciated STR opportunity in the western Calgary corridor.

renter_intel.title: Indigenous Cultural Landscape Requires Sensitive Marketing
renter_intel.description: The Paskapoo Slopes are an area of deep and ongoing Indigenous cultural significance. STR listings and marketing content should treat this context with appropriate respect and accuracy, neither overstating or sensationalising Indigenous heritage nor omitting it from the neighbourhood story.

whats_nearby.items: WinSport on 88 Canada Olympic Road SW is directly adjacent to Medicine Hill and offers year-round programming including alpine skiing, snowboard terrain, luge and bobsleigh tracks, mountain biking, skating, summer camps, and the Athletes' Village facilities used during the 1988 Winter Olympics.

The 350-acre Paskapoo Slopes park area bounded by the Trans-Canada Highway, Sarcee Trail, and Canada Olympic Park provides walking trails, natural grassland, and ravine ecosystems accessible from Medicine Hill. A City of Calgary Park Plan is under development to formalise access and ecological management.

whats_nearby.category: Trinity Hills Shopping Centre and Calgary Farmers' Market West
whats_nearby.items: Trinity Hills, a commercial node on the Trans-Canada Highway, provides grocery, restaurants, and retail within minutes of Medicine Hill. The Calgary Farmers' Market West at West Springs offers local producers, artisan vendors, and prepared food — a lifestyle amenity with strong appeal for both residents and visiting guests.

moraine.name: Moraine
moraine.subtitle: Formerly Ambleton: Northwest Calgary's Newest Master-Planned Community on Stoney Trail
moraine.str_intro:
Moraine — formerly known as Ambleton until a renaming in December 2023 — is a brand-new master-planned community in northwest Calgary, situated in the Glacier Ridge growth corridor. The community is bounded by 160 Avenue NW to the north, 14 Street NW to the east, 144 Avenue NW to the south, and Panorama Road/24 Street NW to the west, placing it at the northwestern frontier of the city's urban boundary. The name Moraine was chosen to reflect the glacial geological heritage of the landscape — moraines being the rock and sediment deposits left by the retreat of glaciers that shaped the terrain of this part of Alberta. Access to the broader city is provided by Stoney Trail NW, 14 Street NW, and 144 Avenue NW, with downtown Calgary accessible in approximately 30–35 minutes via these corridors.

Moraine's lifestyle offer is currently developing alongside the community's infrastructure, with parks, pathways, and commercial spaces arriving in phases as the residential population grows. Adjacent and nearby communities in the Evanston and Nolan Hill corridor provide established retail access including grocery, dining, and services while Moraine's own commercial nodes are still under construction. The West Nose Creek valley near the community's eastern boundary provides natural greenway access and corridor views characteristic of northwest Calgary's topographic diversity. The broader Glacier Ridge Area Structure Plan envisions future schools, commercial nodes, and community activity centres that will serve Moraine residents as the corridor matures.

Moraine's housing mix currently includes new-construction detached homes, semi-detached units, and townhomes, with a growing number of properties offering legal secondary suites as a built-in income feature. Qualico Communities and other builders active in the Glacier Ridge corridor are delivering product to multiple price points. The Not applicable viability designation reflects the community's early-stage status: a very limited number of completions exist, short-stay rental demand data is unavailable, and the surrounding commercial and transit infrastructure is still being established. Investors with patience for a three-to-five year maturation curve may find Moraine — particularly suite-equipped new construction — worth monitoring.

Adjacent and nearby communities in the Evanston and Nolan Hill corridor provide established retail access including grocery, dining, and services while Moraine's own commercial nodes are still under construction. The West Nose Creek valley near the community's eastern boundary provides natural greenway access and corridor views characteristic of northwest Calgary's topographic diversity. Investors with patience for a three-to-five year maturation curve may find Moraine — particularly suite-equipped new construction — worth monitoring.

renter_intel.title: Suite-Equipped New Construction at Pre-Appreciation Pricing
renter_intel.description: Purpose-built legal suite homes in Moraine's earliest phases may be acquired at prices that reflect the community's incomplete amenity base, offering the potential for capital appreciation as the Glacier Ridge corridor fills in with schools, commercial nodes, and transit improvements over the next five years.

renter_intel.title: Stoney Trail Access Supports Corporate Travel Demand
renter_intel.description: Stoney Trail NW provides rapid automotive connectivity to the airport, the downtown core, and northwest employment areas. For corporate guests on extended project assignments who require flexible accommodation, Moraine's ring-road position and newer housing stock are credible alternatives to older inner-city rental stock.

renter_intel.title: Evanston Commercial Node Bridges Amenity Gap
renter_intel.description: While Moraine's own commercial spaces are in development, the established Evanston retail node a short drive south — including grocery, pharmacy, restaurants, and services — bridges the amenity gap during the community's early phases and can be referenced in listing copy as nearby conveniences.

whats_nearby.items: Stoney Trail NW runs along the southern boundary of the Moraine area, providing ring-road access to every quadrant of Calgary and bypassing downtown entirely for north-to-south or east-to-west city crossings.

The West Nose Creek valley corridor adjacent to the community's eastern boundary provides natural greenway access and will form part of the area's long-term linear park network as development progresses northward through the Glacier Ridge Area Structure Plan.

The established Evanston community to the south provides the nearest grocery stores, pharmacies, dining, and services until Moraine's own commercial spaces reach completion — a practical bridge amenity that makes the community more liveable in its early phases.

red-carpet.name: Red Carpet
red-carpet.subtitle: Small East Calgary Community at the City's Eastern Fringe Near International Avenue
red-carpet.str_intro:
Red Carpet is a small community at the eastern edge of Calgary, located in the southeast quadrant between CN Rail tracks, 17 Avenue SE (International Avenue), and 68 Street SE. The community's position at the city's eastern boundary creates a markedly different character from inner-city or even mid-ring suburban communities — land uses in the surrounding area include industrial parcels, logistics operations, and the CN rail corridor that defines the community's northern boundary. International Avenue (17 Avenue SE) to the south of the community is one of Calgary's most culturally diverse commercial streets, hosting an extraordinary concentration of international restaurants, specialty grocery stores, and service businesses representing dozens of nationalities — a genuine and underappreciated urban amenity along Calgary's southeast axis.

Despite its peripheral location, Red Carpet benefits from International Avenue's commercial strip for daily needs: Vietnamese, Ethiopian, South Asian, Caribbean, and other cuisine types are available within walking distance or a short drive south along 17 Avenue SE. The East Hills Shopping Centre at 17 Avenue SE and 68 Street SE provides big-box retail including Walmart, Costco, Canadian Tire, and ancillary services. For outdoor recreation, the Bow River valley and the Fish Creek Provincial Park system are accessible further south. Calgary Transit bus routes on International Avenue connect the area to the 39 Avenue CTrain station, from which the Red Line provides access to downtown.

Red Carpet's housing stock is characterised by older single-family detached homes, some semi-detached units, and limited apartment buildings — reflecting the community's development era and peripheral location. Property values are below the Calgary average, and the community does not attract the investment attention that drives infill and renovation activity in inner-city or established midtown communities. The Not applicable viability designation reflects the combination of peripheral location, minimal STR demand from this part of the city, absence of corporate or tourism demand drivers, and a housing stock that does not support premium nightly rates. The community's long-term trajectory may be influenced by the broader East Calgary intensification underway along International Avenue.

The East Hills Shopping Centre at 17 Avenue SE and 68 Street SE provides big-box retail including Walmart, Costco, Canadian Tire, and ancillary services. For outdoor recreation, the Bow River valley and the Fish Creek Provincial Park system are accessible further south. Calgary Transit bus routes on International Avenue connect the area to the 39 Avenue CTrain station, from which the Red Line provides access to downtown.

The Not applicable viability designation reflects the combination of peripheral location, minimal STR demand from this part of the city, absence of corporate or tourism demand drivers, and a housing stock that does not support premium nightly rates. The community's long-term trajectory may be influenced by the broader East Calgary intensification underway along International Avenue.

renter_intel.title: International Avenue is an Underrated Cultural Amenity
renter_intel.description: 17 Avenue SE (International Avenue) is one of Calgary's most culturally diverse commercial streets, offering authentic international cuisine and specialty retail within walking distance or a short drive south of Red Carpet. For STR listings targeting guests seeking local cultural experiences, this is a genuine differentiator from generic suburban accommodation.

renter_intel.title: East Hills Shopping Provides Immediate Retail Access
renter_intel.description: The East Hills Shopping Centre at 68 Street SE and 17 Avenue SE provides Costco, Walmart, Canadian Tire, and ancillary services within a short drive, making the community functionally self-sufficient for household essentials despite its peripheral location.

renter_intel.title: CN Rail and Industrial Adjacency Limit Premium Positioning
renter_intel.description: The CN Rail corridor that defines Red Carpet's northern boundary creates noise, visual impact, and freight traffic that reduces the community's appeal as a premium guest destination. STR listings in this area would need to address these environmental factors explicitly to manage guest expectations.

whats_nearby.items: International Avenue is Calgary's most ethnically diverse commercial street, running east from Macleod Trail through Forest Lawn and beyond, with a dense concentration of international restaurants, specialty grocery stores, and cultural businesses representing dozens of nationalities from Vietnam to Ethiopia to the Philippines.

Located at the intersection of 17 Avenue SE and 68 Street SE, East Hills provides big-box retail anchored by Costco, Walmart, and Canadian Tire, along with a range of service businesses and dining options serving east and southeast Calgary.

The 39 Avenue Station on the Red Line CTrain network provides transit access to downtown Calgary and south suburban stations. Bus connections on International Avenue link Red Carpet to the CTrain network for car-free mobility across the city.

ricardo-ranch.name: Ricardo Ranch
ricardo-ranch.subtitle: Southern Calgary Bow River Gateway Community — Area Structure Plan Now in Development
ricardo-ranch.str_intro:
Ricardo Ranch is a large-scale planned community in southeast Calgary, encompassing approximately 635 hectares (1,570 acres) of land bounded by Seton and Rangeview to the north, Deerfoot Trail SE to the west, the Bow River to the south, and 88 Street SE to the east. The Area Structure Plan (ASP) was approved by Calgary City Council in fall 2019 and envisions four distinct neighbourhoods accommodating between 16,000 and 20,000 future residents. The community takes its name from the Ricardo Ranch — a working cattle property homesteaded in 1888 by William Crawley Ricardo, one of the founders of the Calgary Ranchmen's Club — and the name endured through subsequent ownership by Senator Patrick Burns and the Soutzo family. The site features dramatic natural geography: a Bow River valley escarpment separates the upland plains to the north from native grassland and river benchland to the south, with mountain views and Bow River access among its defining natural assets.

The Ricardo Ranch ASP envisions a pedestrian-focused village core centrally located along the top of the escarpment, with interconnected open space corridors running along both the escarpment edge and the Bow River. Residents will have access to escarpment pathway trails, a proposed Bow River park area, and the existing infrastructure of the mature Seton Urban District immediately to the north — including the world's largest YMCA, South Health Campus hospital, Cineplex VIP theatre, and Seton commercial district. Deerfoot Trail to the west and future transit infrastructure will provide commuter connectivity to the rest of Calgary. The south Bow River corridor offers one of the most scenic natural settings available in any Calgary community currently in development.

Ricardo Ranch will ultimately offer a diverse mix of low- and medium-density residential formats including single-family homes, semi-detached, townhomes, and medium-density multi-family, with two school sites and a neighbourhood community centre built into the plan. The three primary landowners — Genesis Land Development, Brookfield Residential, and Jayman Homes/Telsec — are at different stages of Outline Plan development. Brookfield's Seton Ridge (on the Ricardo Ranch Drive address) has active duplex product available with legal basement suites. The Not applicable designation reflects the community's planning-to-early-construction status: most residential development is still years from completion, and STR demand data for Ricardo Ranch proper does not yet exist.
ricardo-ranch.str_lifestyle: The Ricardo Ranch ASP envisions a pedestrian-focused village core centrally located along the top of the escarpment, with interconnected open space corridors running along both the escarpment edge and the Bow River. Residents will have access to escarpment pathway trails, a proposed Bow River park area, and the existing infrastructure of the mature Seton Urban District immediately to the north — including the world's largest YMCA, South Health Campus hospital, Cineplex VIP theatre, and Seton commercial district. Deerfoot Trail to the west and future transit infrastructure will provide commuter connectivity to the rest of Calgary. The south Bow River corridor offers one of the most scenic natural settings available in any Calgary community currently in development.

The three primary landowners — Genesis Land Development, Brookfield Residential, and Jayman Homes/Telsec — are at different stages of Outline Plan development. Brookfield's Seton Ridge (on the Ricardo Ranch Drive address) has active duplex product available with legal basement suites. The Not applicable designation reflects the community's planning-to-early-construction status: most residential development is still years from completion, and STR demand data for Ricardo Ranch proper does not yet exist.

renter_intel.title: Seton Urban District Adjacency is a Significant Advantage
renter_intel.description: Ricardo Ranch's northern border connects directly to Seton — one of Calgary's most complete master-planned urban districts, featuring the world's largest YMCA, South Health Campus, Cineplex VIP, and a mature retail commercial core. Guests staying in Ricardo Ranch early phases can access Seton's amenity base immediately.

renter_intel.title: Bow River and Escarpment Views Command Premium Positioning
renter_intel.description: Properties on or near the escarpment edge with Bow River valley views and Rocky Mountain sightlines represent a genuine differentiated value proposition within southeast Calgary's STR landscape — rare terrain in a quadrant dominated by flat prairie subdivisions.

renter_intel.title: South Health Campus Creates Healthcare Worker Demand
renter_intel.description: South Health Campus, Calgary's major south hospital facility, is adjacent via the Seton connection — creating a consistent demand pool among rotating healthcare workers, medical professionals on temporary assignment, and families of patients for furnished short-stay accommodation in the southeast corridor.

whats_nearby.items: Seton — Calgary's most developed urban district in the southeast — lies directly north of Ricardo Ranch and includes the world's largest YMCA at 20 Seton Park Road SE, South Health Campus hospital, Cineplex VIP Cineplex, and a multi-block commercial district with grocery, dining, and services.

whats_nearby.category: Bow River Valley and Escarpment Pathway
whats_nearby.items: The Ricardo Ranch community plan incorporates dedicated pathway corridors along the top of the Bow River valley escarpment and access to the Bow River benchland below, providing an exceptional natural recreation amenity in what will become one of Calgary's most scenically positioned communities.

Deerfoot Trail SE defines Ricardo Ranch's western boundary, providing rapid highway access northward to downtown Calgary (approximately 25 minutes) and to the entire southeast industrial and commercial employment corridor, which is among the city's most active growth zones.

symons-valley-ranch.name: Symons Valley Ranch
symons-valley-ranch.subtitle: NW Calgary's 30-Acre Redevelopment Site — Future Mixed-Use Community Hub Under Glacier Ridge Plan
symons-valley-ranch.str_intro:
Symons Valley Ranch refers to a specific 30.63-acre development site at 14555 Symons Valley Road NW in northwest Calgary, positioned at the intersection of Symons Valley Road NW and 144 Avenue NW, just north of Sage Hill and adjacent to the West Nose Creek valley. The site has a storied history: it operated as an entertainment and hospitality venue from 1968 through the late 1980s, hosting a popular farmers' market that was destroyed by fire in 2017. In October 2023, the property was listed for judicial sale by court-appointed agent JLL Capital Markets at $33.5 million, following financial default by previous owner Capexco. The land has an approved land-use designation under the Glacier Ridge Area Structure Plan allowing a floor area ratio of 2.0–3.0, with approved Outline Plan permitting a community activity centre including approximately 1,500 residential units, 350,000 sq ft of institutional/office space, and 205,000 sq ft of retail and commercial.

The site's vision under the Glacier Ridge ASP is for a SEED (Sustainable Economic Evolution District) concept anchored around a revived farmers' market and food-innovation ecosystem — including restaurants, cooking schools, catering businesses, food research, and post-secondary mini-campuses. The West Nose Creek valley adjacent to the site provides greenway access and scenic views toward northwest Calgary. The broader Sage Hill and Kincora commercial nodes within a short drive to the south provide immediate retail access including a Calgary Co-op, Sobeys, T&T Supermarket, Walmart, and a range of dining and service businesses. Stoney Trail NW provides ring-road access placing downtown Calgary within approximately 25 minutes.

As of the available data, the Symons Valley Ranch site has not yet been actively developed into a residential community — it remains at the planning and pre-construction stage following the 2023 judicial sale process. The land use approval supports up to 1,500 residential units in multi-residential and mixed-use formats. The Not applicable viability designation reflects the site's pre-development status: no residential completions exist, no STR demand data is available, and the development timeline is contingent on the outcome of the judicial sale and subsequent acquisition by a developer capable of executing the approved plan.

The West Nose Creek valley adjacent to the site provides greenway access and scenic views toward northwest Calgary. The broader Sage Hill and Kincora commercial nodes within a short drive to the south provide immediate retail access including a Calgary Co-op, Sobeys, T&T Supermarket, Walmart, and a range of dining and service businesses. Stoney Trail NW provides ring-road access placing downtown Calgary within approximately 25 minutes.

The land use approval supports up to 1,500 residential units in multi-residential and mixed-use formats. The Not applicable viability designation reflects the site's pre-development status: no residential completions exist, no STR demand data is available, and the development timeline is contingent on the outcome of the judicial sale and subsequent acquisition by a developer capable of executing the approved plan.

renter_intel.title: Future Farmers' Market Hub Could Drive Visitor Demand
renter_intel.description: If the SEED concept and revived farmers' market are executed as planned, Symons Valley Ranch could become a destination retail and food experience that draws visitors from across northwest Calgary — a demand driver that would benefit any adjacent STR accommodation once the residential component is completed.

renter_intel.title: Multi-Residential Formats Likely to Dominate
renter_intel.description: The approved land use permits high-density multi-residential development, meaning the eventual housing product at this site is likely to be apartment and condo formats in mixed-use buildings rather than the detached/duplex stock that is most conducive to individual STR licensing under Calgary's current bylaws.

renter_intel.title: Site Has a Complex Ownership and Legal History
renter_intel.description: The site's judicial sale history, two fires, and successive developer defaults are public knowledge in Calgary's real estate community and should be factored into any investment analysis. The 2023 court-ordered process and the involvement of a bank and private lender suggest a complex capital structure that may have implications for future ownership clarity.

whats_nearby.items: The West Nose Creek valley runs adjacent to the Symons Valley Ranch site, providing a natural greenway corridor with views across northwest Calgary's terrain — one of the site's most marketable natural features for future residential development.

The Sage Hill retail node immediately to the south offers a Calgary Co-op, Sobeys, T&T Supermarket, Walmart, Costco (nearby), and a broad range of dining and service businesses — providing the commercial infrastructure that will bridge the gap until the Symons Valley Ranch commercial component is developed.

Stoney Trail NW is accessible within a short drive south from the Symons Valley Ranch site via Symons Valley Road NW and 144 Avenue NW, providing ring-road connectivity to every quadrant of the city and positioning the site within approximately 25 minutes of downtown Calgary.

twinhills.name: Twinhills
twinhills.subtitle: Planned Southeast Calgary Community — Early Design Stage with Limited Public Data
twinhills.str_intro:
Twinhills is a planned future residential community in the east quadrant of Calgary, described in available sources as a burgeoning community with development potential. The community appears in the City of Calgary's community inventory and in southeast Calgary planning maps, positioned within the broader east Calgary growth area. Unlike communities such as Ricardo Ranch or Huxley that have reached the Area Structure Plan or Outline Plan stage with detailed planning documentation, publicly available data on Twinhills' boundaries, development timeline, and planned housing product is extremely limited at the time of writing. The community's east Calgary positioning would place it generally proximate to Deerfoot Trail and the southeast residential development corridor, though exact boundary information requires confirmation from City of Calgary planning documents.

Given the early-stage nature of Twinhills, the community does not yet have an established lifestyle amenity base of its own. Based on the east Calgary context, the general lifestyle offer for future residents would likely draw from the broader southeast infrastructure including International Avenue (17 Avenue SE) for commercial services, East Hills Shopping Centre, and the Bow River valley for outdoor recreation access. Transit connectivity to the rest of the city would depend on which specific bus routes are extended to serve the community as it develops. The community's lifestyle profile will be determined substantially by planning decisions still to be made about the mix of housing, commercial nodes, schools, and parks.

No specific housing product data, builder information, or development timeline is publicly available for Twinhills at the time of writing. The community is included in Calgary's community inventory and in real estate market tracking systems, but active listings are extremely limited and the community's formal development program has not been publicly detailed. The Not applicable viability designation reflects the complete absence of a residential market on which to base STR performance projections. Any investment consideration in this community requires direct engagement with the City of Calgary's planning department to understand the development timeline and any approved outline plans.
twinhills.str_lifestyle: Given the early-stage nature of Twinhills, the community does not yet have an established lifestyle amenity base of its own. Based on the east Calgary context, the general lifestyle offer for future residents would likely draw from the broader southeast infrastructure including International Avenue (17 Avenue SE) for commercial services, East Hills Shopping Centre, and the Bow River valley for outdoor recreation access. Transit connectivity to the rest of the city would depend on which specific bus routes are extended to serve the community as it develops. The community's lifestyle profile will be determined substantially by planning decisions still to be made about the mix of housing, commercial nodes, schools, and parks.
twinhills.str_housing: No specific housing product data, builder information, or development timeline is publicly available for Twinhills at the time of writing. The community is included in Calgary's community inventory and in real estate market tracking systems, but active listings are extremely limited and the community's formal development program has not been publicly detailed. The Not applicable viability designation reflects the complete absence of a residential market on which to base STR performance projections. Any investment consideration in this community requires direct engagement with the City of Calgary's planning department to understand the development timeline and any approved outline plans.

renter_intel.title: No Established Market Data Available
renter_intel.description: Twinhills has no established residential completions, no STR listings, and no public market data at the time of writing. Any performance projections would be speculative. Investors should treat this community as a long-horizon planning opportunity rather than a near-term STR acquisition target.

renter_intel.title: East Calgary Growth Corridor is Active
renter_intel.description: The broader east Calgary corridor including Belvedere, East Hills, and the 17 Avenue SE axis is seeing active development and infrastructure investment. Twinhills' position within or adjacent to this corridor may benefit from the demand and amenity spillover as neighbouring communities mature.

renter_intel.title: Direct City Planning Engagement Required
renter_intel.description: Given the absence of public planning documentation for Twinhills, any investor considering the community should contact the City of Calgary's Planning and Development department directly to request the current ASP status, approved land uses, school and commercial planning, and estimated development timeline.

whats_nearby.category: East Calgary Industrial and Employment Corridor
whats_nearby.items: East Calgary hosts one of the city's most significant industrial and logistics employment clusters, with extensive warehousing, manufacturing, and distribution operations accessible via Deerfoot Trail and the 17 Avenue SE corridor — a demand source for worker accommodation in any adjacent residential community.

International Avenue provides east Calgary's most diverse commercial amenity, with an exceptional concentration of international restaurants, specialty grocers, and service businesses along the 17 Avenue SE corridor between Macleod Trail and 68 Street SE.

East Hills at 17 Avenue SE and 68 Street SE anchors big-box retail for east Calgary with Costco, Walmart, Canadian Tire, and a range of supporting services — the primary commercial infrastructure node for the east quadrant's growing residential population.

albert-park-radisson-heights.name: Albert Park/Radisson Heights
albert-park-radisson-heights.subtitle: East Calgary's Most Accessible Inner-City Value
albert-park-radisson-heights.str_intro:
**Albert Park/Radisson Heights is a large, established, and genuinely diverse inner-east neighbourhood that offers inner-city proximity at a fraction of the cost of the inner south or west — a community where the bones of a good, well-connected neighbourhood are in place, and where smart buyers and renters have been quietly getting excellent value for years.** Sitting along International Avenue (17th Avenue SE), the neighbourhood sits at the heart of one of Calgary's most culturally vibrant commercial corridors, a stretch of 17th Avenue that is genuinely unlike anywhere else in the city, packed with international restaurants, specialty grocery stores, and small businesses representing dozens of countries.

The vibe in Albert Park/Radisson Heights is diverse, genuine, and full of character. International Avenue is the neighbourhood's great asset — not just convenient, but actually interesting, a place where you can eat your way around the world within a few blocks. The neighbourhood attracts a mix of recent immigrants, established multicultural families, young renters priced out of the inner south, and longtime east Calgarians who have watched the area's energy build over decades. The community has good park space and pathway connectivity, and access to Memorial Drive and Deerfoot Trail makes it surprisingly well-connected. **Albert Park/Radisson Heights is the east Calgary neighbourhood for people who prioritize authenticity, cultural richness, and value — in that order.**

Housing is a mix of postwar single-family homes, low-rise apartments, and some newer infill, covering a wide range of price points from Calgary's most affordable single-family stock to modest condos. The rental market is very active and one of the city's most affordable, attracting new Canadians, students, and anyone seeking inner-city access on a budget.
